### nonchalant — 2016-02-05T00:19:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-188248>

I've noticed that water boils faster in my metal teapot than in my glass one. But the glass one holds onto the heat longer. If I heat water in the glass one after breakfast, as the fire is dying down, I'll still have hot water to wash dishes at lunch, and warm water later in the afternoon for DH to wash his hands. (He is so spoiled!) Glass is obviously hydrophilic, and the EZ layer acts like insulation.

Jack says glucose acts like antifreeze in animals. Is that because of a hydration shell that forms around the glucose? I've also noticed that when I need iced tea quickly, and mix cold water with the hot tea concentrate, the tea will become cloudy. However, if I mix in sugar first (DH again), then add cold water, it does not cloud. Is something similar going on? The internet tells me that the cloudiness is from oils or particles precipitating out. I guess an insulator (like EZ around the sugar?) modifies the temperature change? Keeps the particles dissolved in the water?

Perhaps the sugar has a negative electrical charge that feeds the EZ? The internet again tells me that sugar is a polar molecule, with many areas of positive and negative charge, so that should assist the EZ in forming rapidly. Sugar is definitely a hydrophilic substance.

Our blood vessels are hydrophilic, hopefully, so the EZ would form there. But we would also need structured water in the plasma, and surrounding the contents of the vessels, (and in other tissues,) to keep from freezing. Hydration and zeta potential. Elevated blood glucose must be a stopgap measure to compensate for less than excellent energy levels, in cold conditions. Perhaps this is why my foot calouses freeze while the softer skin of the arch does just fine in freezing water. And maybe why I have such difficulties walking barefoot on snow. My calouses haven't improved after a year in the country, and I'm starting to think it's the acidic soil. If I walk on freshly tilled soil, I get rough places on the arches and the sides of my feet, since my feet sink into the dirt. My feet always look nice after a few days at the beach. I'm sure the sand is grinding away lots of dead skin, but there may be another factor at work.

Sorry for rambling, but this **is** a journal...

### nonchalant — 2016-02-12T20:04:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-188872>

Does 'three legged stool' rhyme with mitochondria?
We have electrons zooming their convoluted way across the mitochondrial matrix toward oxygen, their ultimate goal.
Similarly, we have sunshine streaming/cascading a complex path through our water-filled bodies towards it's ultimate goal, the earth.

Night is for recondensing and cooling, for ordering the chaos that sunlight brings. We should have magnetism and grounding. Like for tuning the violin that has played all day. For the mitochondria, we similarly need lots of oxygen at night to allow those electrons to find their home.

When we perform most biohacks, shouldn't we always be in the sunshine and grounded at the same time?
This morning my back was still hurting from a trip to the city yesterday, so I put on my TENS unit, and at the same time drank water, watched the sun rise through the open window, and held onto my bed's grounded headboard. As usual, the pulsing sensation is stronger when one is grounded.

I may have to find my grounding sheet again and give it another trial. Nicotine gum is probably best chewed outdoors. Same for food. Jack would say we should be outdoors 24 hours. True.

### nonchalant — 2016-02-13T05:01:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-188920>

Guess I should have started reading Time#7 before writing that. The 3 leg stool doesn't rhyme with the ECT, it runs right along with it, in the mitochondria. So much for my early-morning flash of insight! Ok, back to catching up...

### nonchalant — 2016-02-20T18:20:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-189453>

The other day my ears were ringing, and I couldn't put my finger on the culprit. I hadn't visited the big city, hadn't even ridden around in our newest car. Hadn't been in any car in days. So it wasn't one of the large stores, either. Those can be a problem, especially when they are busy. It was closer to home. It had been very sunny (glorious days, in fact), so I hadn't sat too close to the UV bulb -- it hadn't even been turned on in days. I hadn't sat close to any oscillating fans -- it is warmer at night now, so we didn't have to circulate heat from the stove. I hadn't spent time near the part of the barn that DH has hooked up to electricity and lit with fluorescents, for making machine repairs and such.

Well, one thing we had been working on was a new garden. We had cut some cedars for posts and put up new wire fencing that day. Perhaps the garden was too close to the neighbors house or the above ground electric line? I took my trusty RF meter over to the area, but didn't pick up much. The neighbors weren't home at the time so I tried later in the day but still didn't read much. The Trifield didn't give me any clues either. The next day I also worked in the garden, working the soil, and that night my ears were still ringing. Not worse, but not better.

It has to be the electrical line, which terminates at the neighbor's house. It is close to the garden. Darn it! I've also noticed some buzzing feelings in my feet, especially the left one. I should ask DH for one of his volt meters and check body voltage again.

### nonchalant — 2016-02-20T18:53:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-189457>

I'm going to start a new biohack today. I'm going to reduce the amount of grounding I get. Wait, hear me out..

As I understand Jack, nnEMF steals electrons in our bodies by exciting them. When we lose electrons, our proteins and other tissues become hydrophobic. So then our EZ suffers. We become dehydrated, and proteins cannot correctly respond to light energy and we lose our water batteries. We also lose water-soluble vitamins. Without the light energy and the batteries, well, we all know you can't improve much in such a cellular environment.

I have been trying to improve my dehydration for years now. But I haven't seen substantial improvement.

I think the key is how I'm losing electrons. Or maybe when is a better word. nnEMF must steal electrons by exciting them, like a microwave would, and they end up going to ground. I usually only put shoes on when going to town. Otherwise I'm always barefoot. So if I'm in a relatively high EMF environment, and I'm firmly grounded, I'm losing electrons. Even when the sun is shining brightly and I'm not wearing too many clothes and gathering sunshine in my eyes often.

So what I'm going to do is limit grounding to the times when the sun is sending lots of UV (electrons) my way, and I'm away from the house or any electrical lines. Like when sunbathing, or tending flowers and trees away from the house. Move the CT tank into full sun. Also at night, when on my Magnetico (before it gets hot at night and we start running the A/C all night.) Additionally, I'm not going to be showering or washing dishes after dark. I won't get a Quantlet for a while, so that won't be a factor yet. I wish I could stay off the computer for a couple months.

I think I gather electrons ok, but I just can't compete with nnEMF. I must be a good antenna. My next labs will be in mid March. Perhaps by then I'll see some improvement.

### nonchalant — 2016-02-20T19:00:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-189458>

My mother has had back surgery to remove a cyst and shave a bit off of one of the tappets. So I was in Dallas overnight. I prepared, keeping a quartz geode in my pocket and taking ubiquinol supplements and extra nicotine. I was still astonished how much water I was wanting. We had to go to the store and buy more. And on my way home I drank all I brought with me and had to stop and buy some more cold ones.

Have they upgraded cell phone communications again?

### nonchalant — 2016-02-20T19:48:11+0200

### nonchalant — 2016-02-26T21:18:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-189837>

Woohoo! I've lost 14 pounds since the beginning of the year!! I'm so excited! I'm going to go hug my box of n. gum.

I think the key was using the nicotine for 6 months first to get rid of cravings and to stop snacking. I know I was building up energy. Probably recycling mitos. Then started noticing that I was less interested in 3 meals per day. So on New Year's Eve came, and it seemed to me that I might be able to succeed this time, without the drama of previous attempts. When I weighed in at the start of the year, I was really surprised to see I had accidentally lost a few pounds in late 2015.

I've been eating regular-sized meals, but skipping one most days, usually supper. I really don't miss it, thanks to the gum. No willpower involved. I've been trying to get more sunshine, but I've probably been getting about the same as last year so far. I love using a wet washcloth to cool the skin, especially when it turns red. Soaking up the energy. Bring it on!

About my biohack, I'm also adding more sulfurous foods for every meal. I always get plenty of seafood, pork, nuts and eggs, but now I'm adding onions more often, and having cruciferous veggies daily. My garlic and leek plants are looking good in the garden. And the brussels sprouts are getting big! Wish I had planted cabbage last fall.

### nonchalant — 2016-03-18T00:52:24+0200

Hi Hope! Congrats on the baby miracle!

Water grounds you, whether you are washing dishes, showering, soaking in it, drinking it, whatever. But based on my hack so far, I am less concerned about grounding when I'm between sunlight times and magnetico times, as long as I'm away from the electric line. My feet/calouses are healing nicely and I'm seeing other skin improvements on my arms and legs. I'm continuing to lose weight. My tinnitus is more predictable.

I don't suggest this hack for anyone else. In a better environment, I'd go back to keeping my shoes in the car and wearing them only when I go to town.

### nonchalant — 2016-03-18T18:06:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-191159>

Yes, I've discovered that I'm sensitive to the power lines, @[Hope](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5412/) , and I think walking barefoot near them is draining me of energy faster than I can gather it. I should be able to hold onto it better, and I've increased my sulfurous foods even more, but staying away from the lines, and insulating my feet when I have to be near them seems to help.

I didn't mention it earlier, but my back also seems to be happier. Previously, I would get large improvements in spine health, only to slip backwards periodically. I would be puzzled, because I couldn't always link the backsteps to lack of sun exposure, CT, seafood, different environment, etc. I've also noticed that cloudy days don't drain my general energy levels so much lately. But I have noticed that stormy weather is giving me headaches now.

@[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) , I keep thinking about what I said a few years ago, about grounding under power lines would protect you. I may have been wrong, at least for my situation... ;-)

### nonchalant — 2016-04-01T19:30:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-191829>

I've always loved gardening. Working with the soil would dry the skin of my hands, but I just accepted the common wisdom that it was the clay soil drying them out. Now I live in a sandy soil area, and I've noticed that they still get dry when working with the soil. My feet have also had skin issues, especially in the summer when I'd go barefoot. Since I discovered the necessity of grounding all the time, my feet got worse. Instead of a few calouses near the ball of my foot and around the perimeter of my heel, I was getting out of control calouses everywhere on the soles of my feet.

Now that I think I have discovered the cause, I'm going to get more graphic. Just a warning.  I think that when I was near the overhead electrical lines, I had that 19-hole flowerpot thing going on. Only the pot was made of leather, and there were thousands of pin-holes created by tiny lightning bolts leaving my body and going to ground. While I would be walking on the newly-tilled damp garden, enjoying the sunshine and the tingly feeling of my feet on the cool soil, the thick calouses on my heel were being loosened by this electrical damage and their edges would curl. Later I would be picking at the rough heels and end up pulling off a thick strip of skin, often an inch or so long. When the garden was wet and my feet would sink in deeper, I'd later notice rough skin creeping up the sides of my feet, and also on the arches of my feet.

Or perhaps the doc is right, and my basal skin cells were in hypergrowth, pushing out the older cell layers too quickly.
Either way something was wrong in my environment. These aren't high-power lines, just the typical ones you see in neighborhoods.

### nonchalant — 2016-04-25T19:06:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-193455>

> Growth is necessary in biology but uncontrolled growth is not; but any kind of growth is always stimulated by damage (ROS), but it is normally under feedback control of UV/IR light to stimulate regeneration. -JK

My TMJ is improving slowly, and I think it was damage that started the process of repair. I've had a non-painful, clicking type of TMJ in both jaws since I was about 8 years old. \[My father was using my extended jaw as a model for his Don Quixote painting, lol.\] Last year, when I started using nicotine gum heavily, my jaw muscles became sore, needing ice a few times. Since that time I've been noticing some improvements. Both joints started having a peculiar "sandy" sensation when I'd yawn, but they would click less often. Currently, the left joint no longer clicks at all, and the sandy sound is also gone. The right side, which was always my worst side, still sounds sandy, but vary rarely clicks.

Apparently when I damaged my jaw so long ago, I wasn't getting enough sun to repair it correctly. Perhaps opening my mouth wide while in the sunshine now is helping more than my teeth and blood vessels?

### fitness@home — 2016-04-25T19:49:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-193458>

Interesting Sally. I was at the dentist today and talked to her about TMJ/tinnitus. She gave me a recommendation to a highly recommended (by some of her patients) Chiro/Acupuncture. In me, I think there is some relation because I can open and stretch my jaw (think making funny faces) and it changes the pitch and sound level of it. Unfortunately, I can't walk around all day holding the funny face pose .

Earlier in your journal you mentioned your dental apt results and some gum issues. I use the little nicotine lozenges. Todays exam was really good. Almost zero plaque and the gum line around my teeth were all 1-2s, with the exception of a couple 3s on the lower left side - due to brushing too hard (and I try really hard not to!). So for me, no dental/gum issues with the nicotine.

I am going to try the acupuncture. Will let you know if it helps any.

### nonchalant — 2016-05-22T20:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-195183>

I've been working on rehabilitating my lips these past few months. Foregoing the lip balm, sunning the lips and squirting water on them. It's working like a charm.

I've used chap-stick since an early age, mostly during winter as I recall. I gradually increased the use, and by my early teens I needed it daily. Then several times a day. In my twenties I switched to a sheer lipstick. Then I started working in IT, and after a few years I noticed that I had to apply the stuff hourly. I switched to a "natural" lipbalm and continued as before. A few years ago I found a better lipbalm, with no parabins and whatever, but saw no improvement. My lips would dry out quickly without continual application.

So earlier this year, after discovering the joys of spraying on water in sunshine, I tried foregoing the balm. At first I couldn't stand much sunshine on them, despite frequent spraying, and I would retreat inside and put on the balm. But fairly soon I noticed they were a slightly deeper color. Once I think I slightly burned them, but I persisted, and now I only apply lip balm at night, right before bed. An amazing change for me. I feel like a normal person!

### endless — 2016-05-22T20:03:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-195184>

Wow, that's interesting....my lips are the same. I use a lot of lip balm. Is it the magnesium water you are spraying on your skin, or just water?

### nonchalant — 2016-05-22T20:15:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-195185>

### nonchalant — 2016-06-11T20:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-196388>

Note to self: Be careful with aspirin use in Summer!!
This morning I had some back pain and took a couple aspirin. An hour or two before noon I was out in the barn painting some wood for DH's homemade chicken-door opener. I started feeling bad, but I was almost finished, so I continued. But just a couple minutes later I thought I was going to pass out. My head was spinning and my tummy felt sick and my arms and legs were trembly. I dropped everything and went indoors for the air conditioning. Laying down helped and I was able to get up and get more cold water and go to the bathroom.

I realized that I had overheated, though I was in shade and the barn hadn't really warmed up yet. It must have been the aspirin, forcing my mitochondria to uncouple and produce more heat. We need tightly coupled mitochondria in the heat! Yes, Jack is right as usual. I should have gotten a clue earlier, when I was sunbathing and couldn't stay out as long as normal, though I've started using epsom salts in my spray bottle. I had cooled off after the sunbathing, and my ever-present glass of icewater was with me.

I also realized that I was comfortable last night, though DH said he was warm during the night. I hadn't taken any aspirin before bed. The back pain followed a trip to the city.

Excuse me while I do some CT...

### nonchalant — 2016-07-08T00:34:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-198033>

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...mitochondrial-ineffieciency.5346/#post-197583](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/diabetes-and-fuel-switching-low-carb-flu-poor-mitochondrial-ineffieciency.5346/#post-197583)

I think I need to exchange my guinea fowl for some winter-laying chickens. In Winter I can't get enough bacon and eggs for breakfast, but when it started getting warmer I just couldn't get interested as much. I would have sardines some mornings, or just bacon, but other mornings I would just be frustrated because nothing sounded good. Some nuts maybe. I finally started cautiously eating more carbs for breakfast and found that I was once again experiencing hunger in the morning. A win for the pituitary!

So once the guineas finally started laying in the Spring, it wasn't long before the eggs just started to stack up in the fridge. Once or twice a week they are good, or to have boiled in salads. Good thing the rest of the family loves them. The winter eggs from our single chicken, however, were hoarded for some spectacular breakfasts.

Now I just need to find some chickens that can hold their own against coyotes like the guineas can. :/

### nonchalant — 2016-07-10T17:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-198180>

I think I've found the last detail that is enabling great sleep for me for the past week or two. As Jack mentioned recently, I've stopped chewing N. gum when the sky gets dark. Now I drop off to sleep quickly, and stay asleep. I'm waking a little earlier, alert and happy to start the day, in time to watch the red sky before dawn. Previously I needed sunlight on my skin to banish the grogginess.

I know that there is more to it than just the gum, because long before I started that I would lay awake quietly, waiting for sleep, while DH would be snoring or at least fast asleep. That has been going on for years!

Probably the biggest step towards good sleep for me was to start skipping supper, which I have been doing since the beginning of the year. I usually eat only two meals per day. My nightime insulin must be low now, which will allow leptin to do it's thing.

Well, maybe it wasn't the biggest step. Early this year also I started avoiding being barefoot near the power line. That has made a huge difference in my ability to hang onto energy. I don't seem to have to 'start from scratch' as often. My health gains stay with me longer so that I can build upon them.

One thing that I have noticed, though. I'm getting up to go to the bathroom sometime before dawn. I go back to sleep and I'd guess about an hour later I wake up ready to jump out of bed. (Before DH even!!!) Maybe that will settle down. Perhaps my AM cortisol level is higher? That would be awesome! Gimme more AM hormones!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-198216>

Crap! That'll learn me.
I am the groggiest this morning that I've ever been! I even had a cup of coffee. Note to self: stop bragging! I'll sit in the sun and analyze this.

### harmony — 2016-07-12T01:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-198236>

Three steps forward, two steps back! You made me laugh!  I do that so often - tried this "Yay it worked!" Next day - "Bother - no it didn't!" But you probably did take one step in the right direction, so brag away anyway. I remember my toddlers learning to walk when I stumble like this, and how surprised they look when they take a few steps and then topple.  Then they get back up and do it again... Happy sunny contemplation

### nonchalant — 2016-07-13T01:25:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-198276>

Well I screwed up my courage and tried MB again. Yesterday was sunny, so I took one drop of 1% MB. About 5-10 times the amounts I took before. This time I noticed an increase in overall energy, instead of the large decline in energy and constipation last time. So today was also sunny and I took another drop. Nice. I might just make this a habit.

### nonchalant — 2016-10-29T20:00:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-203432>

I think I've figured out my shin issue. The front of my lower shins tend to get dry skin. This summer the problem went away, but recently it came back, and I don't think it was because it is Autumn here. I think it is nnEMF. A week ago, I followed DH, walking in the pastures and along the power line easement. I have been really good at avoiding the lines for a few months, but he really wanted to show me some sights. Of course I had shoes on, but I ended up with scaly skin there, right in the front of the lower legs, just above my feet.

I think the problem is the tall grass, weeds and vines that hit that part of my legs as I walked, briefly grounding me as I walked near the power lines. Of course the sides of my legs and tops of the feet would also touch some grass, but those areas of skin with muscle underneath are able to mask the problem better. I have had this shin issue before, and it doesn't seem to matter if it is summer or winter.

A couple days later I made the problem much worse. I heard the guineas suddenly start screaming while I was reading underneath a tree. I walked to the other side of the house and they were really carrying on, and then I heard crows. Not a good sign. I decided to investigate further, despite bare feet. It's been almost a year since I walked over there barefoot. Sure enough, I startled a a red-tailed hawk which flew out of a nearby tree. Crows always seem to accompany the hunting hawks. The guineas calmed down, and I went back. Not long after I could see edema in both legs, below the knee. I'm still dealing with it. As luck would have it, it's been warm recently, and my CT tank isn't as cold as I would like. Guess I could thaw out a roast in it, to bring down the temp.

### nonchalant — 2016-11-16T18:47:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-203997>

About a month ago, I started using my grounding cloth in a different manner. Once we no longer needed the air conditioning at night, I had unpacked the cloth and laid on it like I used to. But I noticed in the morning quite a bit of new stiffness which I hadn't been feeling previouly. And Jack was making comments against grounding sheets. So I removed it, but found I missed it. So I decided to use it for short periods of time, like 30 minutes while reading in the evening. That seems to be the sweet spot, for me. If a knee is bothering me, I wrap it around the knee for a while and notice a distinct improvement. Much like using a TENS unit (which also runs 30 minutes, coincidentally.) Similar effects on the back, hip or hand.

This short period of grounding also led me to realize that grounding also helps ward off the cold. I already knew this, from my experience grounding in the sand at the beach when the evening got cool. So now if I wake up at night and the single bedsheet is no longer warm enough, I just reach up and gather a bit of the grounding cloth (that hangs from the headboard) in my hand like holding a rope. In about 10-20 seconds I start feeling comfortable. I'll hang on for a minute or so, then let go and go back to sleep. Currently I do this about once a night.

It feels much like doing CT, where the water is really COLD at first, and then the intense feeling just shuts off in a few seconds. The water is still cold, but the body can deal with it effectively. I thought it was due to the insulating effects of the EZ, but the grounding effects of the EZ must be a big factor. I already knew this, also, from my experience with running water over my hands while wearing a TENS unit.

This sure beats what I used to do, pulling the second bedsheet on top of me, or even the quilt, but only a few minutes later tossing it back off because I got too warm. And then repeating that action a couple more times a night. Silly, really.

### nonchalant — 2016-12-18T23:44:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-205449>

Jack mentioned "A fib is another proxy for a body devoid of light." in another thread. It made me realize that my irregular heartbeat is gone. Sometimes, usually at night, I would notice that I would get a single, very strong heartbeat, that was unlike the others. And occasionally those strong heartbeats would come regularly, like 3-4x per minute. I could even tell when one was about to happen, in one of those series. Not sure if that is A-fib; I never consulted a doctor about it (along with all my various other strange problems.) I would guess that this problem started about 10 years ago.

Anyway, I can't remember the last time I've felt that big thump in my chest. Gotta be a good thing energy-wise.

Makes me wonder if my heart murmur can heal. Most docs don't mention it, but my current one has, and one I had when I was about 20 yrs old. That doc long ago said it wasn't bad, but I should be aware of it. If a valve is stressed, then I'd think that a healthy heart/body would be able to identify the problem and fix it.

### Scompy — 2016-12-19T00:14:00+0200

### nonchalant — 2017-01-09T19:42:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-206395>

I'm pleased, so far this winter. We've had low temps down to 10F so far. Several days of cloudy weather interspersed with some gloriously sunny days. Amazing to get too hot laying outside in January! Water to the rescue. I sense a decrease in energy on cloudy days, but it is subtle, not the crash I used to experience, even last winter. On Oct. 30 last year we removed the magnetico for a hack. Since we are out of the city, and the air conditioner wasn't running anymore at that time, I thought that we might not need the magnetico as much to make things still and quiet in our cells. I figured that maybe without it, our cells will be better able to sense the natural, changing magnetic field.

So far I've been happy with the hack. DH still drops off to sleep in 5 min. or less (a huge improvement brought on immediately after installing the magnetico while we lived in the city) unless he eats late. My sleep seems to be good; perhaps it is slowly improving. My back seems to be retaining its improvements and building upon them. Brief uses of the grounding towel eliminates any joint pains before sleep.

### nonchalant — 2017-02-08T02:01:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-208163>

My body has discovered that sunshine fulfills it. In the afternoon, I'll sometimes experience a feeling of "I want." Water and nicotine gum don't help, nor does the idea of any sort of snack, though I almost always skip supper. Certainly not screen time, though that used to be a beloved past-time so long ago. I glance outside...yes, that's the ticket. Though I may have been outside quite a bit already that day, there's nothing like being outside, avoiding the tree shadows that are starting to creep across the lawn. Drink in the sunshine. No more "I want."

### Valerie — 2017-02-08T02:47:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-208166>

Could you elaborate on your grounding cloth and how you use it to take joint pain away?

### nonchalant — 2017-02-08T21:19:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-208238>

> [Valerie said:](/goto/post?id=208166)
>
> Could you elaborate on your grounding cloth and how you use it to take joint pain away?

Sure! Read this:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/page-14#post-203997](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/page-14#post-203997)
I've been using the cloth on fewer areas, lately. My joint pain has subsided.\* I still use it on my hand, and my arthritic pinkie seems to be healing slowly. It used to be so bad that that fingernail was oddly-shaped. But sunlight and cold water returned the nail to to normal, and the extra grounding seems to be further decreasing the swelling of that last joint. Perhaps some day it will be able to bend slightly backward, like it used to.

\*Maybe using the cloth on my hand still benefits the elbows and knees?
The cloth is, of course, connected to wire running to a 6 ft grounding rod in the earth outside my window.

This summer, when the A/C unit starts running day and night, I plan on continuing this short-term grounding at night. Not sure if it will be a benefit or not.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-02-08T22:05:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-208243>

City life is getting tougher:
5G = faster mitochondrial decline coming soon to a city near you. Terahertz Transmitter Under Development Capable Of Data Rates 10 Times Faster Than 5G - The Marshalltown, 8th February 2017

We're really moving up the electromagnetic spectrum now - this transmitter operates between 290 GHz and 315 GHz. I wonder what our already radiation-compromised bodies will make of it...?

Panasonic Corporation, Hiroshima University and Japan’s National Institute of Information and Communications Technology announced Feb. 6 that they are developing a terahertz transmitter that can exceed data rates of 100 gigabits per second or at least 10 times faster than fifth-generation mobile networks.

Expected to be finished by 2020, the transmitter is set to be presented at the International Solid-State Circuits Conference 2017 in San Francisco, California.

As a frequency band, terahertz is new and vast, designed for use in ultra high-speed wireless communications in the future. The transmitter under development has been shown to achieve communication speeds of 105 gigabits (0.1 terabit) between the frequency ranges 290 GHz and 315 GHz. Currently, this frequency range is unallocated but sits within 275 GHz and 450 GHz — frequency ranges whose use will be discussed in 2019 at the World Radiocommunication Conference — as guided by the section on International Telecommunication Union Radiocommunication......[http://www.themarshalltown.com/tera...e-of-data-rates-10-times-faster-than-5g/19266](http://www.themarshalltown.com/terahertz-transmitter-under-development-capable-of-data-rates-10-times-faster-than-5g/19266)

### nonchalant — 2017-03-26T18:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-211133>

I've been messing around with turpentine, and it has given me some insight about my dry skin. I've had this hazy white layer on my arms and legs, particularly my shins, for a few years now. And my heels have been rough for even longer. I noticed a couple years ago that the hazy skin got worse if I watched an occasional movie at night. Sure, I was wearing my blue blockers, but I realized the blue light was every where else on my skin. So I avoided that light at night, and the dry skin improved some. Also I noticed that if I were barefoot near the power lines the soles of my feet would start to peel horribly. So I stopped that, and my feet improved quite a bit. But the problem has continued, to a lesser extent, despite good grounding and lots of sun on those surfaces.

Recently my doctor has urged me to work on candida. So I have been ingesting a small amount of natural turpentine occasionally. I make sure I have regular bowel movements (squats rule!) But I've noticed the hazy skin becomes uglier the next morning, with large flakes and all. I think the dry skin comes from candida. Someone online thought the shiny skin and flakes were not dry skin but candida poop. It does have an odd odor. Sorry if TMI.

So I have also purchased some pine tar soap to bath with, and it really seems to help. Even my heels are settling down. And I got a pine salve which I put on my heels at night, because I don't like to put any oils or creams on my skin during the day--they change the sunlight.

I would have thought that the strong sunlight would get rid of any candida on the skin, but if the hazy (sometimes shiny) skin coating also reduces the power of the sun, perhaps this yeast could still survive. Blue light at night on the skin, and being near the power lines would excite the candida, so it all seems to fit.

### nonchalant — 2017-03-26T18:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-211138>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=208238)

> [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/page-14#post-203997](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/page-14#post-203997)
> I've been using the cloth on fewer areas, lately. My joint pain has subsided.\* I still use it on my hand, and my arthritic pinkie seems to be healing slowly. It used to be so bad that that fingernail was oddly-shaped. But sunlight and cold water returned the nail to to normal, and the extra grounding seems to be further decreasing the swelling of that last joint. Perhaps some day it will be able to bend slightly backward, like it used to.

I think the grounding is definitely helping my pinkie finger. It's still swollen, but I don't feel like hiding from others like I used to. The swelling isn't so noticeable. I'm going to be sure to wrap the grounding cloth around that hand consistently. Also the elbow on that same arm has a spur of some sort on the outside of the joint. I can ground them both at the same time, if I lay on that side for a while.

(I'm documenting this to see how long it takes. Perhaps this time next year my pinkie joint will be able to bend slightly backwards, and that bump on my elbow will be better?)

One thing else I have done; instead of grounding my second USB port in my laptop, I'm grounding the same port that the mouse uses. I don't notice it tingling any more, though I certainly don't let my hand hover on it (or my hands on the keyboard). And I still keep my knees out from under the laptop. Darn technology.

### Scompy — 2017-03-27T00:01:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-211162>

If your laptop has a 2-pronged plug in to the wall socket, it's ungrounded. Typically, touching an ungrounded laptop could send an extra 4+ volts into a person's body. But because the electronics are right under the keyboard on a laptop, it's also putting a person in a typical magnetic field around 2 to 4 milliGauss. The other way to hack this is to get a USB wired keyboard and plug that into the laptop so you don't have to touch the keyboard itself (only use the wired keyboard).

### nonchalant — 2017-03-27T00:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-211165>

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=211162)
>
> If your laptop has a 2-pronged plug in to the wall socket, it's ungrounded. Typically, touching an ungrounded laptop could send an extra 4+ volts into a person's body. But because the electronics are right under the keyboard on a laptop, it's also putting a person in a typical magnetic field around 2 to 4 milliGauss. The other way to hack this is to get a USB wired keyboard and plug that into the laptop so you don't have to touch the keyboard itself (only use the wired keyboard).

I've been using a "plug to gator" grounding device from lessemf.com to ground my laptop to an electrical outlet. I cut into a USB connector from an unwanted device to connect the gator clip to a USB port. My trimeter showed a good improvement. This grounding cable virtually eliminated the "sparks" I felt in my hands and knees, but somehow it must not have been grounding my USB mouse enough. This time I'm grounding the hub that I connect the mouse in, instead of the second USB port.

A USB keyboard would be even better, as long as it too were grounded. I'm a sensitive gal. ;-)

Oh, word to the wise, do not clip the gator to the card reader! Mine stopped working immediately after that fiasco.

### nonchalant — 2017-04-13T17:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-212273>

I'm sweating!!! Not right now, lol, but twice now when doing yardwork, I've found sweat running down my face, getting into my eyes, etc. Not the gentle, ladylike perspiration I've experienced for years now. This stuff actually cools me off! Not sure how my redox has finally shifted. I haven't been doing much differently lately. Same gallon of water per day, CT, seafood, sunshine, grounding. I've been automatically waking everyday as the sky turns red for a long time. Since the days are longer this Spring, I've been going to bed closer to sunset. Perhaps it just took a really long time, as Jack warned me once.

Maybe reducing candida helped? Assuming it has been significantly reduced. The treatments slow my bowels, but daily squatting allows me to be regular.

Whatever the reason, I'm so happy! I've been dreading Texas summers for a really long time. Perhaps this year will be more tolerable.

### nonchalant — 2017-07-03T21:54:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-217870>

Temps got high here, so the A/C is back on. After a couple nights of discomfort, I reinstalled the Magnetico. Much better, thanks.

I was sitting under a tree, watching the morning sun after a rain. The sky was mostly clear but there was not even a whisper of wind. Completely calm. Eventually a cloud obscured the sun and I got wet a bit later as rain dripped off the leaves. Now what caused that? I looked up--still no wind. Could the wet leaves have formed a large EZ layer in the sunshine, which collapsed after a minute of no sun? Sure enough, the cloud moved off, and the dripping quickly stopped. I wanted to see if it would happen again. A wispy cloud didn't have the same effect. Before another big cloud came along, however, the wind started to stir, probably because the sunshine was gaining in power. I got wet again.

### nonchalant — 2017-07-20T01:19:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-219436>

I have noticed that sometimes my vision (without glasses) is sometimes sharper than expected. Other times it is more fuzzy than usual. Also I've noticed that sometimes I can look at the summer sun easily when it is high noon, startling even me, and other times the 10am sunshine is just too much. I knew I wasn't doing something right on those "bad" days.

Jack's comment about preconditioning the skin and eye with early morning sunshine seems to be the key. We need our proteins to be 'in shape' first. Literally. Remember each morning cortisol goes on a rampage, stealing electrons from our collagen, which makes it lose it's perfect helical shape.

It's not easy catching UV rays with loose collagen. Kinda like playing jai-alai with a rubbery basket.

### nonchalant — 2017-07-23T18:39:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-219833>

I think Jack's Vermont video and his Solar Callus Rx are going to be huge for me. I've been getting morning sun on my skin for a very long time, before the UV shows up and after. But I've been missing the very early light in places. I would get out each morning, and my face, arms and legs got the earliest light, but no nude exposure until the sun reached my "tanning place" in the yard. For the last few days, though, I've been getting full exposure to the 15-30 minutes of red light bouncing from the available clouds. Everything looks red outside, including the skin at that time. Temps are relatively cool, and I get 2 exposures each day, morning and evening. Already I can feel a difference in the squiggly areas.

Yesterday, however, I didn't get much sun during the heat of the day, and I sure paid for it. Low energy and hunger. I'm going back outside for some UV!

### nonchalant — 2017-09-14T19:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-223640>

With Jack's recent postings about MB, I've started playing with it again. Previously I tried drinking a bottle of water with a drop of MB. But I've read that there might be problems taking it orally; not enough gets absorbed. This time I'm going with sublingual drops. For two days I tried a single drop under the tongue for a minute. Didn't notice anything, as usual. But this morning I tried diluting the drop with a dropper full of water. Then I put a single drop of that under the tongue for a minute. Tired of cleaning the sink of blue stain, this time I just swallowed that drop, along with the rest of the diluted solution.

I went outside, strapped on the quantlet, and chewed nic gum for good measure. Nude sunbathing as usual, water at my side, feet grounded. Didn't really feel any different, but after an hour or so, I noticed that my vision was quite sharp. Those leaves and pine needles looked like I was wearing glasses. And the air looked cleaner, a bit like it looks near the Rocky Mountains. That has to be good. Perhaps the 'clean air' effect is a lack of light scatter? Maybe the vitreous humor is shaping up? I sure would like to get rid of my floaters.

### nonchalant — 2017-09-14T19:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-223645>

Speaking of nicotine gum, I have cut way back. I take about a quarter of what I used to, and only when I'm in the sunlight. No withdrawal symptoms. But the thing is, if I'm already outside, I don't really need it anyway. I guess there is just no good substitute for sunlight. Food and exercise are great, but the free radicals created are harmful if you stay inside. Perhaps water (and cold and IR) are the only good inside supplements. I'm still on the fence about UV indoors.

### WalterNL — 2017-10-01T20:06:50+0300

Thinking out loud, you could say that when humans moved north/south and started wearing clothes, the hands became much more important since these, along with the face, became the few areas exposed to sunlight. In wintertime far from the equator, the hands and face also get consistent cold therapy being exposed most often.

Then there's also the fact that we use hands to literally connect to other humans. There are practical reasons, but there's probably a lot more to this story that I can think of now.

### nonchalant — 2017-10-11T23:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-225616>

This red light stuff is interesting. It has much the same benefits as CT, but it lasts longer. I'm guessing the light my mitochondria put out isn't as effective as the red sunlight. It's so wonderful to wake up in the morning and feel the improvements in my problem areas.

Like CT, it also causes increased urination. That must be from the collagen. As Jack said, cortisol removes electrons from collagen, causing it to unwind, and more water rushes in to bind with the filaments. So if these filaments get light energy, and tighten, water would be released. Here's to tighter collagen!

The cool temps lately have been helping a lot, allowing me to bake in the sun until close to noon. Also makes sleep more enjoyable. Back in my teen years I really disliked winter, complaining that I couldn't get warm enough. Times change.

### nonchalant — 2017-10-24T00:38:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-226452>

I cannot find who posted on the forum recently about putting a drop of methylene blue on her mole daily until it disappeared, but thanks very much! I've been putting some on DH's large wart near the hairline. He's had it for about a decade. Not applying daily, but every week or two, straight from the bottle. With the first drop it decreased noticeably. After the next drop, he was left with a star-shaped wart. Now those arms have disappeared, and it is less than half the size. This is amazing. He asked me how it worked, and I told him it is simply a blue dye that attracts healing light.

I have noticed that after the MB disappears the wart will darken on its own, which must be melanin. Then that goes away, and I apply more MB.

### nonchalant — 2017-10-24T00:48:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-226453>

I have discovered an easy way to ground on the go when you can't touch the earth. When I am cooking indoors or traveling in an automobile, I use a wet washcloth. It's really handy when my hand is sore, just carry the cloth around. If needed, reapply water. It's not as fast as washing dishes by hand, or taking a shower or CT bath, but it gets the job done. I suppose for a sore knee or ankle, I could strap it on somehow. The neck would be easy. I haven't tried holding it yet in the hand for a remote problem, like with my back, but I'll check it out someday.

### nonchalant — 2017-10-24T19:50:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-226493>

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=226456)
>
> Non, I'm lost! Seems like a paradox working? so how does a wet wash cloth ground you?

Hi Jude! Sorry, I should find my old post and link to it. From trial and error I've learned that water, even disconnected from the Earth, will offer up electrons. The best evidence came from wearing a TENS device. I'd turn it down so that I could just feel it, and then try grounding in different places. The best grounding would make the TENS sensation quite strong. I discovered that holding my hands underwater in a bucket worked quite well. Removing my hands, and letting them drip, decreased the current, but it was still strong. Then I'd dry my hands and the TENS device would settle back down to a gentle sensation. I found that drinking a glass of water would also excite the unit for a bit. Oh, and I discovered that even fluoridated city water would ground me.

I haven't used the tens thing lately. I discovered that the wet cloth was enough to ease pain in my hand, similar to what a grounding cloth would do, but it took more time. So even that small an amount of water does some good. Water's amazing. I suppose all it takes is a thin layer next to skin to start building an EZ, and we can grab electrons from it at will.

### nonchalant — 2017-10-29T19:10:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-226882>

Well I sure messed up today. The sun was at about the 10am position, though artificial time was about an hour later. And it was so bright! I couldn't look anywhere near it. Even looking to the far right, with the sun in my peripheral vision, I had to blink a lot to see that tree. After a while, it was more comfortable, so I similarly looked to the far left, and that was way too bright as well.

What happened? I saw the sunrise as usual, outside barefoot in the first freeze of the season. I took my MB, had sardines for breakfast, and watched the sun for a bit while eating. But the day before, I promised DH that I would help him cut and split more firewood. So I did some laundry and dishes, kept the stove going and put in some leftovers to warm for lunch, and hung the laundry out on the line. But plans change, and DH had to do some maintenance to the chainsaw first, so I finally got a chance to do some sunbathing late in the morning. Guess my eye proteins didn't like the neglect. They just weren't prepared for the bright light. I think today I'll be cautious about looking at the sun, and be sure to get plenty of red light this evening.

I am thinking of moving to the countryside to...but would love some sunshine ( i live in UK )..

I agree about seeing all night too if i drink all the water !

### nonchalant — 2017-11-04T17:35:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-227351>

Now this is unexpected. My handwriting is improving? I was addressing and signing some greeting cards, and my writing wasn't cramped as usual. It looks more like what I want it to look like. I could write the word "Congratulations" and not have it start big but end small and nondescript.

I got excited, and decided to try a better test: write a letter. Well, it has improved, but there is plenty room for more improvement. But my handwriting in that note was better than it has been in years, and I didn't feel frustration with the process. It's like my brain no longer disconnects from my hand, leaving my hand to make do on its own.

### nonchalant — 2017-12-17T00:04:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-230445>

In Reality 4, Jack said that the brain cells are designed to undergo autophagy, not apoptosis. I wonder what happens when things get so bad that the mitochondria in the brain start to signal for apoptosis. Do we also get a sensation of doom? Do we get increasing suicidal tendencies? Or is it just the lack of proper brain function and hormones and neurotransmitters that make people want to end it all?

I lost one family member to suicide years ago. It was so frustrating and baffling. Why didn't he just leave? Start over elsewhere? But a friend who knew all about those tendencies told me that it isn't so easy. They know their problems were internal and will follow them everywhere. But perhaps that friend was wrong. A new, more natural environment might make those issues simply disappear.

### nonchalant — 2017-12-26T18:22:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-231103>

I've been cutting back on nicotine gum, but was unable to drop it completely. Usually in the afternoon I'd really want some. Recently Jack said "Methylene blue oxidizes NADH into NAD+, and increases the NAD+/NADH ratio." Wow. I immediately thought about nicotine. So in addition to my diluted sublingual MB in the morning, I tried it at night and soon noticed a vision improvement while reading. Anything that boosts my vision is good. So the next day I also added it around noon. And I noticed my afternoon had passed without the desire for nicotine. Amazing! Thanks Jack for the Christmas gift. Nicotine is expensive, in more than one way.

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...tralians-the-options.20398/page-7#post-230821](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/deuterium-depleted-water-for-australians-the-options.20398/page-7#post-230821)

### nonchalant — 2017-12-26T18:31:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-231105>

I've been making DDW for a few weeks now, and I really like the improvement in taste. I've found that I don't always have time to make it like the instructions I found online -- freeze, strain, sunlight, shake, freeze, strain. So I just do sunlight, twirl in a vortex, freeze, pour into another vessel. It seems to work well for me.

### nonchalant — 2018-01-09T00:45:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-232018>

Found another use for my grounding cloth -- in winter I tend to use my clothes dryer for laundry more, which means static electricity. You guessed it, I dump the basket of clothes on my grounding cloth and fold them there. Works like a charm.

### nonchalant — 2018-01-27T01:49:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-233591>

Last year I noticed that every time I would sit in the sun for several hours, grounding and reading, that I would be stiff afterwards, and feel a loss of energy, though my skin and eyes would feel great. Even though I would be relatively far from the electric line. I got the idea that perhaps (gasp!) limiting grounding might do me some good.

For several weeks now, I have avoided daytime grounding except while looking at the sun. Night time grounding stayed about the same, about 30-60 minutes nightly. At first, I thought I might be onto something good, based on general energy levels, but later I realized why I was feeling so chilled. I couldn't believe I was feeling cold with temps above freezing. DH was often surprised when I would insist on building a fire in the stove. I would be okay outside barefoot, looking at the sun, but otherwise my cold tolerance plummeted. I was using some hot water in my showers, even, not having the nerve to face the cold.

I've also been spending some weeks lately in the big city, on and off, for family reasons. I couldn't wait to leave that perpetual summer in the middle of winter, but I came home and just about froze one night with just my usual two sheets. But the outside temp was just right at freezing. The next day I rediscovered my old fear of the cold, staying near the fire and dressing warmly. That just won't do. In addition, my energy levels haven't continued to improve. I think they are about where they were before.

So yeah, a big hack fail. I'll go back to my usual grounding. Perhaps being almost motionless in the sunshine for hours is a bad idea for me, lol. It was a big shock to me that I am not permanently impervious to the cold in this area after all.

### nonchalant — 2018-02-18T21:27:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-235067>

I've been working on making DDE (deuterium depleted eggs). Besides separating the yolks from the whites. Smaller eggs have relatively less whites, so that's another way. When you fry an egg, part of the white is viscous and stands up high, while a smaller amount coats the frying pan on the perimeter. I've decided that this thinner white might be what we want to get rid of, because it does not take part in the gel. So to that purpose, I tried straining the egg in a colander. It seems to work quite well; about a tablespoon of the white does not stay with the rest of the egg. The colander can be of the round perforations in metal type, or made from wire screen.

Ok, I'll admit, I never liked that paper-thin layer of white surrounding my over-easy eggs. It must be the bad stuff.
Also, when I scramble eggs now, I strain the whole eggs, and then add additional egg yolks for a yummy treat. Don't forget a small amount of water to add EZ energy and fluffiness to breakfast.

### nonchalant — 2018-03-31T19:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-238410>

Often this past winter I have awakened quite warm and comfy. I'll be a bit disappointed as I walk through the house, because a fire in the stove for breakfast wouldn't be in order, since it's going to be a warm day. I'll go outside, and yes, it is not very cool. But after sun gazing a while, I'll notice my toes are cold. And my fingers too. I check the thermometer, and it's down in the 40's. It's cooler than it was when I went to bed, not warmer. So I get to light the wood stove after all. Come to think of it, it IS chilly in the house!

I don't remember anything like this last winter. Perhaps my cortisol levels are improving?

### nonchalant — 2018-04-06T22:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-238870>

Yes, Jack, plants can too live under a tarp:
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I saw some plants like these the other day. Couldn't believe it. Spray-painted plants? Blue? Pink?
According to this article,
[https://www.houzz.com/discussions/4801258/kosmik-kaktus](https://www.houzz.com/discussions/4801258/kosmik-kaktus)
"New spines emerge as a glistening white or golden yellow." (Instead of the normal green.)

So some plants can actually live under a tarp, as long as the resultant accelerated growth can punch a hole to allow its strange, off-color flesh into the sunlight. Guess those spiny tips can be useful sometimes.
OK, now I look and it appears these succulents are CAM plants. Well, anyone who is a gardener would know what you get under an intact tarp. Accelerated, pale yellow or white growth for a bit, and then death. Tarps are awesome for killing large amounts of weeds. And they make a good compost underneath, as long as you don't mind all the fungus.

### nonchalant — 2018-04-14T19:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-239402>

My neighbor calls this Sour Dock. It has a wonderful, tart, citrus taste.

I usually chew the stems a few times every Spring. This year I'm chewing it every day. Perhaps it's all the rain we had earlier, or maybe it's just me. Maybe I'll try making a lemonade out of it. That sounds awesome, and something I could share with other family members. I know they liked the sassafras root "tea" I made one year, and the red sumac "lemonade".

### nonchalant — 2018-04-15T18:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-239436>

>
> With taste profile like that I'd be tempted to use it interchangeably with lemongrass in Thai and other Asian dishes...
>
> Keep us informed about your culinary exploration please.

Thank you drezy! I've been looking for this online. I'd look for dock, but only find plants with hefty leaves. Leaves are the last thing you notice on this plant.
Looks like it is _Rumex hastatulus.

"Of all the Rumex that grow in the South, _Rumex hastatulus_ is probably the most pleasing. The tart-tasting intensely green leaves are hard to misidentify — edible wise — and the bloom that turns from white to red is pleasing to the eye. Its nickname is the Heartwing Sorrel, describing the mature reddish, winged seed pods."
_
I've tried eating the thin, tiny leaves; the long juicy stems are superior in taste.

### nonchalant — 2018-05-16T19:31:38+0300

The de Broglie-Bohm pilot wave theory seems, to me, to describe intuition. Once you get in the zone, with the sun rising, the leaves swaying in the breeze, birds tweeting, and the grass tickling the toes, all you have to do is contemplate something that has been puzzling you. Then...wham! There it is. It's like the answer was already in your mind, it just needed to find its way home. Just set up the right environmental waves, and the path is clear. Organization from chaos.

### nonchalant — 2018-07-12T03:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-245696>

Does skin, like the eyes, lose its focus?

This summer I've been gradually getting better at sweating. First the head, then the lower arms, and now the shoulders and chest. My BUN/creatinine ratio is also falling. I attribute it to my MB use. It's the only thing I can think of that has changed much in the last year. For a while I was taking some every two hours during the day. Now it's more like 2-4 times/day.

I know the red light energy MB absorbs helps my eyes focus. Perhaps the skin proteins are also using the energy to align properly. All the more reason to avoid artificial light--if my skin no longer has cataracts, that blue light can penetrate farther.

It's interesting that my emerging sweat pattern is so different from the skin improvements that daily seafood give me. Instead of the areas that get the most sun, DHA first improves the skin normally hidden under clothes, gradually spreading outward toward the hands and feet.

Those nerves have myelination, right? \[The myelin sheath is a greatly extended and modified **plasma membrane** wrapped around the nerve axon in a **spiral fashion** .\] It's easy to expose the entire iris/eye to the sun, and when you are looking the other way, the back could take up the slack. One thing I've done this year is bought an extra Kiniki short beach wrap in the same fabric as the one I already have, and the same as the sarong I have. If I tie it up in front and then just twist it round so the knot is in the middle of my back, I've covered up the girls with no chance of a side boob mishap. With bikini bottoms its much more comfortable than a swimsuit. Or use it with the beach skirt tied at the side and I have a short dress. Its shorter than I'd like but I can use the sarong as a skirt instead and I have a long dress if I've got visitors.

Kiniki do a dress but I don't think it would be very flattering, so my next plan is to buy a couple of sarongs when they're on sale and ask my very good at sewing friend to use the fabric to run me up a dress to suit my body shape, and to the length I want.

### nonchalant — 2018-09-16T19:50:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-251153>

Well, heck. A combination of working indoors cleaning out a house plus several days of rain/clouds has allowed me to catch a cold. And here I thought I was impervious. I was in a rural area, so my old remedies (iodine, silver) were not available. Just gargling in salt water. A couple days later we made it back home, at dusk. I quickly sprayed the colloidal silver, then realized that I would really make my throat an antenna for the air conditioning EMF. Oh well, I'll use iodine tomorrow. Sure enough, my sore throat got worse that night, waking me up a couple times. But dawn yesterday was clear and bright. I got some good red light in my throat (and nose, and ears) plus light at other times. My throat never bothered me again after that sunrise. I didn't even bother swabbing in iodine; it just wasn't needed. I'm still coughing occasionally, though. Is there a shortcut for getting sunshine to the lungs? My guess is that it would have to be red blood cells. But still, the T3 vertebrae nerves go in that direction...

edit: Perhaps sunshine on the sternum would be good for the lungs as well as the heart...

### nonchalant — 2018-12-17T00:38:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-258257>

I've got to toss out more clothes from the closet. The other day I felt a bit of a 'cold sweat'. My hands and feet were chilled, but my torso was, well I guess the closest feeling is 'warm'. I changed shirts and felt fine the rest of the day. The stretchy shirt I had on was 51% cotton, the rest plastic. I guess I can no longer get away with wearing so much plastic. Are silk and wool more conductive than cotton? Flax should be similar to cotton, right?

### nonchalant — 2018-12-17T00:53:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-258260>

Trying to reproduce a cool visual effect, but it's difficult. On two hazy/cloudy days around noon, I was able to see tiny arcs of light in my eyes while looking straight up with the hazy sun in my lower peripheral vision. I had to focus to a point about at arm's reach. On the second occasion, I was able to see that the lights were circling many separate points in my vision. The lights lasted less than a quarter turn around the circles. It would help if I could see them again, because I think the arcs of light were running counter clockwise. Really can't be sure yet. The effect disappears about the time UVA light wanes. Unfortunately I could only look at the effect for a few seconds at a time. For some reason it was too bright. Perhaps my lower peripheral vision is not used to handling even decreased sunlight, and neither day did I see a good red sky in the morning.

Just because some nighties or undies are 100% cotton, doesn't mean they don't have an elastic band pressing against your tummy (or especially the back, in my case.) That elastic might be "gentle and soft" but it's still plastic. I believe I've finally found the source of my persistent back problems. Always right at the small of the back. I kinda wish my skin in the area had given me a warning of the problem. (Sometimes a rash isn't all bad.) Three cheers for the "covered waistband" style!

### nonchalant — 2019-03-18T21:34:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-265824>

It all comes back to nnEMF, doesn't it. If you're doing the protocols, and you still have problem spots, look for an emf problem.

After a little over a month of avoiding elastic, I've noticed that my shorts are starting to get baggy. My back has been much happier, except for those times that we went to town. Makes me think of those times in the past where us adolescent girls were getting "saddlebags" on the upper thighs, and when my daughter started complaining of a "muffin top". All you need are radio waves and elastic. Or pantyhose.

I've already ditched the croc sandals for travel and started wearing shoes with cotton socks (difficult to find 100% cotton) but those have elastic in them too. Should help my recurring plantar faciitis.

Now I'm turning my attention to the bags under my eyes. They just don't make sense, unless I've been eating artificial fats. And I seldom wear glasses with their wire frames under the plastic. But I've just ordered a down pillow covered in cotton. Perhaps that's where I've been picking up the stray emfs. Here you've got the eye with it's salty tear fluid right next to a polyester lump that is covered with a thin layer of cotton.... But then a bag of bird feathers probably has it's own conductive properties. :-/

### nonchalant — 2019-04-20T00:06:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-268464>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=268167)
>
> what differences have you noticed Sally? with your new pillow? and new mattress topper?
> What kind of a mattress do you have?

I just have a cheap mattress, Audrey, like I've slept on all my life. Probably a very high polyester content. Springs too. Since I first got a grounding sheet, years ago, attached to a dedicated ground rod, I've noticed twitches in myself also. But I just knew that the grounding was good, because I felt warmer in winter, and it was better than aspirin for aches. Slowly I realized that such a grounding technique just wasn't for me. So now I'm going the other way. I still stand barefoot outside in the morning, but I limit it.

I haven't noticed a marked difference with the feather pillow; but I have noticed that heat buildup isn't so obnoxious. Similarly with the feather pad, I get warm, but not incredibly hot. A new mattress will probably be my next goal.

In the mean time I'm watching my clothes starting to slowly get baggy. I'd like to weigh myself, but I don't want to jinx anything. ;-)

### caroline — 2019-04-20T05:13:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-268471>

yes ....I have given up weighing myself too - at least for awhile.

What is your timetable for eating these days? what are you eating?

### nonchalant — 2019-04-21T00:54:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-268519>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=268471)
>
> yes ....I have given up weighing myself too - at least for awhile.
>
> What is your timetable for eating these days? what are you eating?

I'm pretty much eating as usual, breakfast and lunch, skipping dinner. Avoiding milk, wheat, artificial fats, tropical fruit. I'm considering doing another '30 day challenge' for seafood. I'm getting tired of aphasia. At least I'm not alone, most everyone I know now forgets words here and there. We're all getting good at helping to fill in the blanks of other's speech.

I just realized--my craving for sweets has really diminished in the past few weeks. Another positive effect of avoiding EMFs.

### nonchalant — 2019-05-05T19:03:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-269725>

I recently bought a soft organic latex mattress topper. I tried it out one night, without the feather topper but with my usual mattress pad. Very comfortable and pleasant, but a big mistake for me. The next day my back was terrible! Quite painful if I stood for very long. I was also tired, and couldn't resist laying down for a nap, because the new topper felt so good. Sure enough, I noticed a slight tingle from the bed, especially at the small of my back where it is weakest. Not a good sign.

So I got up and put the feather topper on top of the latex topper. My bed is so tall now! I made the bed again and tried it out. No tingle at all. The next day my back was almost back to normal. And I wasn't tired.

### caroline — 2019-05-06T07:12:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-269756>

Sally - what exactly are your conclusions from your latex topper? Your spring mattress doesn't bother your back?

We have a new latex type mattress for the last 15 months. I have now had horrible back problems twice ....once last August and then just recently. I have never ever had back problems my entire life.

When I got the back problems last August I was going to a physio to help strengthen my shoulder \[rotator cuff tears\] We thought maybe I was doing the exercises incorrectly and created the back issue.

The mattress in our guest room has springs and is relatively new ....maybe I should do a bio hack.

### nonchalant — 2019-05-07T23:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-269892>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=269756)
>
> Sally - what exactly are your conclusions from your latex topper? Your spring mattress doesn't bother your back?
>
> We have a new latex type mattress for the last 15 months. I have now had horrible back problems twice ....once last August and then just recently. I have never ever had back problems my entire life.
>
> When I got the back problems last August I was going to a physio to help strengthen my shoulder \[rotator cuff tears\] We thought maybe I was doing the exercises incorrectly and created the back issue.
>
> The mattress in our guest room has springs and is relatively new ....maybe I should do a bio hack.

Audrey I'm in the process of getting rid of the spring mattress. It's just taking a while because I'm feeling my way. I've ordered a thicker feather mattress topper, 4" this time. My idea is to swap it for the spring mattress, which will bring the bed way lower. I don't know how the box springs (which has no springs but it does have metal straps) will support it. Probably not well. So I'll need to change the bed frame somehow.

Anyway I can't really tell you if the spring mattress also bothers my back yet. It's just **so** much better with the feather layer. The latex topper was slightly large for the bed, extending a half-inch or so. And when my hands or feet (or knees) got too far off the side, I could feel that tingle. Perhaps if I had no springs underneath, I wouldn't have these problems with latex?

### nonchalant — 2019-05-17T04:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-270663>

Ok the coil mattress is off the bed. Last night we slept on feathers over latex. Loved it. DH initially grumbled that it was too thin (another layer of latex is on order) but in the morning he said he slept like a rock. I think losing the springs is important. When I slip my foot over the side so that I touch a latex layer, I no longer feel the tingle. I do, however, feel a vibration. So I think the latex is picking up something; it's just a different frequency. I wish I could find a thick, old-fashioned cotton mattress. You know the kind with the striped ticking, piping around the edges, and the buttons? \[Sorry, old-fogey moment there.\]

Now I'm looking askance at other furniture in the house, and the car seats. Springs everywhere!

### caroline — 2019-05-19T00:38:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-270798>

Interesting Sally ........I am thinking of swapping out our foam mattress for our other coil mattress!

I am waking up a little bit stiff and a twinge in my back .....all new since this new foam mattress. The company has already replaced it once because it started to sag. Apparently \[from the company\] foam needs to be heated at the exact right temperature or there is problems.

What you are doing is so interesting ....I will be following you and your back!

### nonchalant — 2019-05-23T02:09:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-271066>

Got in the final (firm) layer of latex for the bed yesterday. Before I put it on, I went ahead and put the magnetico back on the bed. (DH was shocked, I was able to handle the rolls all by myself.) Last night was some Awesome Sleep. It was warm when I went to bed, so I left the windows and shades open for awhile... But I didn't stay awake long enough to close them or reawaken until dawn. Oops! Normally that amount of light just kills my sleep. The extra layer of latex kept me comfortable even when on my side. The feathers kept me cool. And the magnets...just fabulous.

Today we installed the window air conditioners. Since I'm sleeping cooler now, perhaps the a/c doesn't have to run so long.

That is all so impressive Sally! Jack was saying in the Q&A that if you are getting a good nite's sleep he doesn't worry too much about you .....but - if you are not sleeping well.......

### nonchalant — 2019-05-31T00:34:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-271653>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=271067)

> How thick are your layers of latex?

Thanks, Audrey! I've got 3" of firm, 3" of medium, and 2" of soft latex. We had, for a couple nights, two 3" layers of feathers. (My sister says it's like the princess and the pea.) The first night was Way Too Fluffy! The second night, once they all got packed down (by lying sideways on the foot of the bed, etc), was great. But then I took one layer of feathers off to give to my mother. I kinda miss it...

Based on the way they felt on that last warm night before we fired up the a/c, I think the feathers will be fine during the summer. However, now that we only have one layer, I've noticed some heat. I might be too close to the latex.

BTW--congrats!!!!!

### caroline — 2019-05-31T07:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-271671>

### nonchalant — 2019-05-31T21:44:51+0300

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=271671)

>

Except for turning over, yes I sleep all night. Even DH isn't getting up to go to the bathroom as often. A couple times (before I removed that extra feather layer) he didn't get up at all. Quite a change for him.

These past winters we slept cold. Sometimes we would wake up in the morning and it was in the upper 40's F inside. But that didn't keep the bed from getting hot. My skin would be quite cool on one side and surprisingly hot on the other. I knew something had to change.

### nonchalant — 2019-06-01T16:29:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/goodbye-city-life.12817/post-271759>

Last night was another exceptional night's sleep. It was cool again, so DH turned off the a/c and opened windows. I was reading in bed and thought I'd close the shades later, when the sky got dark. I keep forgetting that I'm sleeping more soundly lately. So I awakened as the sky lightened a bit and turned over. OMG I fell asleep on my right side and stayed there for hours! I have not been able to do that in a year or two because my heart will start to race. Only when I'd roll to my left side would it calm down. Not last night. This is amazing. This is big.

I wish there were a good replacement for innerspring or foam mattresses. I found a manufacturer who will make one from cotton, but the price is high. I'll start saving up.
