I've been reading some things like [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytochrome\_P450#CYP\_families\_in\_humans](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytochrome_P450#CYP_families_in_humans)
and referring back to my test results, now sorted for entries containing "CYP", I can narrow down the most important issues, at least in part, to:

**CYP1 A2** - GS159 faster caffeine metabolism (flagged as "good)
& also:
rs762551 (A;A) faster caffeine metabolism in liver

(Don't know if this is actually good though.. wikipedia says CYP1's function is related to drug and steroid (especially estrogen) metabolizing.. if I metabolize caffeine faster do I also metabolize estrogen quicker? Is that a silly question of apples and oranges?)

**CYP2 C19** - multiple SNPs and notations - **this is placed at the top of my list no matter how it's sorted, due to having the highest magnitude on the "bad" scale**:
GS151 - reduced breakdown of certain drugs
rs4244285 (A;G) - "This variant is the most common reason for poor metabolism of compounds like mephenytoin (an anti-convulsant), some antidepressants, the anti-platelet drug Plavix, and some drugs used for ulcer conditions of various types. The risk allele is rs4244285(A). As a nonfunctioning CYP2C19, this variant would be expected to be a poor metabolizer of several commonly prescribed drugs, including anti-ulcer drugs like omeprazole (trade names Losec and Prilosec), esomeprazole (trade name Nexium), and lansoprazole (trade name Prevacid). In Caucasians, SNPs in CYP2C19 are relatively rare (in contrast to SNPs in CYP2D6), but SNPs in this gene are common in Asians."
rs12777823 (A;G) - "Avoid Plavix, higher risk for adverse cardiovascular events.**"** (related neighbors, these last 2)
rs 1045642 (C;T) - concentrations of certain drugs are significantly increased

**to name a few - these all pertain to poor breakdown of certain drugs** _\-( just about every class of anti-depressants is 7x less effective in me (I could've told them that years ago, and in fact tried to  ), Plavix has a high risk of cardiovascular adverse events in me, bupoprion or whatever will have no effect on me, etc etc.. too many kinds of drugs to mention. More rs's than just the CYP ones cause part of those issues though)_

**CYP2 D6\*4** - rs 3892097 (A;G) - "The (A) form disrupts proper mRNA formation, resulting in a nonfunctional CYP2D6 protein. The associated allele is also known as CYP2D6\*4. The CYP2D6\*4 allele is the most common nonfunctioning variant of CYP2D6. If two copies of this (or similar) changes are inherited, poor metabolism ('PM') of debrisoquine is observed. Many other drugs are typically first metabolized by CYP2D6 including dextromorphan, sparteine, metoprolol, nortriptyline and many other antidepressants and codeine"

**CYP2 A6\*5** - rs50310170 (G;T) - non-functioning variant, affects nicotine metabolism

**CYP2 D6\*10** - GS173 "reduced metabolism" (which is flagged as "Good")
& also:
rs1065852(C;T) - decreased or non-functioning variant (affects drug breakdown/metabolism)
Wikipedia says CYP2's function is drug and steroid metabolism also but doesn't say which kinds of steroids, but my CYP2 drug metabolism functions seem to be hurting so maybe it's steroid metabolism functions are also in trouble?

I don't know if this helps anyone else besides myself, but I'm a huge fan of learning and this way I can at least reference the ones I want to focus on. Thanks everyone!

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-02-13T01:54:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-you-tell-me-about-cytochrome-p450.12891/post-155501>

CYP1B1 is also decreased estrogen metabolism

# Thread: CT Variables, Shivering, Warming up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-02-11T18:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/post-155377>

I'm writing this post in the hope that it gets stickied, after spending ridiculous amounts of time trying to find what should be a simple answer. _I will remove the above txt if it does. Caveat I may not have all of these correct so please jump in and correct me._

Variables for people experiencing a hard transition into the cold tub (based on about 30 different threads):

_Variable = Target_

1.  Length of stay = 45mins (JK's original variable)
2.  Temperature of Water = 50-55° f (JK's original variable)\*
3.  Ice packs on chest = Building up to 30lbs of ice.
4.  Ambient Temp of room = ideally not heated, but may need to be heated in the first instance.
5.  Depth of submersion = Ideally whole body except hands, feet & head.
6.  Compression Vest = 75% of the time not included in Rx or easy start guide for the bath.
7.  Hat, Gloves & Scarf = Initially then removing.
8.  Daily Frequency = 2 x 30 mins better than 1 x 60 mins.

_Gauges_

1.  Shivering in bath = should be mild (i,e, you should be able to voluntary stop them for a few minutes) if it is violent or you have complete loss of motor control, get out.
2.  Length of time shivering on exit = more than 1 hour is probably not ideal.
3.  Frostbite/Urticaria = Not good.

Warming up should ideally be done at ambient temps.

\*@Shijin13 mentioned that colder water temp reduced warming times?

Other things to consider/test/ask Jack if still having a hard time

1.  T3 levels
2.  ???

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T01:03:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/post-155397>

Tests: 1. Redox Rx [http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/)
2.[http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx-2-biohacking-mri/](http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx-2-biohacking-mri/)

### nonchalant — 2015-02-13T18:42:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-variables-shivering-warming-up.12892/post-155543>

Standing barefoot on the ground while you warm up also helps a lot, even if it is chilly outside.

### Hope — 2015-02-12T01:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155402>

And if so, how do you get email while traveling if you need it? Laptop? Tablet?

I would love to hear....

### JMO — 2015-02-12T02:02:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155408>

I don't have a smartphone just a Samsung. My understanding is that its not constantly sending a signal just when I get a txt or phone call. I take my iPad and keep it on airplane mode then just turn it on to check mail.

### Hope — 2015-02-12T04:09:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155425>

Thanks so much, JMO! I have thought about that as well.....we shall see.

### cinnamon — 2015-02-12T04:38:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155428>

I don't have any kind of cell phone - smart or not!

I have a small tablet that I use very rarely. When I travelled a couple of months ago, I turned it on in the airports to get on wifi and check email. It worked just fine for that.

Also, did you know you can send texts to people who have cell phones from your email account? Yes, most mobile providers have an "email to text" gateway. I was able to send a text to my DH (he \*does\* have a smartphone) while on the airport wifi to let him know when my luggage came off the carousel so he could bring the car around to pick me up.

So, I think tablets are a useful tool when travelling. They do require wifi. But that (unfortunately) seems to be ubiquitous these days.

cheers,
cinnamon

### rlee314 — 2015-02-12T05:59:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155433>

i don't have a smart phone.

### Lahelada — 2015-02-12T13:11:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155443>

Careful with the thought that a normal old phone is more benign. Any cellphone needs to position itself constantly. So even the most ancient cellphone will handshake to ask what do I need to connect with. Also if it can send mail it is plenty smart. A better way to evaluate your phone is from the Sar Value available from the FCC website,even if you are not Us based. If that value is not already on the phone(,behind the battery) you can look it up via the Model or Fcc ID. (also behind the battery)

I have a smartish phone but all things being equal would say that for occasional mail access a tablet would be a good choice . Put it in a case though and use it with a stylus.

\* note apart : my smart phone has a Sar value of 0.17W/kg and the FCC category says that it should be carried at least 1cm from the body and that if worn on a belt clip said clip must not contain metal...... Everyone should look up theirs.

### kovita — 2015-02-12T13:40:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-on-here-without-a-smart-phone.12893/post-155446>

I have a smartphone. Currently have it switched on shortly only few times a day, use it much more like a pager. At the end people got used to the fact, I am not available 24/7. For me this is a system that works just well. As for internet I use laptop or tablet for no more than 30min. a day, the same system, check few times a day.

Maybe we had to be a bit more patient.... and wait a few more minutes... but hey, that time you always can use for something useful  maybe just to learn to be in the moment

# Thread: How does the marine food chain link to land food chain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T03:24:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155419>

Where is the base of the Epi-paleo Rx?
PUFA are involved in the regulation of physiological processes by serving as precursors in the biosynthesis of bioactive molecules, and both PUFA and sterols are essential to major membrane constituents of crustacean zooplankton, zoobenthos, and fish. According to Brett & Müller-Navarra, all herbivores convert the short-chain PUFA (i.e., with <20 carbons, DHA has 22) α-linolenic acid (18:3ω3) to long-chain PUFA (i.e., with ≥20 carbons), such as EPA and DHA, albeit with different efficiency. In aquatic environments, most PUFA and sterols originate in the primary producers, essentially the photosynthetic autotrophs. Bacteria are generally poor in PUFA (DHA), especially long-chained PUFA (DHA). EPA-producing bacteria are not common in marine environments, yet Yazawa et al. found 88 strains of bacteria capable of producing EPA out of 5000 strains screened. In fact, it was recently found that several marine bacteria contain EPA and DHA at levels as high as 25% of total cell membrane FA , and it is likely that the polyketide synthase (PKS) pathway for PUFA synthesis (that acts independently of FA elongase and desaturase activities to synthesize EPA directly) is widespread in marine bacteria. This is the key in the marine food chain and goes all the way back 580 million years ago (Cambrian explosion) to when O2 showed up in the oceans after the sun increased its UV light by 10% and this stepped up DHA production from photosynthetic marine organisms where it was first was synthesized on Earth.
Additionally, the culture conditions of the oceans may determine the bacterial EPA content that is dependent on pH, temperature, and other growth conditions. This includes the blue light and nnEMF around it because water's hydrogen bonds REACT to electromagnetic fields all the TIME. We forget this. Some crustacean species (Epi-paleo Rx alert) and nematodes may directly feed on bacteria and act as the first consumers of primary DHA production, forming an important link between the basis of marine food webs and the higher trophic levels, like large metazoans and fish.
Direct feeding on bacteria may be crucial for crustacean species living close to hydrothermal vents, high-pressure low-temperature deep-sea (Seafood and cold linked) habitats and permanently cold marine environments, or when bacteria is the sole item available for food. It is worthy to note that in both deep-sea habitats and in the anoxic sediment of intertidal flats, bacteria may produce PUFA. Nevertheless, the ecological role played by bacteria in aquatic environments cannot be dissociated from that of heterotrophic protists (HP).
These Protista are also known as Protozoa, which means “first animals”; in fact, the term does not mean that all protozoa are the ancestors of the animals, but refers to the fact that they ingest food, which is typical of animals and not plants. They are able to disconnect from the sun and land to survive.
Some of these Protista are predators and feed on bacteria and other Protista, i.e. they feed on dead organic matter, while others are parasites that exploit the resources of several organisms.
They can be divided into three main groups depending on how they move:
1\. Amoeboid protozoa
2\. Sporozoa
3\. Flagellate protozoa
4\. Ciliates: they are considered the most developed group because of their complexity. They can live either in freshwater or salty water
Heterotrophy is practiced by “mixotrophic” protists that include flagellated phytoplankton that may ingest bacteria or other protists and by heterotrophic protists that do not possess permanent chloroplasts (photosynthesis) and rely on other organisms for nutrition. HP's are important consumers of bacteria and phytoplankton in oceanic food webs concentrating DHA, these, in turn, act as regenerators of nutrients for further phytoplankton growth and as a food resource for marine zooplankton for fish consumption. Although HP has been described as early as the 1920's, their ecological importance was only fully appreciated when new analytical methods enabled their identification and quantification (e.g., epifluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry).

The notion of a classical linear food chain in aquatic environments consisting of phytoplankton, zooplankton, and fish predominated until the 1970's, and its replacement by the concept of a trophic web only gained ground in the 1980's. This is an example of a linked coupled cycle that life uses.

In the trophic web concept, dissolved (DOM) and particulate organic matter (POM) consisting of detritus, heterotrophic bacteria, and autotrophic phytoplankton are consumed by HP in a carbon-transfer pathway called the microbial food web. The key point is how atoms of carbon cycle from sea to land in this web. Humans feasted off this part of the carbon cycle. This microbial food web connects to the classical food chain that branches into a network of trophic links. Although picoplankton is responsible for the bulk of primary production in large parts of the marine environment (as well as lakes), their small size makes them largely unavailable for direct consumption by crustacean zooplankton, which has difficulty in retaining these size particles in their filtering apparatus. In systems where microalgal species-rich in high-quality lipids (_i.e._, DHA and other key PUFA's) dominate the phytoplankton and can be directly grazed by crustacean zooplankton (Epi-paleo Rx), the trophic transfer from autotrophs to crustaceans via HP can be considered both as a loss of carbon (_i.e._, losses via respiration), as well as a loss of essential lipid compounds.
Autotrophic Protists: are mostly algae. The term algae embrace all photosynthetic protists. It refers to an aquatic, photosynthetic way of life, not an evolutionary kinship. Most algae live in water, but some are terrestrial. Most algae live near the surface of the water producing **30 to 50 percent of the earth's oxygen**. _This is where the oxygen for DHA construction began._ Algae are classified on the basis of conservative characteristics such as the type of cell wall, flagella, photosynthetic pigments, and the form in which food is stored.

Nevertheless, HP feeding on nanophytoplankton and bacteria may biochemically enhance the quality of the tissues of their prey, which led to the “trophic-upgrading” concept developed by Breteler _et al._ This is how DHA was concentrated in the marine food chain. Thus, heterotrophic protists bridge the gap between the microbial loop in the oceans and higher trophic levels by both repackaging their food and by increasing its nutritional value, which may be especially important when phytoplankton abundance is low or of reduced lipid quality and producers are dominated by prokaryotic picoplankton. All these things are eaten or filtered by crustacean and bivalves and wind up in fish.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T03:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155420>

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T03:50:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155421>

Why is DHA key? What turns light into a DC electric current and back into light? DHA is the only lipid that does it. Can genes be activated by light? YEP
Life = photoelectric effect, magnetism, and water. DHA=DC current=leptin. Light activates and deactivates genes using Archimedes principle.

[http://www.genengnews.com/gen-news-highlights/scientists-coax-genes-into-the-light/81250911/](http://www.genengnews.com/gen-news-highlights/scientists-coax-genes-into-the-light/81250911/)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-12T04:07:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-the-marine-food-chain-link-to-land-food-chain.12894/post-155423>

This helps explain why the DC current is linked to regeneration in all plants and animals on this planet. Dr. Robert O. Becker's research found on the surface of water and proteins there is a brisk DC current. All tissues need a higher redox potential to regenerate. A low redox means low DC current and low tissue DHA levels.
**Pay attention to sunsets and dim light at night.** Though light was known to influence the circadian mechanism via melanopsin, according to the literature, its effects on non-circadian processes were considered minor from 2005 to 2013.

That is now changing, especially when you understand how dim light really works in the brain. It has been recently shown in mammals that when melanopsin signaling is altered in the CNS for any reason at all, **mammals lose the ability to catch up on sleep permanently**. As they lose the ability to sleep, and they simultaneously lose autographic efficiency and _their regenerative potential_ for all tissues, and **they lose the ability to tap into Becker’s DC current.**

When you lose the DC current, it means your redox potential is bad. It also means your tissues are deficient in DHA. **This is why a lack of sleep often leads to disease and an early death**. Autophagy is absent in these cases. Over time, these organisms will lose their memory and they will gain more fat mass as this alteration of melanopsin continues.

### nicld — 2015-02-12T16:32:51+0200

This is what time my bladder has been waking me up and it is getting OLD!!!!. I really try to ignore it and go back to sleep but sometimes I just cannot and have to go. Once I do get up, I try to go back to sleep, sometimes it works and sometimes it does not.

I reread CT7 in hopes of finding something that will let me know why this is happening and I could not really come up with anything.

The one thing that I do in the evenings (usually around 7 pm) is have a nice cup of tea but that is about the only liquid I have after supper but I do drink alot during the day. I will try to stop the evening tea and see if that helps.

If anyone has any ideas, I would greatly appreciate it.

### Sue-UK — 2015-02-15T11:30:50+0200

So maybe its less about my tweaking my water intake during the evening and more about an improvement in the ADH diurnal rhythm.....Whether that is increasing ADH levels at the right time, or my kidneys becoming more sensitive to the signals I'm not sure ..... I do think improving my water consumption has helped though. The other thought I've had is that if the ADH diurnal rhythm isn't entrained to the light cycle (maybe its the earth rotation?) perhaps my outside CT is helping ...... I always try and look up and observe the sun's position in the sky, or mentally acknowledge where it is if I can't see it, in winter it's like trying to set my internal clock to the right time by the earth's position in relation to the sun once a day, and hoping it will keeping ticking at the right tempo, bio hacking what might help or hinder

# Thread: The Right Way To Exercise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-right-way-to-exercise.12902/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BTA — 2015-02-12T18:48:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-right-way-to-exercise.12902/post-155477>

When I see somebody doing crossfit or swinging kettlebells - I know they have no clue how to workout. People are brain washed to think running improves cardio, jumping on boxes improves performance and so on... all of which is garbage. The body has only one way to function - lift very heavy & very slow. Which is way the method called Super Slow is the only effective method based on science. Call it BS - that's fine but do your homework.

Great article:

**P90X, Insanity and Similar Nonsense**

By [Drew Baye](http://baye.com/author/baye/)

Hard workout today:
MedX Neck Extension
MedX Neck Flexion
Weighted Chin Up on the Nautilus Omni Multi Exercise (OME)
MedX Shoulder Press
MedX Leg Press
OME Wrist Curl with thick bar attachment
OME Wrist Extension with thick bar attachment
Total workout time was around 15 minutes. I’ll do it again in 3 or 4 days. Normally I’d take a _little_ longer, around 20 minutes or so – not because I do more exercises but because I rest longer between sets – but I had to get it done before my next client arrived for his workout.
Why so little? Because if done properly _that’s all it takes_. The most important factor in stimulating increases in muscular strength and size, as well as all the other possible benefits of exercise, is the degree of _effort_. The effort put into a workout is what determines its effectiveness, rather than the volume of work performed. If the level of effort is high enough, very little exercise is required, but no amount of exercise will produce much in the way of results if the effort is not high enough and if too much of any type of exercise is performed the results will be worse, not better.
I’m often asked what I think about programs like P90X, Insanity, and similar popular training programs, and one of the first things I usually point out is the volume of exercise is way too high while the quality – both in terms of type of exercise and the manner of performance instructed – is very low.
To save myself the time of having to answer the same questions about these programs over and over, the following is a brief summary of what is wrong with them, and what you should be doing instead.
**Form**
I’ve watched some of the videos and the form on the exercises is atrocious. Too fast, and little attention to proper form despite paying lip service to it. Very fast, sloppy reps.
_What you should be doing instead:_
Move in a slow, controlled manner, especially when reversing direction between lifting and lowering the weight. Don’t just make the weight go up and down, take your time with it and focus on intensely contracting the target muscles throughout the entire exercise.
**Volume**
These programs include almost an hour of exercise plus an additional and completely unnecessary fifteen minutes or so of [abdominal work](http://baye.com/ripped-abs/) a few days a week. These programs have you doing way too many exercises and sets. Unless you’re taking steroids this is overtraining, and even if you are this volume of exercise is completely unnecessary and counterproductive. With proper training and diet you could achieve the same or better results with _less than one hour of training per week, and in some cases less than thirty minutes._
Speaking of which, the results you see in the testimonials have everything to do with the diet and almost nothing to do with the idiotic training program. I’ve had men lose over 30 pounds of fat and women around 20 with less total training time over a period of _two months_ than the P90X or Insanity programs require in _a single week_.
_What you should be doing instead:_
One hard set of only one or two exercises per muscle group, working out no more than three non-consecutive days per week. Advanced trainees should do _less_, rather than more exercise.
**“Muscle Confusion”**
The concept of “muscle confusion” is nonsense based on a misunderstanding of motor learning principles. Muscles do not become resistant to stimulation from a particular exercise, they only appear to because improvements are faster initially due to neural adaptations and slow down after around six to eight weeks when adaptation starts to be more due to hypertrophy. Changing exercises too frequently is a huge mistake. I’ve already addressed this in detail in [The Ultimate Routine](http://baye.com/the-ultimate-routine/).
_What you should be doing instead:_
Don’t constantly vary your workouts. Consistently follow a [well designed workout or routine](http://baye.com/store/high-intensity-workouts/) that effectively works all the major muscle groups and focus on getting as strong as possible on that. If you plateau it’s more likely you need a reduction in workout volume or more recovery time between workouts and _not_ to “confuse” your muscles by changing exercises.
**Abdominal or “Core” Workouts**
Fifteen minutes of abdominal work is neither necessary nor beneficial. One or two exercises for the abdominal muscles, a flexion and a rotation or lateral flexion movement are all that is required. Maybe four, if you’re doing an occasional [abdominal specialization workout](http://baye.com/store/high-intensity-workouts/), but even this should only take a few minutes to complete, and the reason for doing so is to strengthen the muscles, not to improve abdominal definition. Getting [ripped abs](http://baye.com/ripped-abs/) is almost entirely a matter of diet and has very little to do with abdominal exercises or workouts.
_What you should be doing instead:_
Only one hard set of only one or two abdominal exercises at the end of your workout, which should take no more than one to three minutes. If you want ripped abs you need to focus on your diet.

**Plyometrics**
Plyometrics are incredibly stupid. They are relatively ineffective for building strength, unnecessary for developing speed or explosiveness or improving rate of force development, and carry a _very_ high risk of injury. They have no place in any training program.
_What you should be doing instead:_
If you want to develop speed or explosiveness _simply focus on getting stronger_. All these different things are expressions of strength, rather than different types as some people believe. You don’t need to train one way for “maximum strength”, another for “speed strength” another for “explosive strength” or any of the other types these people come up with to make their programs more complex and scientific sounding than they need to be.
**Cardio**
Cardio is included in these programs with the claim it is beneficial for both fat burning and cardiovascular conditioning, however it is very inefficient for fat burning and unnecessary for cardiovascular and metabolic conditioning if you’re already performing a proper high intensity strength training program. Additionally, the high impact and repetitive nature of the movements is more likely to cause injuries and contribute to joint problems in the long run, unlike high intensity strength training which is much safer for the joints when performed properly.
_What you should be doing instead:_
If you want to become leaner you don’t need extra activity to burn calories – which is generally a very inefficient waste of time – you need to eat fewer calories and consume a diet which creates a hormonal situation conducive to fat loss. If you want to improve cardiovascular and metabolic conditioning, high intensity strength training will do that, and you can emphasize it further by limiting rest between exercises.
**Supplements**
The P90X and Insanity programs also encourage the use of supplements, including a meal replacement shake and other products sold by Beachbody. While some supplements are beneficial, they are not at all necessary to achieve incredible results from training. The same seems to be the case with other companies and makes me wonder if the workout programs were developed secondary to the supplements as a means of marketing them, kind of like every new training program introduced on certain bodybuilding web sites.
_What you should be doing instead:_
Whatever your training goals, whether you want more muscle, less fat, better performance or health, your biggest nutritional concern should be your diet. Get that in order first, then _if you can identify a legitimate need or benefit_, consider supplementation. Be very skeptical of supplement advertising, though. Most claims are complete BS.

**But what about those testimonials?**
Beachbody, the company that sells P90X, Insanity, Hip Hop Abs and similar nonsense have done one thing right – marketing. Their infomercials are well done and they have some impressive testimonials. However, as I already mentioned, the results those people achieved were mostly due to the diet. They could have done the same or better faster and with only a small fraction of the total training time. **As Nautilus inventor Arthur Jones** was fond of saying, the fact that a particular method produced some result is not proof the same or better results could not have been achieved by some other means, more quickly and more efficiently.

### JoeBranca — 2015-03-01T18:27:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-right-way-to-exercise.12902/post-156976>

fwiw Ido weighs in on super slow w machines:

> "revolutionary approach" and a forgotten method that has been forgotten for a good reason. This has died many years ago, in athletic circles, with the failed attempts of movement transfer. Movement is a game in which 1+1+1=17. It might work in very specific and isolated cases in which a weak link is addressed but even then integration is needed. As for injury prevention - dont delude yourself. Machines only appear to be safer, just like sitting on the sofa, the type of injuries and their nature merely change. You increase your sensitivity for 'out of alignment' scenarios, you increase risk of overuse injuries, weaken stabilizers and end goal motor pattern and more and more. I've never met someone who trains with machines as a center piece and can demonstrate that strength in movement efficiently. It should be reserved for very very isolated cases.

### Harvey Cheyne Jr. — 2016-10-31T16:50:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-right-way-to-exercise.12902/post-203467>

My understanding/experience: going to failure easily leads to overtraining. I had a bout of overtraining with Superslow after reading that (SS founder) Ken Hutchins' best student would go so hard, he would lay down in the shower and puke afterward. I thought "I will do that; I like that kind of intensity. Sounds good."

"CNS burnout: just overtraining with a cool and scary name attached".

Maybe I did it too frequently. That was my mistake with cold showers; I didn't build up slowly enough and went too cold, too long, too often, and wound up with pneumonia three times until I learned to respect my limits. Now I am ramping the cold exposure much more slowly.

"The human body is very resilient. It ain't made out of glass. However, it has been the experience of many trainees that long term training to failure leaves them very burnt out. This can't be counteracted by perpetually decreasing volume and frequency. " So I no longer go to failure (with weights or cold), and I am continuing to increase my strength and cold tolerance (based on weights pushed/time and temperature in the cold).

[http://www.mikelipowski.com/2015/01/02/slow-training-bodybuilding/](http://www.mikelipowski.com/2015/01/02/slow-training-bodybuilding/)

My $.02

YMMV

### timmothysmith01 — 2023-01-05T16:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-right-way-to-exercise.12902/post-318704>

-   Follow Workout Hydration Rules
-   Use Proper Warm-Up Exercises
-   Cool Down Appropriately
-   Keep Moving and Heart Rate Elevated
-   Strength Train Properly
-   Consider High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)
-   Rest and Slowly Progress without Injury
-   Enjoy Your Exercise

# Thread: Cramping last night
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cramping-last-night.12905/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nittygrittydanny — 2015-02-12T19:18:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cramping-last-night.12905/post-155480>

I work in a Lab testing asphalt and granite. Yesterday was BUSY and I was in and out of the lab (bright sunny day in Marianna Fl.) I drank about 96ozs of good water throughout the day. As I was driving home (about 1hr 15min commute) my hands, mostly thumb and index fingers, pinky were cramping pretty bad, got worse in pain index after I ate dinner (flank steak, green beans and water). I had umpired a softball game the evening prior and drank bottled water while doing so. I noticed my toes cramping some early in the game but it subsided and didn't return? I drank about the same amount of water the day of the game, but it was cool and overcast most of the day so I didn't get as much sun exposure that day.

I had 2 bottles of Coconut water in the cabinet that I drank after dinner and ran my hands under cold water (semi CT), that helped alot. Just read this about potassium on here. Is there a better source of K+ than coconut water? I normally keep it on hand and drink prior to and after Reff'ing a football game due to the amount of sweating in addition to the hydration i do prior to working a game.

**You must pay attention to your Potassium (K+) levels**. The reason potassium is critical inside a cell, and supersedes magnesium and sodium ion concentration is because it is naturally tied to beta and gamma carboxyl side chain groups found in proteins, where potassium specifically binds because of “quantum advantages.” These beta and gamma carboxyl bonds allows K+ to ADSORB to this protein site and donate electrons (this is why K+ has the second highest redox potential to Lithium in life; see below) to the polarized water gel crystal.

Danny

My daughter plays softball and has a tough time at the beginning of the season with the sun. I've been starting her on Quinton isotonic seawater electrolytes and that helps a lot.

# Thread: Thinking about Copper
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-copper.12906/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2015-02-12T23:18:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thinking-about-copper.12906/post-155493>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/)

> **Copper methanobactin: a molecule whose time has come**
> [Ramakrishnan Balasubramanian](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Balasubramanian%20R%5Bauth%5D) and [Amy C. Rosenzweig](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Rosenzweig%20AC%5Bauth%5D)
> [Author information ►](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#) [Copyright and License information ►](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#)
>
> The publisher's final edited version of this article is available at [Curr Opin Chem Biol](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/elink.fcgi?dbfrom=pubmed&retmode=ref&cmd=prlinks&id=18313412)
> See other articles in PMC that [cite](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/citedby/) the published article.
>
> [Go to:](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#)
> **Summary of recent advances**
> Copper plays a key role in the physiology of methanotrophs. One way that these bacteria meet their high copper requirement is by the biosynthesis and release of high affinity copper-binding compounds called methanobactins. Recent advances in methanobactin characterization include the first crystal structure, detailed spectroscopic analyses, and studies of metal ion specificity. Methanobactin may function in copper uptake, regulation of methane monooxygenase expression, protection against copper toxicity, and particulate methane monooxygenase activity. Methanobactin can extract copper from insoluble minerals and could be important for mineral weathering. Many methanobactin properties are reminiscent of iron siderophores, suggesting a similar mechanism of handling. Methanobactin-like compounds have also been identified in yeast mitochondria, suggesting that these molecules are a more universal phenomenon.
>
> [Go to:](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#)
> **Introduction**
> Copper is central to the physiology of methanotrophs, gram-negative bacteria that use methane as their sole carbon source \[[1](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R1)\]. In the first step of carbon assimilation by methanotrophs, methane is oxidized to methanol by methane monooxygenase enzymes (MMOs). All methanotrophs except for one species produce particulate MMO (pMMO), a three-subunit, integral membrane protein complex that is widely believed to contain a copper active site \[[2](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R2)\]. Under conditions of copper starvation, a few methanotrophs can also express a soluble MMO (sMMO) that contains a diiron active site. In strains that express both pMMO and sMMO, copper in the growth medium represses transcription of the sMMO genes while leading to the expression of pMMO and formation of extensive intracytoplasmic membranes that house pMMO. A number of additional proteins are also differentially expressed as a function of copper concentrations in the growth medium \[[3](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R3)\]. Given the important role of copper in both metabolic switching and pMMO chemistry, an effective copper acquisition system is critical.
>
> Evidence for a specific copper uptake system was obtained initially in the early 1990s from studies of_Methylosinus trichosporium_ OB3b mutants that constitutively express sMMO and do not express active pMMO \[[4](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R4),[5](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R5)\]. The extracellular media from these strains also contains unusually high concentrations of copper. On the basis of these data, Georgiou and coworkers postulated the existence of small copper binding complexes that are excreted by the cells under copper starvation conditions \[[5](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R5)\]. Over the next decade, several laboratories isolated and partially characterized several such compounds from both _M. trichosporium_ OB3b \[[6](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R6),[7](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R7)\] and _Methylococcus capsulatus_ (Bath) \[[8](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R8)\]. Designated copper binding compounds (CBCs) or copper binding ligands (CBLs), these molecules had molecular masses ranging from 382 to 1218 Da and bound copper ions with high affinity. Issues with homogeneity and degradation precluded determining the chemical composition of these compounds until recently, however. After the development of a successful purification protocol by Graham, DiSpirito and coworkers, the crystal structure of a copper binding compound from _M. trichosporium_ OB3b was reported in 2004, and the molecule was renamed methanobactin (mb) \[[9](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R9)–[11](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685176/#R11)\]. The crystal structure and subsequent biochemical, spectroscopic, and functional studies over the past three years have provided new insight into the properties and function of mb. This review summarizes these recent advances and considers the possibility that mb-like molecules may be a more general phenomenon.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Josh — 2015-02-13T00:40:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nenets.12908/post-155497>

> The Nenets are reindeer herders, migrating across the Yamal peninsula, thriving for more then a millennium with temperatures from minus 50°C in winter to 35°C in summer. Their annual migration of over a 1000 km includes a 48 km crossing of the frozen waters of the Ob River.
> **_“If you don’t drink warm blood and eat fresh meat, you are doomed to die on the tundra”_**
> The discovery of oil and gas reserves in the 1970s and the expanding infrastructure on the peninsula, has challenged their indigenous lifestyle. From the late Stalin period, all children have been enrolled in Soviet boarding schools, this has become a part of the typical Nenets life cycle.

[http://www.beforethey.com/journey/siberia---yamal](http://www.beforethey.com/journey/siberia---yamal)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:00:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156006>

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:02:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156007>

(**a**) Phylogentic tree of the melastatin subfamily of TRP channels. Note that TRPM3 is the closest relative of the founding member melastatin (TRPM1). (**b**) Pregnenolone is synthesized from cholesterol and is considered to be the prohormone of all steroid hormones. 17

\-hydroxylase activity converts pregnenolone to 17-hydroxypregnenolone (170H-pregnenolone) and converts progesterone to 170H-progesterone. DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone) is converted to testosterone and oestrogenes. Sulphonation of pregnenolone leads to the formation of PS, a robust TRPM3 agonist. (**c**) Some of the positive effects attributed to pregnenolone, explaining its epithet 'fountain of youth'.

> **A TRP channel-steroid marriage**
> Bernd Nilius[1](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#a1) & Thomas Voets[1](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#a1)
>
>
> 1.  Bernd Nilius and Thomas Voets are in the Department of Molecular Cell Biology, Laboratory of Ion Channel Research, KU Leuven, Campus Gasthuisberg, Herestraat 49, bus 802, B-3000 Leuven, Belgium.
>     e-mail: [bernd.nilius@med.kuleuven.be](mailto:bernd.nilius@med.kuleuven.be)
>
>
> **Abstract**
> A surprising functional association between TRPM3, a mysterious member of the family of transient receptor potential (TRP) cation channels, and the sulphated version of pregnenolone, 'mother' of all steroid hormones, has been identified.
>
> The TRP family ([Fig. 1a](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/full/ncb1208-1383.html#f1)) carries the stigma of being 'promiscuous', as its members can be tempted to gate in response to a surprising variety of stimuli, including temperature and mechanical stress, as well as various endogenous and exogenous chemicals.

[http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/fig\_tab/ncb1208-1383\_F1.html](http://www.nature.com/ncb/journal/v10/n12/fig_tab/ncb1208-1383_F1.html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:09:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156009>

> Abstract Steroid hormones are made from cholesterol, primarily derived from lipoproteins that enter cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis. In endo-lysosomes, cholesterol is released from cholesterol esters by lysosomal acid lipase (LAL; disordered in Wolman disease) and exported via Niemann-Pick type C (NPC) proteins (disordered in NPC disease). These diseases are characterized by accumulated cholesterol and cholesterol esters in most cell types. Mechanisms for trans-cytoplasmic cholesterol transport, membrane insertion, and retrieval from membranes are less clear. Cholesterol esters and “free” cholesterol are enzymatically interconverted in lipid droplets. Cholesterol transport to the cholesterolpoor outer mitochondrial membrane (OMM) appears to involve cholesterol transport proteins. Cytochrome P450scc (CYP11A1) then initiates steroidogenesis by converting cholesterol to pregnenolone on the inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM). Acute steroidogenic responses are regulated by cholesterol delivery from OMM to IMM, triggered by the steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR). Chronic steroidogenic capacity is determined by CYP11A1 gene transcription. StAR mutations cause congenital lipoid adrenal hyperplasia, with absent steroidogenesis, potentially lethal salt loss, and 46,XY sex reversal. StAR mutations initially destroy most, but not all steroidogenesis; low levels of StARindependent steroidogenesis are lost later due to cellular damage, explaining the clinical fi ndings. Rare P450scc mutations cause a similar syndrome. This review addresses these early steps in steroid biosynthesis.—Miller, W. L., and H. S. Bose. Early steps in steroidogenesis: intracellular cholesterol traffi cking. J. Lipid Res. 2011. 52: 2111–2135.

[http://www.jlr.org/content/52/12/2111.full.pdf+html](http://www.jlr.org/content/52/12/2111.full.pdf+html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:14:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156012>

Fig. 4.
(_A_) CYP11A1–Adx interface. CYP11A1–Adx contacts involve the interaction domain (F helix) and loop surrounding the Adx \[2Fe-2S\] cluster (yellow) and occur on the proximal surface of CYP11A1 (green). Direct hydrogen bonds between residues Lys109CYP11_A_1-Ala45Adx,, Trp418CYP11_A_1-Leu80Adx, and water-mediated hydrogen bonds, including residues Met120CYP11_A_1-Thr49Adx and Met120CYP11_A_1-Ala51Adx are displayed. Dashed lines indicate salt bridges and H-bonding interactions; water molecules are represented as small red spheres. (_B_) Predicted electron-transfer pathway from the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster to the heme iron proceeds from Fe1 of the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster via side- and main-chain atoms of Cys52Adx and Ala51Adx, through-space jump (3 Å), Gln422CYP11_A_1, Cys423CYP11_A_1 to the heme iron.

[http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139/F4.expansion.html](http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139/F4.expansion.html)

### Josh — 2015-02-19T02:22:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-156014>

> In humans, the precursor to all steroid hormones, pregnenolone, is synthesized from cholesterol by an enzyme complex comprising adrenodoxin reductase (AdR), adrenodoxin (Adx), and a cytochrome P450 (P450scc or CYP11A1). This complex not only plays a key role in steroidogenesis, but also has long been a model to study electron transfer, multistep catalysis, and C–C bond cleavage performed by monooxygenases. Detailed mechanistic understanding of these processes has been hindered by a lack of structural information. Here we present the crystal structure of the complex of human Adx and CYP11A1—the first of a complex between a eukaryotic CYP and its redox partner. The structures with substrate and a series of reaction intermediates allow us to define the mechanism underlying sequential hydroxylations of the cholesterol and suggest the mechanism of C–C bond cleavage. In the complex the \[2Fe-2S\] cluster of Adx is positioned 17.4 Å away from the heme iron of CYP11A1. This structure suggests that after an initial protein–protein association driven by electrostatic forces, the complex adopts an optimized geometry between the redox centers. Conservation of the interaction interface suggests that this mechanism is common for all mitochondrial P450s

[http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139.full.pdf+html](http://www.pnas.org/content/108/25/10139.full.pdf+html)

I think it might help you, you seem to have similar symptoms as me and it made a noticeable improvement for me. In fact Jack recommended it as soon as I posted about my calprotectin levels. I assume it would help by suppressing the bugs in the gut (hpylori in your case) which allows the immune system to work better which means less leaky gut, better liver function, and less overall inflammation.

Anyway I used silver botics ([http://www.healthjunction.co.uk/ame...er-32-fl-oz-946-ml?filter\_name=silver biotics](http://www.healthjunction.co.uk/american-biotech-labs-silver-biotics-10-ppm-silver-32-fl-oz-946-ml?filter_name=silver%20biotics)) but it is expensive as balls so it maybe cheaper to make your own especially if you can't import.

I'm using a DIY one right now but I'm not sure on the quality TBH, asin maybe it is producing too large particles that could cause blue skin. I guess it's okay short term anyway until I learn to make it right.

Anyway you need two 12-14 guage 99.95+% silver wire. 3 9V batteries linked together and connected to the silver wires via ordinary wires/crocodile clips. You need to hang the 2 wires into a litre/half litre jar filled with distilled (important) water. Leave the live wires suspended in the water for 10hrs ish and you've got colliodal silver. It will have a metalic taste if done right so keep testing. Should come to less than £20.

### seanb4 — 2015-03-04T10:28:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-157397>

Spent 8 days on micronized Pregnenolone and DHEA at 50mg each. Noticed digestion improved as did overall energy however sleep was still poor. Not as poor as it was the first few days however I was waking up regularly through the night. Always felt tired come morning. Overall I felt better on preg 50 + dhea 50 than I did without any supps but I feel I can tweak this so sleep isn't destroyed.

Going to take jansz advice and drop the preg for a week starting today and note the changes. Also the new brand of DHEA I just got isn't micronized so I am changing 2 variables at once. I'm taking 400mg of this as apparently only 10-15% gets absorbed properly orally. Would this work out roughly the same dose as the micronized or is there some other reason I can't use non-micronized?

### seanb4 — 2015-03-06T14:52:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-157631>

On day 3 of taking just DHEA without Preg. Sleep seems better however digestion seems worse. I am getting heaviness in my stomach again and my stomach feels "closed off" more and I can't drink as much water even though I am thirsty.
It's still early days but I would take the benefits in digestion over the benefits of sleep...
I will wait at least a week on just DHEA and if digestion is still bad add pregnenolone in smaller doses. Maybe 25mg.

### seanb4 — 2016-01-21T21:50:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenal-stress-profile.12918/post-187028>

Hey @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/), hope you don't mind looking over this old thread again. I'm considering trying the DHEA again. I stopped it ages ago as it was causing severe palpitations and breathlessness, also ruining my sleep. Do you think the dosage was too high? What should I do differently?

# Thread: Mikes Awesome Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 36
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MIKE WILKINSON — 2015-02-14T14:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-155596>

Hi friends
I am not the sort of person that reflects a lot as I am usually busy looking forward all the time
so this is certainly a new experience for me
I only found out about this website when visiting Josh Lamaro down in Torquay
When I was 26 (now 52) I was living in Airlie Beach QLD when I got hit by car
My own stupid fault as I was not looking when crossing a busy highway as a pedestrian
Miraculously no major damage apart from the Car which was doing 80 at the time
I think my body went into some type shock because 2weeks later my red hair started falling out
A few years later I started being concerned about my health with with a really bad fatigue mainly affecting me after sports then feeling really tired all the time
symptoms like elevated heart rate try 130bpm 6hours after exercise then sinuses that I never had before ever and had to stop doing exercise needed lots of sleep etc wasn't probably the classic "chronic fatigue symptoms no aches but something was definitely wrong
Even today I have to be very careful with how much exercise I do which is a pain because I love the outdoors
The fatigue gradually subsided with rest and a good diet but somehow has lingered in the background if I don't take care
Fast forward I noticed a tremor in my right hand and to cut a long story short was recently diagnosed with Parkinsons
Main symptoms from the Parkinson's down my right side with the tremor in my arm but the thing that's really quite debilitating at times is my walking movement with my right leg

I'm still very active and joined gym and working full time
Love to surf when I can and renovating the house when I have time on weekends
Couple of months ago I found out about the Paleo diet and the effect on autoimmune diseases
There is some recent debate on whether Parkinsons is an auto immune disease Anyway
Been Paleo for over four weeks now and strictly off Gluten
Body seems to be detoxing with my sinuses going rampant off the Gluten

Recently visited Josh Lamaro who hopefully will be setting me on a program after my baseline tests come in
I have never had a proper series of blood tests properly analysed

Other than that Always optimistic
I figure I can't wait for conventional medicine to do its thing with doctors just treating the symptoms
There must be more to these health issues than just diet
I have a feeling that a ketogenic diet combined with Joshes guidance and Jack Kruse protocols might make a difference
If it does make a difference I figured Jacks protocols will become famous very quickly as there are 30 people being diagnosed every day with Parkinsons in Australiaand they are getting younger
I would love to one day share my success and cure others with this disease
HELL YEAH LET DO IT !!!

Mike

### nonchalant — 2015-02-14T18:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-155613>

I wish more people here in the US would realize that people are getting sick at younger ages. Anytime I bring up that subject, people just point to lack of exercise (though few people jogged when I was a youngster) and all the fast food places. And TV. Well, they may have a point about TV, but not for the reason they mean.

### Inger — 2015-02-14T19:14:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-155617>

### Martin — 2015-02-14T19:25:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-155619>

Welcome, Mike. This is the place to find Nature.

The onset of your maladies after that accident is an important link. Lots of patients are reporting similar symptoms after a car wreck, loss of loved one, "flu-like illness", and other stressed-out situations.

This happened to me nearly seven years ago now. BAD "flu-like" illness that I never quite recovered from, but I've had some good gains since finding this site. Silver lining to an awful time in my life.

Goggle "NO/ONOO cycle" and "bucket theory". I'm studying how this poorly understood illness (ME/CFS/MCS) is brought on by those big events. So far, I think it is a lack of DHA in cell membranes and how that affects an inability to clear peroxinitrite from cells and thus causing an endless loop that trashes our energy and health. I'm still getting to the details as to how this is linked. Fascinating subject, for me.

Dr Lamaro is on it, I'm sure. You're lucky to have him in your camp. His infographic on ME/CFS that he posted on this site really helped me conceptualize what the hell is going on with this debilitating condition. Thanks Paleo Osteo!!!

keep us posted, we'll all learn something,

NSH

### MIKE WILKINSON — 2015-02-15T01:14:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-155640>

Wow thanks for the support guys

I must admit I have a lot of learning here some very knowledgeable people on this forum
So far I've made these chages:
Quit Beer One or two glasses of red only a week
Gone Paleo removed Gluten
Changed to fluoride free water this week
Ordered some blue blocker glasses
Trying to get to sleep and a good time as I have had very bad habits
This Emf thing is really interesting
All I know is when I'm on the computer most of the day does my head in and makes my Parkinsons symptoms a lot worse
I suppose it doesn't help having the main electricity and TV connections to the front of my bedroom and a smart meter outside my wall ha ha ouch !
I need to try and find a balance with work on the computer
Keep you posted Martin thanks for the advise mate

### Penny — 2015-04-01T18:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mikes-awesome-journal.12929/post-160071>

I would be pounding lots of hydroxy b12... and just fell into this nattokinase pit this morning - don't even remember how I got here but - it seems to be great for protein folding diseases -
**Amyloid-degrading ability of nattokinase from Bacillus subtilis natto.**
[Hsu RL](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hsu%20RL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402)1, [Lee KT](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lee%20KT%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Wang JH](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Wang%20JH%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Lee LY](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lee%20LY%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402), [Chen RP](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chen%20RP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=19117402).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19117402#)**

**Abstract**
More than 20 unrelated proteins can form amyloid fibrils in vivo which are related to various diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, prion disease, and systematic amyloidosis. Amyloid fibrils are an ordered protein aggregate with a lamellar cross-beta structure. Enhancing amyloid clearance is one of the targets of the therapy of these amyloid-related diseases. Although there is debate on whether the toxicity is due to amyloids or their precursors, research on the degradation of amyloids may help prevent or alleviate these diseases. In this study, we explored the amyloid-degrading ability of nattokinase, a fibrinolytic subtilisin-like serine protease, and determined the optimal conditions for amyloid hydrolysis. This ability is shared by proteinase K and subtilisin Carlsberg, but not by trypsin or plasmin.

Can you opt out of a smart meter in AU? We can here for a fee... which I gladly paid -

# Thread: Cold baths and migraine onset!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SarahF — 2015-02-14T17:26:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/post-155605>

After a week or so of immersing myself in the coldest water from the tap in the UK for about 30 minutes I started to get daily migraines. I have been a sufferer before but previously they were associated with my menstrual cycle. The only thing I could come up with after hours searching online, was a possible link with histamine release from any change in temperature, women in their late forties seem to be more vulnerable to this. So I have embarked upon a lower histamine diet to see if I can reintroduce the cold baths, but don't really have any idea if I am barking up the wrong tree or not. Does anyone have any other thoughts/experience with this sort of symptom?

### Inger — 2015-02-14T18:17:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/post-155609>

The only thing I do know is, at the beginning of CT you might experience detox... I did too. Migraines could be because of that......? Just thinking out loud...
I would maybe take some liver support like milk thistle, and drink plenty of good water, back off a little with the CT and increase slower. Maybe some B vitamins too or loads of raw egg yolks. I craved those all the time when I started CT
You can always try a lower histamine diet too, it want hurt I think!

Have you bin doing the CT protocols like Jack has said it to be done, or did you just jumped straight into the baths?
If that is - it might be wise to follow the CT protocol........ and start with face dunks

### nonchalant — 2015-02-14T18:24:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/post-155611>

At the beginning I only soaked for about 7 minutes or so. But in a week or two, I was able to be comfortable for much longer times.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-05-08T03:53:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/post-163565>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=155609)
>
> The only thing I do know is, at the beginning of CT you might experience detox... I did too. Migraines could be because of that......? Just thinking out loud...
> I would maybe take some liver support like milk thistle, and drink plenty of good water, back off a little with the CT and increase slower. Maybe some B vitamins too or loads of raw egg yolks. I craved those all the time when I started CT
> You can always try a lower histamine diet too, it want hurt I think!
>
> Have you bin doing the CT protocols like Jack has said it to be done, or did you just jumped straight into the baths?
> If that is - it might be wise to follow the CT protocol........ and start with face dunks

^^^^^This is what I think too. I've been getting headaches a bit more 'unpredictably' since I started CT as well.. but I get them all the time anyway. The headache 'patterns' just seem to be a little more unpredictable. For instance, I had one headache that came on fast and furious at work even though I really tried all I could to avoid one ...like drink TONS of water. That doesn't happen usually.

### Janet Shold — 2018-02-20T08:19:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-baths-and-migraine-onset.12930/post-235174>

I wonder what the temperature of the water is. Here, in the middle of Vancouver island (slightly south of the UK) the water runs at about 42 degrees this time of year, so I use a thermometer and adjust the temperature to 50f.

# Thread: Advice needed for leaky gut and leptin reset.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-needed-for-leaky-gut-and-leptin-reset.12931/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BWJustice — 2015-02-14T21:28:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-needed-for-leaky-gut-and-leptin-reset.12931/post-155623>

I would appreciate any advice on the level of physical activity tolerated during leptin reset and leaky gut fix. I'm a relatively fit T2D and have been eating ketogenic for several years. My blood sugar can reach mid mid 80's after 72 hrs of fasting or 72 hours on coconut oil only. Normally my HB1ac is around125 and I've been off all meds for a year. I was taking 2000mg of berberine daily until I read about it causing mitochondrial oxidative stress. This may not be an issue since it actually seems more effective than the 2000mg of metformin I was on for 10 years. Isn't a certain amount of oxidative stress a good thing in your mitochondria? My main issue has been inflammation with neck and left shoulder pain, as well as severe jaw and headaches. Ice is my friend. Early morning is about the only time I can't control blood sugar. I would like to be physically active and have plenty of time to do so but am afraid to trigger more inflammation with the severe headaches. If you have any experiences to share, I would be greatly appreciative.

### caroline — 2015-02-16T02:59:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-needed-for-leaky-gut-and-leptin-reset.12931/post-155740>

Have you read the leptin reset? C/T is really important ...there are easy start guides on the home page.

Please put up a pic ..... JK's forum rule

start a journal in the journal section - Dr. K. needs context

Have you read Dr. K.'s book? The Epi-Paleo RX

this book is invaluable to wrap your head around all of this.

### BWJustice — 2015-02-16T04:37:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-needed-for-leaky-gut-and-leptin-reset.12931/post-155747>

>
> Please put up a pic ..... JK's forum rule
>
> start a journal in the journal section - Dr. K. needs context
>
> Have you read Dr. K.'s book? The Epi-Paleo RX
>
> this book is invaluable to wrap your head around all of this.

Your response is much appreciated. I'm in the process of reading Paleo Rx again. Initially thought only a section or two applied to me, but now I realize how everything is connected to everything , all the time. Much learning to do, so I'm actually making a lot of notes as I re educate myself. There are no cookie cutter solutions, so I'm beginning to understand how important labs will be, as well as individual strengths and weaknesses, such as fitness levels and habits. I will post a journal and picture soon. Thank You!

# Thread: RewiringTheBrain - Moving away from AF & Anxiety
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Knghe — 2015-02-15T00:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-155629>

So, as my last post described: My being (brain&body) is in a state of 'cellular' anxiousness. It is very distractable, anxious, unpeaceful, unable to focus, nervous, etc etc etc.

I am improving, but still in a state of imbalance (mind&body).
My brain feels very chaotic.

As Jack and the rest have suggested camping, yet I cannot do that at this time.

So in an attempt to rewire and calm my brain, nervous system, etc, I will embark on the follow routine/protocol:

1\. Jump in my cold pool (routinely).
2\. Ground myself at the park grass (routinely).
3\. Up my intake of raw oysters (weekly/biweekly).
4\. Do my best to reduce exposure to EMF (purchased a Q-Link Pendant => any thoughts?)
5\. Constantly use my breathing to ground myself (breathing with my whole body, breathing into my feet) \[I think this is key because my being feels very ungrounded right now, easily flustered\]

These steps I will take to heart and apply them into my life inorder to get me out of this sub optimal state of inner anxiousness. (I know what it feels like to be completely balanced because this is the first time I ever experienced any time of 'anxiety disorder')

Does any have any advice or anything else to say?
Has any1 ever beat this very strange sensation of bodily/cellular/neuro anxiety?
Are these good steps which will help me heal and get out of this chronic anxious state-of-being?

My brain just feels totally imbalanced at the moment (although improving).

### Inger — 2015-02-15T08:14:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-155666>

Well... I have felt that anxiety often. I used to be a very anxious person.

My advice would be, the things you list above are great, + eat ketogenic with lots of seafood, raw always worked best for anxiety for me. Don´t ask me why!
Then, look up into the sky, often.... be as much outside in daylight as you can be in the dark in the evenings after sunset! This is so important. You need the opposites, the dark AND the light.
Try to spend at least 1 hour in the woods / nature every day when light outside. And watch up into the sky. Take off your glasses  (important!)

Jumps into the cold pool are great for anxiety

### Knghe — 2015-02-15T22:13:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-155708>

Thank you so much,

My brain has been in a drifting/daydreaming/extremely, chronically introspective state for so long it sucks soo bad.

Its a very unlively state of existance.

### Antonis — 2018-07-23T10:08:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-246498>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=155666)
>
> Well... I have felt that anxiety often. I used to be a very anxious person.
>
> My advice would be, the things you list above are great, + eat ketogenic with lots of seafood, raw always worked best for anxiety for me. Don´t ask me why!
> Then, look up into the sky, often.... be as much outside in daylight as you can be in the dark in the evenings after sunset! This is so important. You need the opposites, the dark AND the light.
> Try to spend at least 1 hour in the woods / nature every day when light outside. And watch up into the sky. Take off your glasses  (important!)
>
> Jumps into the cold pool are great for anxiety

This is so helpful. I am very anxious person and am trying to do the best to beat the anxiety. I think for some people including me are like this because of our environment. Thanks for the tips

### Kara Nova — 2019-05-11T21:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-270224>

As a creative person/athlete who struggles with anxiety and finding balance, exercise that requires a strong mind-body connection like yoga or dance really helps to ground me. I’d also add that any creative activity, drawing, sculpting, photography, anything— helps to channel and ground that energy that needs somewhere to go. Give the anxiety an outlet and, at least while you’re “doing the thing,” you should feel less anxious.

### 8Phoenix — 2019-05-16T04:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewiringthebrain-moving-away-from-af-anxiety.12932/post-270589>

it seems that you are stuck in The **fight-or-flight response**
i agree with Jack when he recomend to go camping

May be if you go to sleep to a friend house fro a few days you would realize
That there is nothing wrong with you, dont try to supress your instinct instead embrace and follow through

there two ways to see this
1 somethig change recent in your enviroment
2 you just activate to defense system

for either one , you just need to identify the danger
