# Thread: Daytime napping & circadian signalling (?)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daytime-napping-circadian-signalling.13014/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-22T04:45:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daytime-napping-circadian-signalling.13014/post-156259>

I'm curious if anyone knows how much a daytime nap, like say an hour after work (4 PM-5 PM), if it would cause significant impairment of our natural cellular signalling.

When I come home from work, I feel like I absolutely need a nap and I will usually drift into a pretty deep sleep pretty fast - for about an hour and a half.

I've heard that it is not good practice but in other parts of the world, people take "siestas".
Also, a lot of animals will nap during the day as well.

If I skip that nap - boy I sure notice as I usually am quite fatigued the following day.
Has Jack discussed napping anywhere on this site?

### caroline — 2015-02-22T06:15:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daytime-napping-circadian-signalling.13014/post-156264>

good question Mike ....

### Inger — 2015-02-22T10:10:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daytime-napping-circadian-signalling.13014/post-156273>

Jack has said... sleep is good anytime !!!

My parents use to take naps at daytime often! And they are both pretty healthy.......

I know if I feel like taking a nap in the daytime.. I always do.. and it makes me feel awesome. It happens very seldom tho, but when, I know my body needs it.
I think we should always sleep when we feel like it! It is a clear signal from our body, it needs rest and regeneration
Maybe it is like... The Great Medicine. And it is all for free

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-22T21:08:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daytime-napping-circadian-signalling.13014/post-156306>

I know some very accomplished figures throughout history have been known to nap.
The only person I can remember right now is Winston Churchill (but he was also a drinker!).

..then you have the "Iron Lady" who days "napping is for wimps!"

A lot of people advise against napping saying that it will impair your nighttime sleep.
I'm really starting to trust Jack's approach which, like he says, is really not his approach at all.
..it's Mother Nature's approach.

I used to trust the "conventional wisdom" but that just made me sick!
I'm kind of bitter about it.

"Whole grains and dairy is good for you!"
(yeah right. Interesting that these are agriculture industry recommendations!)

"Eat several times a day to keep your blood sugar stable" -
(A great contributor to leptin resistance! I totally fell for this one!)

"Ketosis is dangerous when you are cutting down! "
(I learned a lot of garbage "rules" when I was weight training a lot in the '90's)

Now I ask myself instead "What would happen if this was say 100,00 years ago. and how does that answer match with 1,000,000,000 years ago?" and so on..

# Thread: Shellfish and food allergies (?)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-23T01:04:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/post-156320>

If shellfish are so healthy for us, why do so many people have terrible allergies to them?
When I told my naturopath that Dr Kruse recommends seafood, especially shellfish, she was surprised.

"When I do food sensitivity testing on people, shellfish is a really common reaction." she said.

Is it because they are "bottom feeders" and end up with a lot of toxins in them?

I barbequed a bunch of oysters this morning and honestly, I don't feel that great.
Not bad, but not good either.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-23T01:10:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/post-156322>

I just looked at my journal and saw that the last time I did CT (earlier this week - for 30 minutes), I didn't feel that great afterwards.
I actually started catching a cold again whereas the day before I felt great afterwards!
I ate oysters for breakfast on the day I didn't feel good.
(?)

### Inger — 2015-02-23T13:45:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/post-156374>

Was it the oysters in sunflower seed oil?

Oysters always makes me feel good.... I eat them raw tho.... never had canned. So I cant comment on that. I know CT makes you detox and if you do too much in the beginning it might make you a bit sick.... it will pass tho - just increase gradually, I would say

I do not believe shellfish has a lot of toxins in them... at least not if they lived in somewhat clean waters

The Baltic Sea is very polluted BTW.. still.. the health benefits of eating the fish from there is 40+ times bigger than the issues any pollution will do...
This I read on the site of the Finnish government
and I believe it, because the statistics show the fishermen (that eat a lot of Baltic Sea fish all the time) are among the healthiest in the population.....

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-02-25T05:23:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/post-156548>

Thanks for the clarification. I did escalate the CT pretty quickly - The first day I just dipped myself for about 20 seconds at a time, four times, but I kept my feet in the water the entire time - about 30 minutes.
The second time I managed to keep myself under (up to my chin) for a whole half hour!
I was so proud of myself but I didn't get the energy boost like the day before.
Maybe I was detoxing.

Hey Inger! I was born across the water from you - in Stockholm!

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-02-25T05:34:30+0200

in the face of a poor redox people can develop intolerances to many different proteins. it is the context that matters, not the "offending" protein, but rather they way the system handles it.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-25T05:58:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-and-food-allergies.13017/post-156550>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=156549)
>
> in the face of a poor redox people can develop intolerances to many different proteins. it is the context that matters, not the "offending" protein, but rather they way the system handles it.

This is a Random Clinical Trial using fish oil, but not fish, but what the hell  -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0091674903022711](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0091674903022711)

A more important paper **'Chitins and Chitosans as Immunoadjuvants and Non-Allergenic Drug Carriers**', which I just briefly skimmed  (but will give a full summary of eventually) -- [http://www.mdpi.com/1660-3397/8/2/292/htm](http://www.mdpi.com/1660-3397/8/2/292/htm)

This paper seems to indicate that the main allergen in shrimp is tropomyosin -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropomyosin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tropomyosin)

Tropomyosin is abundant in actin filaments. Actin and Integrin are critical components of the collagen super-information highway of the body.

Fundamentally, a "seafood allergy" is due to malfunctioning T-helper cells (usually Th2). We should be asking why these cells are malfunctioning, instead of avoiding shellfish forever (only avoid them until immune system is fixed).

What then likely happens with shellfish allergies, is that you get lots of tropomyosin, and create antibodies against this tropomyosin, which end up destroying your own collagen networks.

The issue here is T helper cells not having enough energy (electrons), and being unable to distinguish dietary tropomyosin, and over-reacting to cause unwanted antibody production.

\----

**Why I don't like allergy tests**

Allergy testing nowadays usually looks for the presence of particular antibodies (immunoglobulins). Typically you will have antibodies floating around in the body for quite some time, but the fundamental issue here is T-helper cell dysfunction.

The job of antibodies is to mark "invaders", and also to activate "defenders" (eg: phagocytes). Both of these processes depend on enzymatic function, which we know is definitely reliant on proton tunnelling and electric resonant effects.

So what happens when the redox potential of your body's own collagen networks improve, and the actin within them no longer resonate with the antibodies you produced when you were sick? The antibodies are no longer harmful , and you also stop producing extra antibodies because your T-helper cells can now recognise "invaders" appropriately.

\----

Sidenote: Chitin in shellfish is awesome animal fibre! ​

...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-26T08:29:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-2-is-now-live.13031/post-156679>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=156671)
>
> Yes indeed...........I just read that again and I remembered that a bit. BUT I will admit I am quite mystified by this thing of 'organ clocks' running too fast etc. I mean I just read an article in Scientific American that explained 'clock genes' quite well it made sense that say a bacterium is 'programmed' by genes to be in a certain state during daytime and another state in nighttime to take advantage from an energy standpoint to the different environment(s).
>
> But as far as organ clocks running too fast or the SCN running too slow I am drawing a blank on understanding. No need to answer if it will be explained later in the coming blogs or whatever but I am just saying I feel a bit mystified. Question: are organ clocks related to 'clock genes' or maybe better said are organ clocks determined by clock genes? And if so what does it mean to say 'clock genes' are running too fast (or too slow)
>
> I know this has implication for T2D (my problem) so is the organ clock of the pancreas too fast then and if so can I get a clue what that means? How does it tie into mitochondria? Into light release? Timing ?.................signalling ?.................some clues would be helpful.

Alright, let's use the case of T2D

First, bear in mind that we do not have an unlimited supply of energy. Also, energy production bears with it various consequences. If energy production introduced no negative tradeoff at all, we'd be able to stay awake all the time, do whatever we need to do, and still never have any notion of "being tired". Physics doesn't work that way of course .....

Now assume that the clock in the brain is running 1 minute slower than the rest of the body (huge slowdown, but this is just for illustrative purposes).

Of course, this is not an accurate picture of the signalling cascade. It's just there to illustrate problems with timing differences.

\-----
**4:00pm body clock, 3:59pm brain clock**:

You eat some food.
Gut says to brain, "Food incoming! Insulinz PLZ!!"

\----
**4:01pm body clock, 4:00pm brain clock**:

Gut says to brain, "WTF! Where is my insulinz??!!" .
Brain says, "Pancreas! Some insulin please!", unaware that the gut is pissed.
Pancreas goes, "Whatever bro " and gets to making insulin.

\----
**4:02pm body clock, 4:01pm brain clock**:

Brain thinks, "Another message??  Alright .... Moar insulin incoming".
Pancreas goes, "How much insulin do you want me to make?! "

\----
**6:00pm body clock, 5:59pm brain clock**:

Gut to brain, "Food is now finished with digesting, don't need dem insulinz anymore "

\----
**6:01pm body clock, 6:00pm brain clock**:

Gut to brain, "Hellloooooo???  Why do I still seeez Insulinz "
Brain goes, "Stop insulin Now "
Pancreas, "Wutever bro "

\----
**6:02pm body clock, 6:01pm brain clock**:

Brain now sees pissed-off message from gut

\----

In real life, you'd probably expect a similar story, but of course at much reduced timescales, with chronic signal degradation over time.

If the brain's clock is slow, it is essentially "forced to predict the future", and you know how well that goes

\----

Don't worry too much about "clock genes". Those are basically genes which encode the proteins that are used for signalling of circadian rhythms, so that the components of the body know when to switch off and save energy (again, wouldn't need this if we had unlimited energy).

Clock genes are basically a biological implementation detail. "A Slow SCN Clock" literally refers to a different physical time reading.

....

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-26T21:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-2-is-now-live.13031/post-156741>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=156679)
>
> Alright, let's use the case of T2D
>
> First, bear in mind that we do not have an unlimited supply of energy. Also, energy production bears with it various consequences. If energy production introduced no negative tradeoff at all, we'd be able to stay awake all the time, do whatever we need to do, and still never have any notion of "being tired". Physics doesn't work that way of course .....
>
> Now assume that the clock in the brain is running 1 minute slower than the rest of the body (huge slowdown, but this is just for illustrative purposes).
>
> Of course, this is not an accurate picture of the signalling cascade. It's just there to illustrate problems with timing differences.
>
> \-----
> **4:00pm body clock, 3:59pm brain clock**:
>
> You eat some food.
> Gut says to brain, "Food incoming! Insulinz PLZ!!"
>
> \----
> **4:01pm body clock, 4:00pm brain clock**:
>
> Gut says to brain, "WTF! Where is my insulinz??!!" .
> Brain says, "Pancreas! Some insulin please!", unaware that the gut is pissed.
> Pancreas goes, "Whatever bro " and gets to making insulin.
>
> \----
> **4:02pm body clock, 4:01pm brain clock**:
>
> Brain thinks, "Another message??  Alright .... Moar insulin incoming".
> Pancreas goes, "How much insulin do you want me to make?! "
>
> \----
> **6:00pm body clock, 5:59pm brain clock**:
>
> Gut to brain, "Food is now finished with digesting, don't need dem insulinz anymore "
>
> \----
> **6:01pm body clock, 6:00pm brain clock**:
>
> Gut to brain, "Hellloooooo???  Why do I still seeez Insulinz "
> Brain goes, "Stop insulin Now "
> Pancreas, "Wutever bro "
>
> \----
> **6:02pm body clock, 6:01pm brain clock**:
>
> Brain now sees pissed-off message from gut
>
> \----
>
> In real life, you'd probably expect a similar story, but of course at much reduced timescales, with chronic signal degradation over time.
>
> If the brain's clock is slow, it is essentially "forced to predict the future", and you know how well that goes
>
> \----
>
> Don't worry too much about "clock genes". Those are basically genes which encode the proteins that are used for signalling of circadian rhythms, so that the components of the body know when to switch off and save energy (again, wouldn't need this if we had unlimited energy).
>
> Clock genes are basically a biological implementation detail. "A Slow SCN Clock" literally refers to a different physical time reading.
>
> ....

Thanks a lot Yew I really appreciate your little 'play' Good script and well directed! Now I realize I should have paid attention to Cinnamon's question earlier somewhere............what does it mean to say the SCN clock needs to run 'faster' the real point is it runs AHEAD it is 'faster' in that sense. (Reminds me as a child my mom always had the clock -only one in the house no watches or god forbid cell phones in those days) - running 10mins AHEAD of the actual/real time. So her clock was running 'faster' but NOT faster in some exotic relativistic kind of way)

So now I am getting it a bit the SCN is primary it is BEFORE the pancreas 'clock' so to speak and has little or nothing to do with 'clock genes' etc. See the answer is way simpler than I thought I was looking for some exotic Einsteinian time speeds up relativistic obscure (mumbo jumbo?) . So when Dr K says the atomic clock for GPS runs 'faster' I think it would be more clear to say it runs AHEAD since it is compensating for the rotation of the Earth. BTW I have a hard time with Einstein I know he is a 'genius' but there is something about a lot of his stuff I just instinctively don't care for. Maybe something else to 'overcome'................

Thanks to every one for bearing with me. Maybe my 'job' here is to ask the 'stupid question'. BTW this morning I was thinking in terms of 'psychology' and say an infant with it's mother. Food is VERY important but it is not the ONLY thing..............there are things 'MORE' important for one thing in the womb there was no 'food' in the accepted sense of that word, after being born food becomes an issue of course...........but there are other things the sense of night and day, heat and cold, the mother's presence (or absence) the light in the environment and the light in the mother's eyes .................so many things. And I know it's just a 'conceit' but I thought this thing of making food the most important thing is like a limitation in thinking. It's like saying none of these things are so important only food is important. The 'paleo leaders' are in a way repeating that kind of history. Maybe a world so poor that the only thing is food..................anyway I am not explaining well but it made sense at the time!

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-26T21:42:32+0200

Next question HOW do I make my SCN run 'faster' actually ahead or at least I prefer that word................I'm thinking have plenty DHA in the eyes to pick up the sunshine, actually go in the sunshine, do everything to 'sense/feel' the environment, heat, cold all the rest of it. See one reason I was confused also is I'm thinking the SCN CANNOT run faster/ahead of the pancreas say because it is getting it's 'information' FROM the pancreas, how could it possibly be ahead of it. I pictured the SCN ONLY getting information from the 'inside' all the organs like the liver, pancreas etc. What I was forgetting was the 'outside' so if the SCN is receiving all the outside information properly and efficiently it then relays that information to the pancreas etc it is then 'ahead'. I pictured it getting ONLY information from the 'inside'....................another limitation in thinking that I have just realized. This is one of the things I love about this place, I feel I am literally clearing up my 'thinking' and even 'feeling' too........................

I searched gut organ clock and found this
[http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/37269/title/Out-of-Sync/](http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/37269/title/Out-of-Sync/)
This led to the thought that if the gut is where we take in DHA, a heavily seafood diet should help the gut clock run faster than the liver and pancreas clocks....? And maybe a bit of inside-inside signalling to the SCN?

When my FIL was still living at home he would get up, wash and dress and then have breakfast (within 30 minutes). Lunch and evening meal were at roughly the same time, a bit of supper before bed ...... now in the nursing home, as his SCN has become more and more uncoupled he has become increasingly fixated on meal times. Not the food itself, the timing of the food. I said to DH it was like he was trying to use mealtimes to tell time, to make up for the uncoupling of the SCN. If meals are at exactly the same time each day, (he expects it to the exact minute, no chance in a nursing home with 50 ) there are 3 times in the day his gut clock could potentially be right ...That is on a good day, on a bad one (the more out the SCN is?) the more he seems to look for food at other times too (helping himself to anything else he can find ) What might seem to be completely irrational behaviour may have a rational survival mechanism behind it?

# Thread: Great article on importance of touch
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-article-on-importance-of-touch.13045/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2015-02-24T15:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-article-on-importance-of-touch.13045/post-156475>

[http://www.huffingtonpost.com/kindn...on\_b\_6530658.html?ncid=tweetlnkushpmg00000067](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/kindness-blog/touch-as-nutrition_b_6530658.html?ncid=tweetlnkushpmg00000067)

We mistakenly think that touch occurs on the periphery of our self, a skin thing. But truthfully each surface stimulus travels far into the most hidden interior landscapes of our self, traversing long nerve cells right through the buried spinal core to enter and gather in the deep folds of our brain. It's not by accident that our skin and brain each are generated from a single **ectodermic substance**, cascading outwards and inwards as we grow in the womb, because right at the very root and origin of us, we are built to connect the inner and outer worlds.

### nonchalant — 2015-02-24T17:53:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-article-on-importance-of-touch.13045/post-156485>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=156475)
>
> [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/kindn...on\_b\_6530658.html?ncid=tweetlnkushpmg00000067](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/kindness-blog/touch-as-nutrition_b_6530658.html?ncid=tweetlnkushpmg00000067)
>
> We mistakenly think that touch occurs on the periphery of our self, a skin thing. **But truthfully each surface stimulus travels far into the most hidden interior landscapes of our self,** traversing long nerve cells right through the buried spinal core to enter and gather in the deep folds of our brain.

I can personally attest to this.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-24T21:44:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-article-on-importance-of-touch.13045/post-156494>

Hope, that is a very interesting article, I was kind of speculating too how food could become a substitute for touch. Like the way Dr K talks about food as something that 'un-condenses' the body, things 'swell' as proteins are un-condensed and then this swelling causes pizo-electric currents to flow (Hope I am right about this!) result is we are getting a kind of 'internal massage' but if this was supplied by the environment by actual touch the need for 'internal touching' might be less. Could it be that the 'touching starvation' we are trying to make up with 'internal touching' through food. This might be an interesting idea in terms of food addiction or whatever. I count myself as someone who was/is 'addicted' to food and is in part occasioned I now think by 'touching starvation'. I think also that is why when Dr K says food is not primordial it is putting us into the right track if we believe it is we keep doing the same thing looking for a different result and not finding.................

Anyway that aside I really liked that article. Thanks Hope.

### Hope — 2015-02-25T00:01:09+0200

I think also that is why when Dr K says food is not primordial it is putting us into the right track if we believe it is we keep doing the same thing looking for a different result and not finding.................

That's great, PatGrif!! I have always loved physical touch - even holding hands with my husband is still special to me

I'm not sure there is a substitute for touch........even food or vice versa! Though I will say, my desire for food has gone down in recent years, and my desire for touch has gone up. Wonder if there is a connection?

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-25T01:02:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/great-article-on-importance-of-touch.13045/post-156516>

Yes Hope I would agree there is no 'real' substitute but substitutes are by definition you might say not that real. Sometimes I think of 'healing' as getting under our substitutes to what is real at least as best we can. Anyway enough rambling from me I love this song "Substitute" by The Who

# Thread: Jaime's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 73
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-02-25T00:03:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156505>

following @[sebbie](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12462/) 's lead i am startinag journal for jaime, my wife as she has been up and down with a number of issues for quite some time.

around 8 or 9 years ago after we had been doing a long distance (160km...not that long) Jaime was on her way to work on her bike, when a man aggressively bumped her in a crowd. being the staunch no-fucks-given woman that she is she gave him a mouthful for the incident, and when the traffic lights changed to allow pedestrians to cross, he turned back and pushed her off her bike onto the road, and she was almost run over by a truck. the truck driver leaped out of the truck and chased the man, but no other pedestrians stopped to see if she was ok, rather they were whispering to each other "did you see what just happened?"

that morning jaime called me in tears and told me she was moving to Bendigo and was done with Melbourne, and in two weeks she was up here where i was working

Bendigo has had unemployment issues for some time as a lot of industry shut down, and even though jaime was etxremely talented in hospitatility managemnt, having managed some of melborunes most prestigious venues, it was hard to get a job here....the scene was different...

Jaime hit a depressive stage where she wouldnt leave the house, put on some weight, and started to get intense dermatitis on her feet

soon after she made some freiends although nobody was anything liek our friends in melbourne
i continued just doing my job feeling awful for her

cutting the story a bit short she ended up managing a very busy cafe but benig the kind of worker she is she was doing too much for everyone else and getting sick in the meantime iwth stress and exhaustion...she also hated the town as it seems very backwards and isolated

after a number of job changes and increasing disdain for living this way we decided to move to the coast (jaime grew up on the beach in NZ, and central vic is the furthest thing one could imagine from coastal living)

late last year i did some intolerance testing for jaime as bloating was s till a major issue, and candida was detected alongside many strange intolerances (rosemary etc) suggesting a poor redox
always great when on holidays or not at work, always bloated after work

recently after a stressful week lookking after a sister cafe to the one she manages now there was an outbreak of rash on her L hand ring finger, looked quite blistery and raw, as if a topical yeast infection (onset after helping wash dishes)
candida is still up and down
tested the coffee machine she works in front of - 7mG field
we are now assuming t hat her work in nthis field is the major factor in her health as the candida and bloating is worse at the end of a day, and appears fine when not at work. she is extremely lethargic at the end of the day and waking up tired. She has been Epi-Paleo for 3-4 years with occasioanl lapses into indulgences (bread - very bad, chocolate addiction tho i assume this is a brain gut issue with regard to the candida - poor redox = poor decisions)

ASI and 24hr hormone test to come, as well as all the usual labs...

thx for reading

### fitness@home — 2015-02-25T00:14:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156507>

Great picture Josh!

What a wonderful thing to do for your wife

### cantweight — 2015-02-25T00:47:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156512>

Wow...she is beautiful....you are lucky bearded Josh. I hope the labs give you what you need....what is driving her inflammation....

I took a sort of leave no stone unturned approach with labs and have been whittling away at all the shitty shit in my bod for 6 years now. I can't say it is the right path but it has served me. She sounds so inflamed that all kinds of random crap is popping in response to any perceived negative stimuli. Ive been there. It sucks.

Any books by Natalia Rose...Especially Detox for Women and Raw Food Detox Diet were incredibly helpful to me. Dont be too offput by the sound of them. She basically recommends clean veggies, low sugar fruit, seafood, occasional raw cheese....all good ideas. She also hits gentle IF type eating. She is gentle and 100% geared toward women and helped me when I was an auto-immune nightmare. My moods changed rapidly for the better, and slowly it felt like fires were being put out in my body.
[https://www.detoxtheworld.com/shop/books/natalias-books1/](https://www.detoxtheworld.com/shop/books/natalias-books1/)

I had insane candida, it was a bitch to get rid of and it was the last thing that went. I think food has so little to do with it right now its not even funny. There is not a candida starvation diet I didnt try....they didnt work.

Do we ever talk about lymphatic crap and sweating on this forum? They were both a huge help to me and I have left them by wayside....bringing them back, along with the damn juicing that always seems to get chuckle from folks....this shit makes me feel good.

Does Jamie have any interest in CT? Even just spot or face dunks?

This shit was like turning a screw for me....little by little, when I pushed too hard it backfired. Reading good books like a New Earth....finding some peace and happiness in my world was always directly impacting how I felt. Simplifying every single thing I could....cutting the lifestyle fat that didnt serve me.

Hopefully this doesn't sound silly. Its just all the smaller gentler things are what made the biggest differences over the years.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-02-25T01:50:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156522>

she CTs in the ocean with me most days, it is now at 17C which isnt really cold at all...in winter she was quite good when it was 10-12C
simplifying is definitely a good thing
there are also childhood problems that affect her deepy (her mother was 16 when she had her, and we both think her mother resents her for "taking away her teens and 20s" - although we all know that is hardly the fault of an unborn child, it reflects a lot in interactions with her (often negative) mother.

example, when telling her mother we are going on the cruise:
"so do you go extended times without seeing land? ohh i couldnt do that"

rather than

"THATS SO EXCITING YOURE GOING TO AVE THE BEST TIME!!"

is one example of her mothers negative approach to every conversation

### cantweight — 2015-02-25T03:51:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156536>

Definitely get the book A New Earth by Eckhart Tolle in her hands....this book in its beautifully complex simplicity let me put down childhood baggage I had carried for far too long. I too have a very negative mother...its a tough one.

Dr Ohiras Professional Strength in the blue box was far superior to the regular in my opinion....I took 4-5 daily while healing

Have you mixed up mags? Malate is a household fave and we have lots of similar snp's around here

[https://www.xymogen.com/products/product-detail.aspx?pid=25](https://www.xymogen.com/products/product-detail.aspx?pid=25) We used this, not sure if it is similar to yours....

The field is important but she can get well there....I got well in a nasty place with no access to sand and sea.

We did juice greens....I always cut it with lemons and apple to make it taste yummy for the kids. My go to was kale, romaine, lemon, apple. But we changed it up all the time....cucumber, fennel, celery, dandelion greens....what ever was on sale or in season or came in CSA. It was energizing and mind clearing....I regret giving it up and labeling it a poor choice. It was not a poor choice for me or my family....my kids have begged for the stuff. As we move into spring I'm bringing it back. Going to try briefly blending in vitamix (after juicing) with mct and aloe.

My kids were very young when they were dealing with nasty gut issues. We had to use smoothies as medicinal tools. We did green smoothies with fruit. I added this... [https://www.xymogen.com/products/product-detail.aspx?pid=155](https://www.xymogen.com/products/product-detail.aspx?pid=155)
and aloe, swedish bitters, L-glutamine....all kinds of random crap. No more belly aches!

It really is a N=1 thing. Perhaps these things are only successful in sick bodies....optimal bodies may run optimally but so many of us need these "baby steps" to get us to the place where our engines run on fat and seafood...even if they are supposed to already. The science is there, it should work....but when it doesnt we have to try these back door methods and some have found great success in doing so.

Well, Josh, I know something about being a child to teen parents. Sounds all very familiar. I can relate to COMT Snps and anxiety and unhappiness and many other shit feelings as long as i can remember. I may be a good match for your lovely wife. I bet her secretory IgA would be low. Does she ever get really sick lately? I mean fever and being down in bed and all this. This has been as well part of my story, no acute diseases, just accumulating more rubbish.

I would do everything possible to bring to under control her emotions, selfesteem etc. I am convinced this is a gate to hell and she sounds like more than a probable candidate. Men can never really catch a women's feelings and this layer of their personalities. My husband has been trying to help and "fix my head" ever since we are together. He really did a way too much, but to no relief at all. Whatever worked to help to get slowly out of the hell of my previous emotional life had to come from myself. Pretty sad and frustarting for him I must say. Maybe it is supposed to be this way, than no pther person can do this job for you...because it includes to regain control over yourselfs. COMT met/met "mutants" are lifelong worriers. We tend to take the weight of the world over our shoulders. I found some initial relieve in matcha tea, which seems to speed up my slow COMT and slowly work my way through mental exercises, visualisation etc. Just taking a break to concentrate slowly on each centimeter of my body worked magics, because at that moment i did nothing more than taking a pleasure in energy originated in my own body. Sounds freak. I still struggle to share myself and relax for the others, my next step I guess. And I lost interest in wine, my favourite method to relax. i do not know whether you make anything from this mental vomit coming from keypad right now. basically i just wanted to say that the head of a woman could be as healing as hands of a saint and as poisonous as a black mamba ;-)

really amazing response, and absolutely echoes my feelings on what is happening
jaime had some problems where she gave every person she met 120% of her, and did everything in her power to lift them up and give them everything, and continually was shit on by their horrible behaviour towards her. it is the kind of stuff that makes somebody question whether they themselves might be the problem, which she asks me all the time. i have to continually reassure her how amazing she is, and how much she lights my world on fire, and sometimes she does not believe it  i agree it must be fixed within at the root cause of childhood, she agrees too but does not know how to go about it or confront her mother

thanks for the great reply

I seem to be working it out by becoming a real egoist at first. This helped me to understand what is it like to do the thongs I really want and to stop caring about the thoughts and opinions of the others. I helped to many but stayed lifelong loner. I reached now the point when I finally confronted my mother, rather unexpectedly and with cruelty. i really became a cruel selfish egoist. Rather happy one, I must say. No accusasions at all and I think she understood. I am pretty sure Jaime's mother knows the truth pretty well, but it takes guts as well for her to embrace it and all this...it is comfortable to ignore it.

Sadly, it did not cure me ;-( so far. I am however starting to experience a true peace of mind every now and than. I sense anytime soon I may be able to leave my egoistic phase behind and become who I was meant to be ;-)

Such great insights Kovita…..and I don't think taking care of your self, or standing up for yourself is egoism….we are taught as women to take care of others first, but the nurturing that is part of our biology should be yoked with being incredibly strong and sure within ourselves ….humans seem to have corrupted that in various cultures (not only ours) so that women's health being sacrificed for others is the norm…not good. When we permit ourselves to do what it takes to become healthy in all ways…well then watch out!…you are a great example.

Josh, I hope Jaime finds this "inside out" path….it is there for everyone and is different for everyone. In the work with horses that I do, we talk about becoming familiar and at ease with the periods of time that seem like big chasms or voids, because out of that place, where everything you know and believe is tossed away…out of that place comes the chance to meet your self…the one that's been there all along patiently waiting. What feeds that self? What gives it energy and passion? That's the ultimate n=1. Sounds like she has a wonderful journey ahead of her

Seahorse, your words abput horses are so interersting. I always madly feared horses and the horses were very unquiet while i was around them. Last early summer I brought my girl to the farm because she loves horses and the horses love her so I take here sometimes for a pony rides. It came to my mind that time, that I desire to sit on one of them and do it. Well, everyone (and me) was shocked since i was able to go galloping on the first lesson, it felt like the horse was part of my body and i was proclaimed supernatural horse riding talent ;-) now, when you wrote the things about horses, maybe it is why my relation with them changed so much out of nothing. it was just about the time when I for the first time manipulated provenly something inside my body using my own mind. they say the animals still can smell your soul.

### Shijin13 — 2015-02-25T16:49:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-156582>

@[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/)

my thoughts echo Christi's. sometimes we're each different. I've done juicing before and felt great doing it. Recently I've been craving veggies in epic proportions - enough that I can't eat what I want across the day. So Every couple of days I toss some steamed kale, cucumber, carrots, apples, lemons (maybe even a pineapple - gasp!) and ginger into my vitamix with water or coconut milk and blend away. Also suddenly after almost 2yrs of not being able to stomach eggs - I want them again. Sunday I just had my "juice" and then sunny side up eggs fried in kerry gold on a Big Ass Salad.

I've also backed off the high fat - and just going for moderate fat, moderate protein, and low carb - all from veggies and fruit. I haven't been able to do the BAB for almost 2 yrs - but I know I still have leptin issues. I'm following my season cravings.

the interesting thing is since engaging this - over the past week and 1/2 I haven't wanted Chocolate. Yesterday was the first day I had chocolate in almost 2 months (But I also spent two consecutive days in EMF WiFi hell atwork)

I really thing that some of us are so mitochondrially messed up that sometimes you've got to do what you can to support SuperOxide generation....

I like smoothies rather than juicing myself - juicing is a blood sugar binge-fest IMHO - smoothies give fiber to the gut flora - plus fiber removes toxins -

Personally, I dole out 500mg of magnesium malate to the kids because the malic acid gets rid of aluminum -

I also use Xylitol as a sweetener because it tastes just like sugar and it kills strep - and it doesn't spike insulin -

I just read a cool book on google books about chromium and depression - really it seems quite effective -

The usual supplements to get rid of extra estrogen are DIMM I3C and chase it with a calcium d-glucarate - brocolli sprouts have tons of I3C -

Calcium d-glucarate will also help glucoronidation plus there are bugs which eat the glucose molecule your phase II liver enzymes just slapped onto a toxin to remove it - and glucoronidation is also huge in hormone removal/disposal - so if you have clostridia, it loves to screw up glucoronidation - even if you don't have Gilbert's...

My happy lunch is a smoothie with lots of nuts, coconut, seeds, cacao, greens - I am not hungry for hours - coconut products are amazing for candida - and for any virus, bacteria, or fungus -

I think cortisol also spikes blood sugar -

And getting rid of people in your life who are downers is actually quite freeing - truly, who needs it -

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-03-16T04:13:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-158616>

Arsenic is perhaps the best known of the metal toxins, having gained notoriety from its extensive use by Renaissance nobility as an antisyphilitic agent and, paradoxically, as an antidote against acute arsenic poisoning. Even today, arsenic is still 1 of the more common toxicants found in insecticides, and leaching from bedrock to contaminate groundwater.

The toxicity of arsenic is due to 3 different mechanisms, 2 of them related to energy transfer. Arsenic covalently and avidly binds to dihydrolipoic acid, a necessary cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase. Absence of the cofactor inhibits the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl coenzyme A, the first step in gluconeogenesis. This results in loss of energy supply to anaerobic cells, the predominant mechanism of action of arsenic on neural cells that rely on anaerobic respiration for energy. Neuron cell destruction that occurs after long-term energy loss results in bilateral peripheral neuropathy.

Arsenic also competes with phosphate for binding to adenosine triphosphate during its synthesis by mitochondria via oxidative phosphorylation, causing formation of the lower energy adenosine diphosphate monoarsine. This results in loss of energy supply to aerobic cells. Cardiac cells are particularly sensitive to this form of energy loss; fatigue due to poor cardiac output is a common symptom of arsenic exposure.

Arsenic furthermore binds avidly with any hydrated sulfhydryl group on protein, distorting the 3-dimensional configuration of that protein, causing it to lose activity. Interaction of arsenic with epithelial cell protein at the sites of highest physiologic concentration, the small intestine and proximal tubule of the kidney, results in cellular degeneration. Epithelial cell erosion in the gastrointestinal tract and proximal tubule are characteristic of arsenic toxicity. Arsenic is also a known carcinogen, but the mechanism of this effect is not definitively known.

A wide range of signs and symptoms may be seen in acute arsenic poisoning including headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hypotension, fever, hemolysis, seizures, and mental status changes. Symptoms of chronic poisoning, also called arseniasis, are mostly insidious and nonspecific. The gastrointestinal tract, skin, and central nervous system are usually involved. Nausea, epigastric pain, colic abdominal pain, diarrhea, and paresthesias of the hands and feet can occur.

Arsenic exists in a number of different forms; organic forms are nontoxic, inorganic forms are toxic. See ASFR / Arsenic Fractionation, 24 Hour, Urine for details about arsenic forms.

Because arsenic is excreted predominantly by glomerular filtration, analysis for arsenic in urine is the best screening test to detect arsenic exposure.

seafood in diet regularly may be confounding results

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-16T05:10:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-158626>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=158616)
>
> Arsenic is perhaps the best known of the metal toxins, having gained notoriety from its extensive use by Renaissance nobility as an antisyphilitic agent and, paradoxically, as an antidote against acute arsenic poisoning. Even today, arsenic is still 1 of the more common toxicants found in insecticides, and leaching from bedrock to contaminate groundwater.
>
>
>
> The toxicity of arsenic is due to 3 different mechanisms, 2 of them related to energy transfer. Arsenic covalently and avidly binds to dihydrolipoic acid, a necessary cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase. Absence of the cofactor inhibits the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl coenzyme A, the first step in gluconeogenesis. This results in loss of energy supply to anaerobic cells, the predominant mechanism of action of arsenic on neural cells that rely on anaerobic respiration for energy. Neuron cell destruction that occurs after long-term energy loss results in bilateral peripheral neuropathy.
>
>
>
> Arsenic also competes with phosphate for binding to adenosine triphosphate during its synthesis by mitochondria via oxidative phosphorylation, causing formation of the lower energy adenosine diphosphate monoarsine. This results in loss of energy supply to aerobic cells. Cardiac cells are particularly sensitive to this form of energy loss; fatigue due to poor cardiac output is a common symptom of arsenic exposure.
>
>
>
> Arsenic furthermore binds avidly with any hydrated sulfhydryl group on protein, distorting the 3-dimensional configuration of that protein, causing it to lose activity. Interaction of arsenic with epithelial cell protein at the sites of highest physiologic concentration, the small intestine and proximal tubule of the kidney, results in cellular degeneration. Epithelial cell erosion in the gastrointestinal tract and proximal tubule are characteristic of arsenic toxicity. Arsenic is also a known carcinogen, but the mechanism of this effect is not definitively known.
>
>
>
> A wide range of signs and symptoms may be seen in acute arsenic poisoning including headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hypotension, fever, hemolysis, seizures, and mental status changes. Symptoms of chronic poisoning, also called arseniasis, are mostly insidious and nonspecific. The gastrointestinal tract, skin, and central nervous system are usually involved. Nausea, epigastric pain, colic abdominal pain, diarrhea, and paresthesias of the hands and feet can occur.
>
>
>
> Arsenic exists in a number of different forms; organic forms are nontoxic, inorganic forms are toxic. See ASFR / Arsenic Fractionation, 24 Hour, Urine for details about arsenic forms.
>
>
>
> Because arsenic is excreted predominantly by glomerular filtration, analysis for arsenic in urine is the best screening test to detect arsenic exposure.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> seafood in diet regularly may be confounding results

Josh, this is really a shot in the dark but I just read this incredibly great (to me) book about the polio virus among other things. ("Fear of the Invisible" by Janine Roberts) The author speculated that these 'polio outbreaks' far from being caused by some contagious virus were possibly the result of environmental toxins and poisoning. And she mentions pesticides etc. But your phrase "Neuron cell destruction that occurs after long-term energy loss results in bilateral peripheral neuropathy." seemed to be close to the so called polio type symptoms. This is as I said just a shot in the dark but I think it is well worth really re-considering most of not all of the 'virus hypothesis' of disease. I am now completely convinced HIV is the wrong target like someone who has spent 30+ years in jail and now should be let out. But can't because it would make the authorities look too bad.

### Josh — 2015-03-17T01:46:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-158712>

> **Clinical Information**
> Thallium is a by-product of lead smelting. The clinical interest in thallium derives primarily from its use as a rodenticide since this is the most frequent route of human exposure.
>
> Thallium is rapidly absorbed via ingestion, inhalation, skin contact, and through the mucous membranes of the mouth, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs. It is considered to be as toxic as lead and mercury, with similar sites of action.
>
> The mechanism of action of thallium is:
>
> \-Competition with potassium at cell receptors to affect ion pumps
>
> \-Inhibition of DNA synthesis
>
> \-Binds to sulfhydryl groups on proteins in neural axons
>
> \-Concentrates in renal tubular cells and reacts with protein to cause necrosis
>
> Patients exposed to high doses of thallium (>1 g) present with alopecia (hair loss), peripheral neuropathy and seizures, and renal failure.

[http://neurology.testcatalog.org/show/TLB](http://neurology.testcatalog.org/show/TLB)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thallium\_poisoning](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thallium_poisoning)

[http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/PHS/PHS.asp?id=307&tid=49](http://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/PHS/PHS.asp?id=307&tid=49)

> **1.5 Is there a medical test to determine whether I have been exposed to thallium?**
> Reliable and accurate ways to measure thallium in the body are available. The presence of thallium in the urine and hair can indicate exposure to thallium. Tests of your urine can detect thallium up to 2 months. The normal amount of thallium in human urine amounts to less than 1 ppm and 5ï¿½10 ppb in human hair. Although thallium can be measured in blood, this tissue is not a good indicator of exposure since thallium stays there too short a time. We do not know yet whether thallium levels measured in the body can be used to predict possible health effects.

2 possible routes seem obvious:

1.  persistent environmental use of Thallium as a rodentacide
2.  smelting in or around Bendigo now or in the past

The info on seafood is vague at first pass. This may be a Red Herring?

### Josh — 2015-03-17T02:54:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jaimes-journal.13049/post-158720>

> Brockhaus et al. (1981) conducted an epidemiologic study of a group of 1,200 people living near the cement plant in Lengerich. Urinary thallium data were also collected from two reference populations without increased thallium intake—one group consisting of 31 persons living in a small (rural) city in northwest Germany and a second group consisting of 10 persons living in an urban area in Dusseldorf, Germany. The study investigators did not perform specific tests for toxicity but surveyed for the presence of certain symptoms by using questionnaires. Thallium exposure was assessed by measurements in urine and hair. The thallium body burden of the study population was increased over the reference populations, as indicated by a mean urinary thallium level of 5.2 ± 8.3 μg/L (range: <0.1–76.5 μg/L) in the study population compared to the reference population means of 0.4 ± 0.2 μg/L (rural) and 0.3 ± 0.2 μg/L (urban) (range: 0.1–1.2 μg/L). The predominant contributing factor to the thallium burden was consumption of homegrown fruits and vegetables. When the consumption of homegrown foods was restricted, thallium exposure was reduced, as indicated by decreased thallium in the urine. No correlation between dermal or GI symptoms and thallium level was observed. There was a negative correlation between urinary thallium and hair loss (13.6% with urine levels <2 μg/L, 6.6% with urine levels 2–20 μg/L, and 5.9% with urine levels >20 μg/L; 10.7% with hair levels <10 ng/g, 9.6% with hair levels 10–50 ng/g, and 2.3% with hair levels >50 ng/g). These data appear to conflict with other reports that indicate hair loss increases with increasing thallium exposure. A positive association was observed among thallium levels in urine or hair and the following self-reported symptoms: sleep disorders, tiredness, weakness, nervousness, headache, and psychological alterations as well as neurological and muscular symptoms (Brockhaus et al., 1981).

[http://www.epa.gov/iris/toxreviews/1012tr.pdf](http://www.epa.gov/iris/toxreviews/1012tr.pdf)

all out war on estrogens should be in order.

Limit production of estrogens. --->[exemestane](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exemestane) (Aromasin)

Maximize processing them out ---->
10 Ways To Lower Estrogen Toxic Load

[http://www.poliquingroup.com/articl...01/10\_ways\_to\_lower\_estrogen\_toxic\_load\_.aspx](http://www.poliquingroup.com/articlesmultimedia/articles/article/801/10_ways_to_lower_estrogen_toxic_load_.aspx)

..........

Irreversible steroidal inhibitors, such as [exemestane](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exemestane) (Aromasin), forms a permanent and deactivating bond with the aromatase enzyme.

Non-steroidal inhibitors, such as [anastrozole](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anastrozole) (Arimidex) and [letrozole](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letrozole) (Femara), inhibit the synthesis of estrogen via reversible competition for the aromatase enzyme.

Hey folks,

I've [launched a Kickstarter project](https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/1410466887/the-worlds-only-proven-calorie-burning-weight-loss) to fund 2 experiments on cold thermogenesis...

1\. comparing the Cool Fat Burner to other cooling vests (raw calorie burn numbers)

2\. university group study on the Cool Fat Burner, and it's effects on calorie burning, blood glucose levels, body composition, and most importantly, the hormones adiponectin and irisin.

And if you've been on the fence in regards to getting a Cool Fat Burner, the pledge rewards are offering it lower than off the main CFB site.

[PLEASE CHECK IT OUT!](https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/1410466887/the-worlds-only-proven-calorie-burning-weight-loss)

thanks a million,
Eric

# Thread: !!!oysters***oysters***oysters!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-oysters.13051/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 9

### ON MY WAY — 2015-02-25T05:04:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-oysters.13051/post-156545>

Hi Group!

**WOW**, Dr. Jack, you rock!! I'm so blown away. I have been reading you for a year now but only this past few weeks have I been _getting_ some of it (despite that it all always RINGS true)...

My main note here to all is that I've been doing the Leptin Reset (for the most part, haven't gotten the RO water unit yet though I do have a water restructuring unit AND I eat an apple in the afternoon sometimes)....

...and I live an hour and a half from the famous HOG ISLAND OYSTER farm, so weekly, I drive up and buy a bag of 50!!! I shuck for breaky, I shuck for lunch, and sometimes dinner too! I'd have more but honestly my wrists can't take any more shucking!!! (And it's a little hard on the pocketbook) I am blown away by how extremely fantastic I feel as a result. Symptoms persist but that's okay. It takes time, I know.

I have a whole big sad sally health story like the rest... some scary things, some debilitating things, but I am not going to share all of that now. I'm so glad to be here with you on doing something _just feels so right_ on for the body/mind/spirit.

And Jack, you're so articulate and AWARE of all of the corners! I like to garden, and in doing a planting, one must know what is blooming at what time, at what height, and when it fades out in order to choose what other plants go where so that you can have an intentional 3D color composition that is always changing. I feel like that is kind of what you're doing.... planting and pruning with us until the garden is just a symphony, all of the brilliance is there to behold and nature just takes over!

Chow Chow, amigos!

In Bacon We Trust,

Freya

### Galena — 2015-02-25T19:36:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/figuring-out-thyroid-labs.13060/post-156593>

Hi Everyone:
I am new to this forum, have been following Dr. Kruse's teachings for some time. Was eating Paleo, low-carb before starting to read Dr.Kruse, and now transitioning to Epi-Paleo ketogenic over last few months.
Just got my Magnetico pad set up yesterday (10 gauss), and excited to see the effects on my sleep.
I work in an office setting where non-native EMF is high (computers, cell phones, Wi-Fi), at home we have done what we can for now to minimize exposure, until someday hopefully can move to better location.

Brief history:
Suffered from recurring depression and binge-eating and food sensitivities most of my adult life. Managed to keep normal weight through "diet and exercise". Currently I weigh 135 lbs and height of 5' 2 inches. Past 10 years, binge eating has become more manageable by using low carb diet and basically avoiding trigger foods (abstinence). I do not take any antidepressant medication.

For last 5 years, I noticed my sleep was getting progressively worse, until I started wearing blue-blocking glasses in the evening, CT in the evening, and lying on a sleep-induction mat (from Dave Asprey) before bed. These things have greatly improved my quality of sleep.

First went to anti-aging doctor in 2011 mainly to check on my hormones ( i am currently peri-menopausal), and was surprised to find out that my Free T3 is rather low. Surprised, because I don't really feel strong symptoms of hypothyroid, only that my feet are very sensitive to the cold, and sometimes feel some fatigue, but not extreme. The thyroid panel has been re-checked a few times since 2011, and always stays about the same.

Have had many other tests (including ASI, GI-effects, heavy metals (urine), 23-and-me SNPs), the main findings that stand out are:
\*all day long my cortisol is at the low end of the normal range on ASI
\*I have low diversity of gut microbes, high fecal secretory IgA (indicating some food sensitivity that is not yet identified)
\*high levels of Lead and Mercury
\*homozygous for MTHFR A1298C (23andme)
\*low HS-CRP (0.4 mg/L)
\*DHEA-S =4.5 umol/L (convert to U.S. = 166ug/dL; Dr. Kruse advises >250 is optimal)
\*Bun:Creatine ratio = don't know yet, waiting for next set of labs to do the measurements.

Last thyroid panel Sept.2014:
\*TSH = 1.67 mIU/L (range 0.2 - 6.0)
\*Free T3 = 2.6 pmol/L (range 3.5 - 6.5) --> in U.S. units 2.6 pmol/L translates to 169 pg/dL
\*Free T4 = 14.0 pmol/L (range 10 - 25)
\*Anti-TPO = 7.0 kIU/L (range 0 - 34) ---> considered negative for Hashimoto's

I started doing CT, worked my way up to 25 min per day in water about 48-50Degrees (cold straight from the tap in tub, waist -deep). Also, it is winter here in Calgary, so I walk outside and get cold often.
Have been doing CT almost daily now for 4 months. Just got my thyroid panel re-tested, and waiting for results to see if CT alone has any effect on thyroid. I certainly notice that I sleep more soundly with doing CT.

My anti-aging doctor is recommending the next step may need to be heavy metal chelation, but I am sceptical about this. For now I am working on gut issues, and using methods to increase Glutathione to support detoxification of heavy metals. Her interpretation of the Low Free T3 is that there is a problem with conversion of T4-> T3, and heavy metals may play a role. I need help trying to understand what limits this conversion.

I appreciate any feedback from other members !

### Josh — 2015-02-25T20:58:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/figuring-out-thyroid-labs.13060/post-156597>

Consider that T4 conversion to T3 requires good sulfur handling to make the enzymes. This may be a CBS pathway issue. If you have CBS methylation issues based on SNP's and or poor redox, conversion and detxification of heavy metals will both be unreliable. Conversely, if these are resolved, the metals may be detoxified by your body without intervention. The MTHFR SNP will predispose you to acumulate metals when it expresses. Detoxifying you under these circumstances may actually do more harm than good. Also where is the reverse T3? You may be converting, but deactivating it to minimize inflammation?

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-02-26T00:23:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/figuring-out-thyroid-labs.13060/post-156622>

I've been wondering what Jack Kruse thinks about these things, but, myxedema at the Achilles heel tendon and/or bicep have proven to be equally, or many times more accurate than labs for diagnosing hypothyroidism; then there is the Barnes Temperature test. He treated thousands of patients with thyroid hormones and a high saturated fat, low carb diet, and autopsied many humans and animals in Europe after they died to analyze what thyroid hormone did systemically. He came to the conclusion that the best test for thyroid status, outside of myxedema, was body temperature first thing upon waking in the morning, taken under your arm. He even ran mini-trials on his patients utilizing multiple batches of the first blood tests, and found them all to be unreliable.

[http://www.turner-white.com/memberfile.php?PubCode=hp\_jan04\_thyroid.pdf](http://www.turner-white.com/memberfile.php?PubCode=hp_jan04_thyroid.pdf) **Woltman's Sign of Hypothyroidism - Mark A. Marinella, MD, FACP, CNSP**

[http://www.cmaj.ca/content/179/4/387](http://www.cmaj.ca/content/179/4/387) **A classic sign of hypothyroidism: a video demonstration**
[http://press.endocrine.org/doi/abs/...id=ori:rid:crossref.org&rfr\_dat=cr\_pub=pubmed](http://press.endocrine.org/doi/abs/10.1210/jcem.82.3.3810?url_ver=Z39.88-2003&rfr_id=ori:rid:crossref.org&rfr_dat=cr_pub%3dpubmed) **Estimation of Tissue Hypothyroidism by a New Clinical Score: Evaluation of Patients with Various Grades of Hypothyroidism and Controls**
This is just a snippet of the literature. Other studies found absolutely no association between T4 and TSH with thyroid hormone reactions, levels, etc. but strong associations with myxedema at the Achilles heel in+total cholesterol. This makes even more sense in regards to what Dr. Kruse has stated about T3 combining with LDLs and Vit.A to form pregnenolone in the mitochondria.

### ktrosper — 2015-02-25T20:51:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-an-alternate-hypothesis.13061/post-156596>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3492120/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3492120/)
Neovascularization of coronary tunica intima
Dr. Kruse,
I've read you and listened to you enough to understand that you're not afraid to punch the conventional wisdom right in the mouth when it's deserved. I wanted to bring this gem of a paper to your attention. I think you'll appreciate the implications, yet I doubt any of it will surprise you..

I think of how you on your blog/podcasts/"Epi-Paleo Rx" book and Taubes', in his book "Good Calories, Bad Calories", document the initial inertia built up by the (faulty and fraudulent) lipid hypothesis... How quickly it was backed by the entire weight of the established political, medical, nutritional and scientific communities.. That inertia, as you know, continues to this day and at times it seems unrelenting.. It's pulling most research, science and medicine down a fruitless, harmful path.. not to mention the damage it's causing the growing population of metabolically deranged people in this world..

I have a Type1 Diabetic boy and I'm all too familiar with the harmful dietary/lifestyle advice given to that community as standard of care by even the best doctors in the world. It breaks my heart to think of the immeasurable, inevitable pain and suffering that advice, built on the faulty lipid hypothesis premise, has caused and will continue to cause the diabetic community. It's inexcusable. (Your work has helped me normalize his hba1c, by the way!! I can't tell you what that means to me.. but that's a story for another day.)

Back to the paper. I've been conversing with this scientist, Mr. Subbotin, over the past few weeks. Sounds like the gatekeepers who control funding and publishing have made it hard on him, tried to marginalize him as a "plumber or handyman among scientists".. That won't surprise you either and probably rings close to home.. That's the sad state of science and medicine we find ourselves in today. Might they be feeling threatened by a clear, rational-thinking scientist who dare try to correct a flawed premise upon which the most profitable pharma-interventions in the history of man and thousands of life-long careers have been based?

When asked why he persists? "Because it is the meaning of my life.."

I immediately thought of the engraved slab of granite that dominates the entry to my boys school. It reads in Latin, "Quid enim iustificabit vitam tuam?"

What will justify your life?

I like that question..

Anyway, I've got more info from Mr. Subbotin to share if you're interested.

Thanks for all you do.
KT

### Jack Kruse — 2015-08-05T04:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/atherosclerosis-an-alternate-hypothesis.13061/post-173974>

To understand where I am going you need to understand optics. And not simple optics.

What does Vitamin K2 and porphyrins have in common? = All K vitamins are sensitive to **UV** radiation. Very few people know this or understand why this is key pieve to HTN, heart disease and PAD as a surface level disease. This also implies that fake light diminishes Vitamin K. K2 is very sensitive to light and is easily destroyed. This is why K2 has to be with other proteins in a tissue media to work well. This has huge implications for animals who spend most of their time in and unnatural or indoor lit world. ( Macorquodale, Binkley, McKee, Thayer, and Doisy, 1939). You'd be wise to remember that vitamin K2 and CoEnQ10 havea very similar atomic structure. CoEnQ10 is also very sensitive to altered light environments.

Vitamin K2 also protects many cell lines from glutathione depletion making it more important than any glutathione supplement.
[http://www.jneurosci.org/content/23/13/5816.full.pdf](http://www.jneurosci.org/content/23/13/5816.full.pdf)

UV spectra:
UV max 242, 248, 260, 269, 325 nm in ether. UV max (ethanol) 243, 248, 262, 270, 330 nm; IR spectra: (liquid) : 6.05m (CO), 6.21, 6.28m (aromatic nucleus tend to love UV light)
Consider where K2 works.......gut, bone and vessels. Becker showed us bone uses the photoelectric effect, we know the gut does too, and we know the vessels do as well with the skin. Vessels absorb at the same frequencies as proteins in blood. 280 mm 420nm 540nm and 580nm and has sharp cutoff occurs at 600 nm.

[http://www.jbc.org/content/131/1/345.full.pdf](http://www.jbc.org/content/131/1/345.full.pdf)

### ktrosper — 2015-02-25T23:18:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.13062/post-156605>

I'm Kelly.
Not new here, but have never posted before today. Just my personality, I guess.. Quiet, yet confident and observant.. Always learning and I'm not afraid to bring my own fundamental beliefs and premises into question when conflict occurs.. It's how truth is discovered.

I'm self-educated for the most part. No fancy PHDs, Certificates or impressive acronyms follow my name, but maybe that's a plus this day and age.. I've got a natural curiosity and a desire to learn and to know that thankfully was never stomped of me, and I have to thank my parents and my childhood educators for allowing and fostering that... Sad, but those are not traits that are allowed flourish these days..

They say that pain focuses the mind, right?
My insatiable appetite for all things "optimal" was discovered about 5 years ago when my then, 9yr old boy was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes.. My road to optimal started with a simple question.. "Huh? What's insulin??!"

Well, I've come a long way since that day. Thanks to the work that Dr. Kruse (and others) have done I've been able to normalize my boys HBA1C and improve the health of my family. As you all know, this road to optimal.. it doesn't merely take you to a place, it takes you on a journey..

Thanks to Dr. K and all of the brilliant contributors here for the education.
I look forward to continuing this journey until the day I die

### ktrosper — 2015-02-26T18:26:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.13062/post-156708>

> Please start a journal and allow us to follow your journey...
> what are you doing to help your family? what do you eat? drink?
> There are lots of folks here struggling with sick children etc.
> nnEMF???

re journal: Will do. I've been thinking of this for a while. It's not in my personality to be out there on FB or other social platforms saying "look at me!!!" but friends and family who have watched our progress, especially with the T1D boy, encourage us to get out there and give testimony to this lifestyle... If I can help others with sick kids by sharing what I've learned and what we do, that's cool. Seems to me that y'all have covered most of the bases, but I can at least get my info out there and put it in context.

That's one of the biggest things I've learned here over the years. **Context matters.**

re eat/drink: I've come to understand that it's not about macros, but if I'd define it in those terms - VLC/MP/HF - heavy on seafood - fruits in season, tons of water/no milk, some cheese - is where we're at, "epi-paleo, trying to pay attention to circadian cycles" I believe...

re nnEMF: This is something I'm still wrapping my mind around.. nnEMF takes a lot of time/effort and energy to understand. To tell ya the truth, I didn't want to believe it. I wanted desperately to believe that Dr. K was wrong, for many reasons.. But as I began to read some of the underlying materials that he's recommended over the years and connecting some of my own dots, I've come to realize he's not. For the most part, to believe - I must understand.. Like I said, this is a journey and I'm still very much engaged in the "understanding" phase and although I have made some basic changes to limit nnEMF I've got a LONG way to go.

> I have a brother that is a physician and his son was diagnosed as a T1D when he was 18 mos (now 15yrold). I've supported him by attending the JDRF walks every year. So hard to watch all the bagels and Popsicles and carbs and just offset it with insulin attitude.

I just don't get that.. The day to day empirical evidence alone should demand that your brother ask the question "is there a better way?" I wasn't too far down the self-educating path before I realized the long-term damage that was being caused to my boy every time I saw a BG number over 140ish.. and I probably don't have to tell ya how scary the lows are.. always wondering in the back of your head "what if? Will he wake up tomorrow?"

The standard message to this community is the "count 'em and chase 'em with insulin".. as you know, it's built on the faulty lipid hypothesis. If hell exists, there's a special place in it for the bastard who cherry-picked that data to make the case that fat was bad - it wasn't an honest mistake.. think of the course that cherry-picking sent us down and think of the millions world-wide who've experience pain, suffering and death due to that course we've taken.. I dunno much about Keys, but I can tell ya that he didn't share our value system - love of truth above all else would have prevented such behavior.

We're playing with fire when we consistently set off massive immune responses within our bodies.. Sooner or later it's going to pick on something it shouldn't..

Acceptance of these theories often times comes with a certain amount of well deserved guilt. It's our jobs to protect our kids from danger and ignorance is not an excuse.. I failed him in that regard. If I could trade places with him or give him my pancreas I would in a heartbeat.. At this point all I can do is learn from it, learn how to best maintain his health moving forward and teach him as much as I can.
