### ktrosper — 2015-03-01T07:02:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.13062/post-156966>

> Truth does piss a lot of people off.

Yep. Truth is sometimes scary because it always comes with the possibility that it'll invalidate a long-held premise or belief.. I think we (humans) are very good at remaining ignorant of truths that will cause us pain. I'm sure it's some kind of protection mechanism.. Because, like I said, truth sometimes comes with pain and guilt.. one inconvenient truth can unravel a tapestry of values, beliefs and practices that took a lifetime to build.. one inconvenient truth can invalidate entire careers, billions of dollars of research aimed at the wrong target.. Marching 20 miles barefoot in the hot sun with no water, only to be told you've gone in the wrong direction?? That's a painful moment of truth and I'd bet large parts of our psyche would resist learning it..

It's why men like Dr. K (Taubes, Rosedale, Lustig, Bernstein, etc. etc. etc.) are attacked relentlessly with an attempt to discredit and marginalize. They are revealing inconvenient truths that, if accepted, will topple multi-billion dollar pharma-interventions, the junk-food industry, our current health (sick) care paradigm and invalidate thousands of careers and god-knows how many mal-invested research dollars... It takes great courage to stand out in front and shout to the entire human race that, "You're ALL wrong! And here's why!"

> I can't tell you how many T1D's I've tried to get on a ketogenic diet and off of wheat/dairy - it's like I have 2 heads

I know. My wife usually asks, "When are you gonna learn to keep your mouth shut??" after I try to share some of what we've learned with other diabetics. It's like I'm questioning their religion or political beliefs.. BTW Penny, is that Anitschkow's rabbit?

### ktrosper — 2015-03-01T07:03:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-and-thank-you.13062/post-156967>

> Sounds like your son is very fortunate to have a dad like you....you are way ahead of 99% of everyone else.

I sure appreciate that. My heart breaks for the diabetic community and the advice they are getting on a daily basis.

Check this out..
I'm gonna try to honestly portray my path from "idiot to Kruse", if I can without boring y'all to tears..

I told you my self-education started the day our boy was diagnosed.. Although this is true, I can't honestly say that I "dove in and didn't come back up for air for 5 years".. The truth is that I was ignorant and in shock. When my wife called to tell me she suspected diabetes, I honestly asked myself in my head "wtf is diabetes?!" I remember the moment. I remember the denial and the excuses I made.. "nah, my boys fine! I just gotta stop giving him so much to drink before bed (he'd been wetting the bed..). Ahh.. He's just inherited his daddy's shitty eye sight! (he'd gone from 20/20 to virtually blind almost overnight..). nah, he's just tired from these LONG sunup to sundown busy days, we gotta cut out some activities! (he'd been lethargic and not wanting to do anything but nap in his free time).

So yeah. I didn't want to believe that something was wrong with my boy. I didn't want to accept that he now had a lifelong issue that he'd have to contend with day in and day out for the rest of his life.. I was in shock.

I hadn't had much experience with ANY medical staff up to that point.. We were "healthy" I thought. Only saw doctors once a year, if that.. So, I trusted them 100%. I mean, this is what they do right? They know everything about this disease, right? It was amazing, the efficiency with which they rolled in the ignorant Trosper family and got us up to speed on this disease, answered all of our newby questions and taught us how to "take care of our boy".. It was welcome. It was reassuring to be surrounded by all of those "experts" who had perfectly logical explanations to every newby question I threw at them. We were in good hands!

And the message of "Ahh, you don't have to change a thing! Just count carbs and chase them with insulin!" was welcome too! Whew, what a relief! We weren't going to have to upend our entire lifestyle! Easy Peasy, right??!! What a selfish, shitty thought. To be glad that you weren't going to have to change your entire menu for a disease...

So anyway, off we went. Trained on how to test his blood. We had our initial insulin ratios worked out.. We were warned about an initial "rocky" period while we were dialing things in, but not to worry.. We'd get it all figured out so that we could avoid "complications" as long as possible.

"Complications"... That's the term they ALL use, ALL the time. It's like they are afraid to put a name to the actual pain and suffering that inevitably awaits these diabetics.. The CVD, blindness, kidney failure, amputations, neuropathy, stroke, obesity, hypertension, dental disease, skin problems, hypo-comas, hyper-comas... "Complications" was the only real risk every mentioned and besides.. they were soooo far down the road, no need to worry, right? That's the attitude..

My initial attempt to educate myself cost me a lot of time because it took me down the status quo path.. I made the mistake of asking our doctors, doctors at the "best childhood diabetes center in the world", to recommend some reading so I could better understand the disease and how to take care of the boy.. Well, hindsight is 20/20 right? Of course, they pointed me to all the same material that they had learned from. And guess what results I got?? The SAME AVERAGE SHITTY RESULTS..

But I trusted them, and armed with much the same knowledge and philosophy they were armed with, we were determined to be the BEST patients we could be and get his disease under control! We were gonna run "tight" and eliminate his highs and lows and ensure those "complications" remain a thing off in the distant future, a thing not to be talked about or even worried about

And we were the best.. We followed EVERY direction to the T. Religiously weighed his food, counted his carbs, chased his carbs with X amount of insulin, checked his blood every couple of hours, every single day to adjust... His numbers were still all over the place, all the time!! Up down up down - Scary highs, scary lows.. We'd go in, voice our frustrations and concerns.. We'd get pats on the back that we were doing everything right.. That our numbers were exactly what they'd expected and even better than most! And besides, those "complications" were so far off in the future that they really weren't worth losing sleep over.. "Enjoy life, do the best you can..."

I was doing everything "right", yet my boys disease was out of control. I blamed myself and his mother for doing "something wrong"... I secretly blamed him for .. doing SOMETHING wrong. It didn't make sense, we were doing EVERYTHING we were taught, yet it wasn't adding up..

My sister has an autistic boy. I was poking around one day and clicked on a Dr. Kruse podcast.. don't remember which show, they were talking about methylation defects and stuff I didn't understand, but I DID understand one point he was making over and over..

"We've got to ask better questions.."

And so, I started to ask better questions... It started with these:

Am I expecting too much?
Is this as good as it gets?

If this is as good as it gets ("you're numbers are better than most!).. And I'm still disturbed by where those numbers lead... Well, maybe I'm expecting too much and just have to suck it up and accept the reality of the situation. Do I need to have a come to Jesus moment and just admit that my boy is destined for this future misery???

Before I was willing to accept that this was his "fate", I had to answer a few more questions.

I'm sure that this is why Dr. Kruse insists that we HAVE to ask better questions if the answers aren't adding up... Conflict is a sign to us that we've got a flaw in a premise, belief or value..

Questions beget questions.

Are better numbers even achievable?
If I'm better than "most", then some are doing better than me..
What are they doing?

Wow! That was when I stumbled upon the one "inconvenient truth" that started to unravel an entire tapestry of "knowledge" and beliefs that I'd built to date with status quo materials recommended by status quo doctors..

"There is no such thing as an essential carbohydrate." -Taubes
(huh.. But the docs told me the brain needs glucose..)
"Somewhat, but what little glucose it does need can be created 100% by the body.. No intake of carbohydrates is technically required to support the brain.." -Phinney/Volek
"Small inputs = small mistakes" -Bernstein
"Why in hell would anyone recommend a diet with carbohydrate to metabolically deranged patients.. Less sugar you burn over a lifetime, the healthier your be, generally speaking."-Rosedale

Pandora's box was blown wide open by these answers, but only because something in me continued to search for the right questions to ask.

I spent the next year devouring everything I could find on the LC lifestyle.

We explained what we were doing to the boy, told him we were going to try a bit of an experiment and we'd see how it went with his daily numbers and his quarterly a1c.. We saw immediate success.

2 months into the LC/MP/HF experiment we were so excited to get down to Denver and see the results.. Here is what we got:

So there we were, best A1C to date.. The doc was all excited to hear about what we'd done to see such nice progress! When we told him, he recommended that we EAT MORE CARBS! And of course, he was "worried about his cholesterol with all these fats!" So he ordered up a bunch of labs...

This is the standard of care that diabetics receive from the best doctors in the world... These guys are paid to know this stuff.. It's what they do day in and day out. How is it that a curious dad who dared to continue to ask questions ends up knowing this stuff and they remain ignorant?

Because accepting these inconvenient truths would invalidate entire careers. But the pain and suffering that willful ignorance causes .. It's inexcusable.

Thankfully my boy trusts me more than he does that doctor. We read Dr. K's book and cleaned up our diet to the extent that by the next visit, our boy's A1C was and remains normal.. We basically did exactly the opposite of what the doctor told us to do.. You'd think these doctors would be falling all over themselves to pick our brains about how we did it so they could share our secrets with all of their other patients.. It seems like they'd rather not know..

I've spent a lot of time picking through Dr. K's epic blogs and digging up as many podcasts as I could find around the web. I've learned a lot but still have so much more to learn.

### caroline — 2015-03-01T08:19:13+0200

Thankyou so much for that Kelly ....it will help so many people.

What an amazing journey to wellness you are on....you are one incredible starfish!!!

your story parallels Kate's story - and she has a Doctor in her immediate family who won't listen even tho he can see the amazing transformations in his grandson.

### Josh — 2015-02-28T01:40:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biotoxins-mmp-9-lab-test.13064/post-156843>

> **Matrix metalloproteinase-9 in glutamate-dependent adult brain function and dysfunction**
>
> Matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) is extracellularly operating protease that is expressed by the adult brain neurons and released in response to enhanced neuronal activity driven by glutamate. In addition, MMP-9 can also be produced by glial and endothelial cells as well as by infiltrating leukocytes. Extracellularly, the protein is activated in a cascade of steps that involves also other proteases and then is inhibited by tissue inhibitors of matrix metalloproteinases (TIMPs). Under physiological conditions, MMP-9 is involved in neuronal plasticity, including long-term potentiation as well as learning and memory. This function of MMP-9 may relate to its synaptic action that is a consequence of post-synaptic/dendritic locus of mRNA and protein storage and release. Under pathological conditions that involve excessive function of glutamate, such as excitotoxicity, stroke and traumatic brain injury, MMP-9 is detrimental to the brain tissue, probably because of its excessive activity. This complex functionality and dysfunctionality of MMP-9 is well documented by studies on its expression patterns as well as with an aid of chemical inhibitors and knockout mice.
>
> Matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) is a zinc-dependent endopeptidase that together with the most closely related MMP-2, forms a subfamily of gelatinases.[1](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib1), [2](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib2) MMPs operate extracellularly (predominantly secreted pericellularly, and some membrane-bound) and are locally inhibited by endogenous tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) that bind MMPs non-covalently.[1](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib1)
>
> MMPs are expressed as inactive zymogens in which the cysteine residue in the propeptide binds to Zn2+ present at the active site of the enzyme. Activation occurs when interaction between cysteine and Zn2+ is disrupted, mainly by cleavage the propeptide off.[1](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib1), [3](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib3) The activation of the pro-enzyme is controlled by a cascade of steps involving other MMPs and plasmin system ([Figure 1](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#fig1)). When activated, the MMP-9 can be inhibited by TIMPs, particularly by TIMP-1.[1](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib1), [4](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib4) Notably, due to all the regulatory mechanisms, such as secretion, interaction with membrane receptors, internalization and TIMP-1 inhibition, MMP-9 acts outside the cell only focally and transiently to prevent excessive activity.[5](http://www.nature.com/cdd/journal/v14/n7/full/4402141a.html#bib5)

[http://www.smithsonianmag.com/travel/ben-franklin-slept-here-112338695/?no-ist](http://www.smithsonianmag.com/travel/ben-franklin-slept-here-112338695/?no-ist)

**_And then there were Franklin’s “air baths.” As a young man, he had been a long-distance swimmer at a time when swimming was something most people did only to escape drowning (one of his greatest developments, in my opinion, was the swim fin). He used dumbbells and could, by a slight stretch, be said to have invented the StairMaster by walking up and down the Craven Street house’s stairs (still there, still uneven) for exercise. And early most mornings, before he set to work, Franklin would sit, he wrote to a friend in France in 1768, “without any clothes whatever, half an hour or an hour, according to the season,” at his open, first-floor window, letting the air circulate over his, by then, considerable bulk. What the neighbors thought is apparently not recorded._**

I've always been intrigued and in admiration of Benjamin Franklin. Think about this statement above. Not only did he expose the inside of buildings to UV to demolish molds and viruses, he got some great grounding/earthing (avid swimmer and sat on the 1st floor) along with some really good morning sunlight on the skin. Did he also figure out the importance of light benefits 200+ years ago since he actively used it? And this was the guy that studied electricity--knowing what we do now, he was onto something profound.

# Thread: Physiological-"Benevolent" Insulin Resistance/Diabetes Type-0 & EMF
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physiological-benevolent-insulin-resistance-diabetes-type-0-emf.13065/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-02-26T04:00:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physiological-benevolent-insulin-resistance-diabetes-type-0-emf.13065/post-156650>

Please pardon me if this has already been discussed, as I'am currently behind most since I'm not yet a Gold member.

Check out this full paper on rapamycin induced insulin resistance and interestingly, longevity, low inflammation, etc. similar to long-term CRON, though without the CR: [https://www.dropbox.com/s/arv95wooctylko0/Blagosklonny - 2012 - Once again on rapamycin-induced insulin resistance and longevity Despite of or owing to.pdf?dl=0](https://www.dropbox.com/s/arv95wooctylko0/Blagosklonny%20-%202012%20-%20Once%20again%20on%20rapamycin-induced%20insulin%20resistance%20and%20longevity%20Despite%20of%20or%20owing%20to.pdf?dl=0) Once again on rapamycin‐induced insulin resistance and longevity: despite of or owing to

"Calorie restriction (CR), which deactivates the nutrient‐sensing mTOR pathway, slows down aging and prevents age‐related diseases such as type II diabetes. Compared with CR, rapamycin more efficiently inhibits mTOR. Noteworthy, severe CR and starvation cause a reversible condition known as **“starvation diabetes.”** As was already discussed, chronic administration of rapamycin can cause a similar condition in some animal models. A recent paper published in Science reported that chronic treatment with rapamycin causes a diabetes‐like condition in mice by indirectly inhibiting mTOR complex 2. Here I introduce the notion of **benevolent diabetes** and discuss whether starvation‐like effects of chronic high dose treatment with rapamycin are an obstacle for its use as an anti‐aging drug." - Abstract

The researchers go on to discuss these terms and what they mean. Essentially, **long-term (such as 7+ years) CRON leads to _low leptin_, low fasting glucose and insulin, low inflammatory cytokines, but poor glucose tolerance, and, well, longevity.** Administration of rapamycin, which antagonizes mTOR complex 2 does this as well. Now, we know that exposure to EMF can cause increases in glucose turnover in the brain, it causes calcium efflux, etc. Ketosis and ketone bodies, especially BHB but potentially acetone even more so, reverse some of these things; they also inhibit mTOR (I believe complex 2 actually) and over a long period if time, cause "physiological insulin resistance", which to me is the same thing as these states of physiological being the researchers in the above paper elaborate on.

Now, as far as I know, the mere presence of ketone bodies inhibits mTOR - this doesn't even require a full-blown ketogenic diet, simply elevating ketone body presence. Cold adaptation aids in fat metabolism, increases ketone bodies, and causes glucose to be used as a sort of 'anti-freeze'. This all occurs regardless of protein and calorie intake. **So, how can we bio-hack his to the fullest extent to protect against EMF?
**
My line of thinking is something like this, right now, for my environment, until I can move:

\- eat a modified, high calorie, high protein ketogenic diet (_minimum_ 80-100 grams of protein, and not worrying about getting up to 150-200+ (many Inuit sects ate 200+ grams of protein a day from seafood)), especially rich in glycine and taurine (1), with ~10g of carbs/day; eat this way every day (2)

-   supplement caprylic acid to increase BHB levels even more
-   (???) a few days a week, supplement KetoForce or KetoCanna (???) (3)
-   (???) Supplement other ketone bodies - [1,3 Butanediol](http://www.ketoresearchchem.com/product-p/bd.htm), mixed BHB salts, full-blown **ketone esters** (???) (4)
-   Look for **Triheptanoin**, a unique odd "chain" fatty acid that treats glycogen storage disorders, long chain fatty acid oxidation disorders, and increases ketone levels and also that of _**heptanoate (5)**_

_**\-**_ Do CT and become cold adapted, but also maybe:

-   (???) do mild 24/7 CT - we want to keep the pressure on, no? Short bouts of mild CT increase BAT and things like that, deep CT that induces shivering produces more calorie burn, more mitochondrial uncoupling, modulates sarcolipin, etc. but we don't want to really lower body temp, as Dr. Kruse says, CT = surface phenomenon; so we don't want to be shivering 24/7. Staying in mild cold all day every day should increase HSPs more than merely, say, taking a short cold shower each day, or even weekly ice baths; it would also keep one in a state of increased fat oxidation and potentially "insulin resistance" all day, thus not allowing glycation of glucose, yada yada yada; so, maybe wear a cool fat burner essentially all day? (???) (6)
-   Do face/head dunks
-   (???) Wear a cooling cap before bed and/or in the morning and/or in the face of a flare of any neurological or psychiatric symptoms (???)
-   (???)Chew ice to affect the tongue receptors, sending signals to the brain to improve blood flow (???) (7)
-   Gargle ice water to affect the vagus nerve?
-   Ice water in the ears to affect the vestibular apparatus?
-   (???) Warm/hot bath with a cooling cap on+chewing ice, ice on the neck/major arteries in the neck/throat, etc (???)

\- Maybe do a CT+Keto combo?

-   large dose of caprylic acid before shivering inducing CT (send ketones to brain more since blood flow will increase to brain and heart)
-   dose of ketone salts or ketone esters before shivering inducing CT (send ketones to brain more since blood flow will increase to brain and heart)
-   Nicotine, coffee, tea, caffeine, EGCG before shivering inducing CT (exaggerate the effects of CT; increase ketones due to more fat breakdown)

\- CT+Oxygen inhalation **(oxygen inhalations are used safely as a reliable nootropic in _healthy people_) (8)

I'll post cites below...**

Dang ! lol. Any chance to elaborate on any of my questions above, or, maybe instead, drop some breadcrumbs?

I feel as though CT+oxygen+ketones, whether salts or esters (esters being much more potent - inducing starvation level ketone generation), would be a POTENT cocktail. I know you did that lil biohack with CT+H2 filled super-cooled magnetized water from a copper pitcher/tank, and there was synergy. So I think there may be synergy here. I think it may enhance oxygen uptake or spare oxygen, especially in/for the brain, which gets me thinking, check this out @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/): [http://stan-heretic.blogspot.com/2012/06/unknown-ketoglucose-oxygen-sparing.html](http://stan-heretic.blogspot.com/2012/06/unknown-ketoglucose-oxygen-sparing.html) **Unknown keto+glucose oxygen-sparing metabolism?**

"Quote (second paper):
_The landmark study by Fox and Raichle in the late 1980s suggested that there is indeed a large increase in glucose metabolism that exceeds the changes in oxygen metabolism (Fox et al., 1988). The concept of uncoupled oxygen metabolism has been supported by reports of small increases in brain lactate during focal activation (Prichard et al., 1991), that initially were very controversial (Merboldt et al., 1992) and that are very difﬁcult to perform. The relatively small magnitude of change in brain lactate is difﬁcult to reconcile with the reported large uncoupling between oxygen and glucose consumption (Madsen et al., 1999)._

_My comment:

\- **a well known anaerobic (no oxygen) metabolism involves conversion of glucose to lactate, thus the lack of lactate, is the paradox indicating an unknown oxygen-sparing pathway.**_

_Quote (third paper):

_...as stated before, 2.81 mmoles/liter of CO2, should have been produced, with a theoretical respiratory quotient of 0.92 instead of the observed 1.90 mmoles/liter, resulting in a quotient of 0.62. To our knowledge, this deficit in CO2 production can only be explained by a carboxylation \[see [wiki](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carboxylation)\] reaction with the venous effluent transporting the CO2 in a form not liberated by the acidification used in the standard manometric technique for determination of CO2 and HCO3. According to most observations, the respiratory quotient of brain, which glucose serves as sole energy source, is close to unity (2,3,25). Brain, however, contains enzymes for all the major metabolic pathways (29-31), including fixation of CO2 (31,32); and oxidation of keto acids has been demonstrated in vitro (30,33,34). In addition, Kety et al. (35) noted decreased respiratory quotients in patients in diabetic ketoacidosis; but direct utilization of keto acids has not been found in this condition (3) or in fat-fed animals (36). Guettstein et al. also observed decreased respiratory quotients in patients with cerebral arteriosclerosis (37). Additional indirect evidence for a novel carboxylation reaction which would result in a low quotient has been Sacks' studies on glucose-14C oxidation in human brain whereby only 50% of glucose-carbon that is oxidized is recovered in effluent CO2 and HCO3,(38)._
_
_My comment:

Reduction of respiratory quotient to 0.62 meant that the process releases less CO2 than should have been based on the known and expected metabolic pathway. **That indicates that the unknown metabolic process does not oxidize carbon to CO2 but leaves it in the residua, while probably (speculating) oxidizing only the hydrogen from ketone bodies with the oxygen atoms reduced from glucose. The result is less need for external oxygen input and less CO2 production. (Mental note: next post about Dr. Jan Kwasniewski!)"**_

**"Dr. Jan Kwasniewski has always been stressing an importance of _hydrogen transport_. His way of arguing that fat (animal fat) is the superior fuel was because it contains more hydrogen, and that the reason polyunsaturated fats are not so good is because they contain less H. I used to think that this was a silly argument. Not anymore."**

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-02-26T06:38:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physiological-benevolent-insulin-resistance-diabetes-type-0-emf.13065/post-156670>

He also states, before all the quotes above:

"The papers review ketone and glucose metabolism in brain tissue. Among many of the issues discussed one finds that at a time of trauma, injury, hypoxia or during birth, brain tissue switches to some unspecified (unknown?) type of metabolism characterised by:

1.  **increased processing of glucose through the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP)**,
2.  increased expression of ketone-metabolizing enzymes,
3.  **relative reduction of oxygen use and CO2 release, in comparision with the overall rate of metabolism.**

It is important to notice that [PPP](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pentose_phosphate_pathway), as described in the literature is a pure glucose pathway oxydative-reductive (oxygen-sparing as compared with the normal glucose oxidative pathway) that is normally associated with NADPH production, used in reductive biosynthesis reactions within cells (e.g. fatty acid synthesis, RNA, cholesterol etc). Based on the known biochemistry, PPP is not supposed to have anything to do with ketone bodies or fatty acid metabolism in general.

Ketone metabolism on the other hand is not oxygen-sparing at all! A simple stoichometric analysis indicate similar oxygen usage per calorie compared with glucose oxidation (but with a significantly reduced carbon dioxide production!).

**It is very hard to reconcile 1 and 2 with 3 unless one postulates that a new yet unnamed metabolic process is taking place that oxidizes ketone bodies and at the same time reduces (i.e. takes oxygen out of) glucose, with PPP being a side effect.** **(It cannot be the traditional anaerobic glucose metabolism because of the reported deficit of lactate)"**

### Haley — 2015-02-26T05:26:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/am-i-digging-myself-into-a-deeper-hole.13066/post-156663>

I have been biohacking my digestion for at least 5 years now. Without the means of being able to get any tests done I suspect I at least have dysbiosis in my gut. If not some other pathogen. I'm not sure it it's too much bacteria (SIBO) or a lack of. I have been on very strict diets for about 7 years now. And things get better at first but then the bloating, irregular bowel movements return. Right now I feel as if i have adopted every protocol there is and its left my diet extremely limited-and making me fear food more and more. I'm wondering if all of this elimination stuff is back firing on me. This is what my diet looks like:

BAB: 8oz fish, greens cooked in CO, turmeric, lemon, 1/2 avocado
lunch: cucumber, greens, CO/duckfat, 1/2 avocado and maybe some coconut butter
dinner: 4-8oz fish, greens, CO or duck fat, turmeric
then i will have some more coconut butter or a mixture of CO & coconut flour & carob (just started this about a week or so ago)

My bloating was gone when i started the Leptin Rx about 5-6 weeks ago and has come back the last couple weeks. I feel more inflamed and my jeans are tighter again

Basically I have implemented an eli-paleo, SIBO, AIP, low FODMAPs, ketogenic diet. And balancing all of these has left me feeling pretty exhausted, especially when I don't feel that much better, am not loosing weight, have diarrhea/constipation and continue to be bloated.

I am back to thinking elimination is not my friend....I really could use some advice/insight. I feel I need more variety in my diet. And I'm afraid I'm missing vital nutrients. Also, I tried fermented veggies but suspected they were contributing to the bloating so i have stopped them.

Thank you

'smilier' lifestyle, great accidental typo

But more seriously, you may be underestimating the difference in environments that could be experienced in only a few feet of space. All fields, whether the Earth's native ones, our bodies, all other life forms' native ones, or even the nnEMF created by us and our technology must follow the same rule of squares - i.e. the strength increases exponentially (^2) the closer to the given source.

Comparing your current experience to similar experiences from your own past is only practical if you environment is exactly identical (or, even very very close would be acceptable I'd imagine). This would mean, not having added any new appliances/electronics, computers, Wifi, phones, to your own house, while also: not allowing the same in your close neighborhood proximity, and preventing additional cell towers, TV antennas, etc in your surroundings as well. Additionally, those (living beings) you surround yourself with would have to be on similar courses, and your stress/outlook on life/frame of mind would also have to be nearly identical. This means that these days, unless you are living in Green Bank, WV or a cabin deep in the woods (and ideally close to or below sea level...), then it is unrealistic to expect.

You are correct to some extent that 'populations in specific regions resemble each other', even the example of the higher suicide rates in Utah from the UB-1 blog ([http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-1-bio-hacking-altitude-suicide-loss-muscle-mass/](http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-1-bio-hacking-altitude-suicide-loss-muscle-mass/)) shows this at the macro scale. Consider that even those that appear healthy on the outside, or appear healthy when you are in their presence, may be suffering in quiet on the inside. Basically, at the micro scale different fleshpods/bodies will have different 'weakest links', so disease will express differently - i.e. not necessarily as weight gain/obesity or 'visible' symptoms.

Your first paragraph seems accurate - i.e. mitigating/being away from those things would be expected to create a healthier environment, all else being equal. BUT, if I am understanding you correctly in the second paragraph, you moved away from these things while gained 7,000 ft of elevation (correct?). This would be a situation in which all else is not 'equal'.

Although it seems you are indicating that some of your symptoms reduced/reversed when moving up that elevation change, if I'm understanding your note correctly, I'd imagine the elevation difference (vs. the nnEMF density in the 'old environment') is merely having an impact on your body through a different pathway or expression. This could mean different symptoms, but same results in 'appearance' (weight?).

> [Haley said:](/goto/post?id=156823)
>
> I believe people's health manifests in different ways. But I suppose it is my long journey of weight that has led me to focus on the mysteries of it the most. Sheesh, after all of the things i have tried, eliminated, sought after.. I am one of the first to believe weight is not determined by diet alone. Figuring out how physics and biology play together in weight loss (and health in general, of course) is obviously essential. But so difficult

'Your' fleshpod/body is always striving to maintain energy balance. My interpretation, hopefully accurate and definitely simplified, is that energy and mass are essentially interchangeable (E= MC^2). This means if your fleshpod attempts to maintain a certain threshold of energy/charge, and cannot do so at a given mass, one of the options it has to create this balance is adding more mass (i.e. 'potential energy' in the form of fat) to account for the deficit.

Consider a fun example:
Imagine there was a robot that was capable of noticing when its charge level was low, and knew to plug itself in to re-charge its batteries. Over time - as we all know from cell phones - batteries degrade and cannot maintain the same level of charge, so the robot would have to recharge with higher and higher frequency. Well, if that robot was given an additional battery pack to hold charge too, then it would allow the robot to be capable of recharging less often, BUT it would do so at the 'cost' of adding more mass to its system - i.e. the extra batteries.

The alternative option for the robot would be replacing the original batteries with a fresh set that can again hold a full charge - by doing this, it would restore its ability to go longer periods of time between charges while also maintaining the original ('leaner') mass

To correlate this to humans, in the same way as robot batteries degrade, our cells and mitochondria degrade and cannot hold charge as well over time due to chaos/wear and tear from use and from the information and environments they are required to deal with. Our fleshpod can tack on some extra fat/mass, which would be like adding the extra battery pack to the robot - but can you see how this is merely a bandaid vs. a solution though?

If instead, we 'replace' or 'restore' the bad cells/mitochondria with ones that can again function like the 'fresh' batteries for the robot, then we are re-creating the ability to hold a higher charge without adding the mass! It is obviously not as simple as switching batteries out of a robot, because we are much more complex than this... but the action is the same on a much deeper level.

Instead of just focusing on replacing/restoring the cells and mitochondria that have become less efficient, DR Kruse' theme is asking the question of WHY did those cells arrive at that inefficient state to begin with? This entails determining if/what about your entire environment is creating additional chaos, such that your fleshpod cannot keep up with the maintenance and must allow some systems to decline in functionality.

As 'we' get better at mitigating chaos, our fleshpods (i.e. 'robots') have the innate ability to 'replace' and 'restore' these ill cells/mitochondria on their own. But consider, to arrive at the point that our fleshpods will start to fix what we've messed up, it must first arrive at a state where it can maintain functionality without further detriment. Don't quote me on this, but I recall reading it is estimated to take 8 months for our entire bodies to essentially 'turn over'/'replace'/'restore' their cells anew - this is not a fast process, just as the deterioration is rarely a fast process. Doing enough 'right' for long enough for all of this to work for each individual is so unique based upon the state at which the individual deteriorated to prior to starting the corrections, and the speed at which their system can achieve maintenance and then begin 'restore' and 'replace' phases (determined by their overall environment - i.e. the place they are and what they expose their systems to; whether through external/field stimuli or internal foodstuffs).

So, to get your light right, some ideas:
\- this would mean only use artificial light, technology, etc when the sun's out, and ideally when you can be out in the sun. Less use is still better during these times, but mid day when the sun is the most intense would be the time when it would be most acceptable/matching of the spectrum in my understanding. When you cant avoid the artificial stuff after dark, blue blockers may help mitigate some effect.
\- when the sun is NOT out, use candles or fire (i.e. the minimally intense spectrum of light)
\- if possible, shut off the A/C power grid to your bedroom area at night to mitigate nnEMF while sleeping, in order to make it easier for your body to 'hear' the Earth. If not possible, at least get everything unplugged and out of there. Alternative option, would be sleep on the ground in a tent
\- if possible, watch the sun rise and set daily.

I'm unsure where you are in relation to the leptin RX and other protocols, but if you haven't already begun doing anything with CT and believe you are ready, it would be worth considering starting on that journey. The more cold your cells experience, and the more time spent in water, the better their functioning will be. This is why sea level or below would be more 'optimal'.... but sea level and below are also where there is more tendency for population increases, cities, nnEMF density etc. It is a fine balance...

Agree!

> The weight loss is a good sign that energy efficiency is increasing (i.e., in the robot example, we were capable of not carrying as many 'spare batteries' since the ones present can function better/hold a better charge).

I don't agree that weight loss is a sign of energy efficiency per se, just that more energy is readily available.

ie: You can get 80 units of energy by being 80% efficient with a 100 unit battery, or 40% efficient with two 100 unit batteries

Altitude and nn-EMF exposure in general lead to a shift to higher carb metabolism that results in more energy with less efficiency

> As far as the diet goes, this is really where the N=1 experimenting will come into play. From browsing and reading this forum for a while, I recall that many people had difficulty with the more 'strict' dieting during healing. I think it would be important for you to spread your wings a little, and 'test'/experiment with various alterations to your diet while keeping a journal of health, to determine what benefits you NOW at your CURRENT state, vs. what may be more beneficial as you continue to move closer to optimal. This would be an ongoing tracking tool that could change daily, monthly, yearly etc as your body changes. Getting DHA is an important place to start though I imagine you are already on that track
>
> Jack is trying to teach us that light is exponentially more relevant than diet, so I think managing/maintaining a good circadian rhythm would be a good place to start here if you haven't already. You've already moved away from the nnEMF which is good, although the elevation may not be favorable to you. But if you continue to see improvements then I'd imagine it is the 'lesser evil', per se, in your case at this time...
>
> So, to get your light right, some ideas:
> \- this would mean only use artificial light, technology, etc when the sun's out, and ideally when you can be out in the sun. Less use is still better during these times, but mid day when the sun is the most intense would be the time when it would be most acceptable/matching of the spectrum in my understanding. When you cant avoid the artificial stuff after dark, blue blockers may help mitigate some effect.
> \- when the sun is NOT out, use candles or fire (i.e. the minimally intense spectrum of light)
> \- if possible, shut off the A/C power grid to your bedroom area at night to mitigate nnEMF while sleeping, in order to make it easier for your body to 'hear' the Earth. If not possible, at least get everything unplugged and out of there. Alternative option, would be sleep on the ground in a tent
> \- if possible, watch the sun rise and set daily.
>
> I'm unsure where you are in relation to the leptin RX and other protocols, but if you haven't already begun doing anything with CT and believe you are ready, it would be worth considering starting on that journey. The more cold your cells experience, and the more time spent in water, the better their functioning will be. (latest blog discussing the buoyancy, combined with the cold, really makes this a beneficial choice for an overall increase in cellular function)

Agreed!

....

# Thread: Why Paleo has a deep problem: UBIQUITIN 3 is LIVE
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-26T15:07:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156700>

[http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-3-the-rhythm-is-gonna-get-you/](http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-3-the-rhythm-is-gonna-get-you/)

The rhythm will get you if you unplug from nature.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-27T04:10:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156770>

Carbs are not the enemy. Our modern environment is why you have trouble with them.......

There are people who mitigate their environment well and they use carbs theway mother nature intended......to make superoxide signals from their mitochondria. Then there are people who bathed in nnEMF and say carbs are poison because their mitochondria can't make SO to get into autophagy. Your job is to figure out are you one of those people or not and adjust your beliefs. Several LCHF folks have failed in this test in a big way and made a career ($$$) of it. They keep doing what they are doing and believe carbs are the devil........they ar as bad as Eric Topol to me. CONTEXT MATTERS.

Let's go further..........The mTOR pathway is “downstream” from the Insulin/IGF-1 pathway. The mTOR pathway completely “shuts off” autophagy and stimulates protein synthesis. What did this blog say about protein synthesis? This is the primary “danger” of eating too much meat or protein (i.e. stimulating the mTOR pathway). This is WHAT PALEO SAYS TO DO IN THIS ENVIRONMENT!!!! WRONG and not factual. Moreover, the more you exercise what does the literature say in relationship to ubiquination? Eat less protein and more fat otherwise you increase mTOR. Now, continually inhibiting the mTOR pathway is probably not a good idea either, since it is very important to synthesize proteins. When you do that you get an infertile podcaster who hates carbs..............This science shows both Paleo and LCHF holes in beliefs because of their current beliefs exist with understanding the physics of the environment, because it AIN'T food, it is the environment.

New Rule: No human being itself should be considered impaired innately, instead there are environmental short comings that cause the impairment. Thus, it is incumbent on the on the clinician to recommend treatment of the environment their patient is in. People react to an inferior environment, way before their genome is altered.
So, if we want to shut down mTor what are we supposed to eat after workouts? Nothing or water.......Why? Nothing to eat is IFing.

IF gets us to Autophagy and to SuperOxide burst

Why is nothing also sensible? What did CRON data in 1930 show? It worked. Why did I fight with Rosedale 4 yrs ago about CRON in primates and said it wont work any more? Is the Earth today representative of 1930? NOPE

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-27T04:11:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156771>

I just read Ub 3..........................I found the idea of water as a 'de-coherent lens' fascinating it's like 'life' is what enables perception of 'reality' but at a cost of obscuring also it in the process. The quantum processes are 'masked' by water but at the same time water (life) is what enables anything (us) to 'perceive' these quantum processes. So it's like there is a gain and there is a loss too. That's life I suppose. I hope this is not getting too 'philosophical' but I find that so interesting like water is a 'cataract' on reality but at the same time enables there to be a reality. (Thinking out loud)

I also found interesting the idea of 'inactivating' proteins as opposed to the ubiquination process. Is it correct to think of this as being somewhat 'pathological' in that that does not sound 'healthy' You also said these proteins can sometimes (accidentally) be re-activated which can begin auto-immunity. It's like they have lost their 'purpose' and now are engaged in something not good for the cell.

Can proteins be 'marked' for destruction but not actually be destroyed? Is that some of the problem in AZ etc? Should some proteins be 'marked' for destruction but don't actually get marked? I presume these are all varieties of what happens none of them 'good'.

I 'understand' a bit more why I had such a hard time getting this notion of the SCN being faster/ahead of the organ clock. To me coming from a kind of 'reductionist psychology' background the brain is seen as just 'following' the body, the brain for one thing developed 'later' than the body so it made instinctive sense that it was 'later'. You are showing it 'leads' the body though the brain may be 'later' in evolution it now 'leads' the body. It's what makes us human. Last question.............is this a factor in say other mammals? I mean I have heard of diabetic cats I suppose it makes sense that 'domestic animals' might have a similar problem as we do. I imagine this could hardly be a problem for truly 'wild' animals. Also I presume the brain and the SCN etc is a bigger deal with humans. Anyway sorry about all the rambling I found the blog fascinating and it occasions many many questions in my mind.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-27T05:05:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156775>

the answer to the last one was in the blog too.....ubiquitin is ubiquitous in eukaryotes.......you even asked this during the Q & A.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-27T05:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156777>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=156775)
>
> the answer to the last one was in the blog too.....ubiquitin is ubiquitous in eukaryotes.......you even asked this during the Q & A.

Hmmm.................I guess even 'wild' animals could have a problem in that they are also subject to nnEMF. That is the new 'wild card' affecting everything. I grew up on a farm so I can imagine 'domestic' animals could have quite a few of these 'human type' problems. The way they lived while not nearly as bad as modern day lots etc...........still the cows could not move around so much, were inside in winter on concrete etc. But they had fresh grass in the spring and nice hay in the winter their lives were not as awful as the real 'modern' methods.

Ubiquitin is ubiquitous in that it is in all animals that I get. But what I am wondering is all animals have an SCN..................I presume the answer is 'yes' but it is correct to think that humans have a more 'sensitive' SCN in that their brains are so much more powerful, is it true then that we are more prone to 'damage' from 'timing problems' of all kinds in that we rely on it even more and it is a bigger factor with us. But I presume all life can have this problem too........................

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-27T05:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156778>

Then I remember the whole 'bi-pedalism' thing. Is our SCN more vulnerable to 'mis-timing' because of this? I presume the answer is 'yes' so again it's our greatest glory but also a vulnerability and I suppose more than in animals. I remember Dr K someplace saying the really 'human specific' things like the neo-cortex and a very evolved immune system would be the first to suffer from the conditions of modern life so again I suppose 'last in............' 'first out'

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-27T05:51:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-paleo-has-a-deep-problem-ubiquitin-3-is-live.13075/post-156779>

All animals have a central Circadian clock.

# Thread: Flashing Lights Found On Distant Planet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flashing-lights-found-on-distant-planet.13080/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BTA — 2015-02-27T05:20:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flashing-lights-found-on-distant-planet.13080/post-156776>

**is there anybody out there? What an interesting story -**

A dwarf planet is shining two bright lights at a NASA spacecraft right now, and our smartest scientists are unsure what they are.
As bizarre as that sentence sounds, that's the situation with Ceres — the largest object in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, officially designated as a dwarf planet (the same category as Pluto).
NASA's Dawn spacecraft is approaching Ceres ahead of a March 6 rendezvous. The picture above was taken February 19, from a distance of just unde...r 29,000 miles, and shows two very shiny areas on the same basin on Ceres' surface.

[http://mashable.com/2015/02/25/strange-lights-dwarf-planet-ceres/?utm\_cid=mash-com-fb-main-link](http://mashable.com/2015/02/25/strange-lights-dwarf-planet-ceres/?utm_cid=mash-com-fb-main-link)

Trying to work out best compression outfit. Planning on swimming in sea first. Don't want to lose any weight - be nice to aded more to top bits! Should I have top & pants & should they be tight all over? Thanks

### QuantumRadical — 2015-03-08T22:05:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothes.13081/post-157897>

Try it without any compression in the sea. Your skin will thank you.

Randy

### QuantumRadical — 2015-03-18T01:24:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothes.13081/post-158821>

Everything scales, ocean = giant 'Earth' womb

### Jenny S — 2015-03-18T03:02:00+0200

### Linz — 2015-03-18T11:32:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothes.13081/post-158853>

### Da-mo — 2015-03-18T12:11:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothes.13081/post-158856>

> Compression shirts collapse the surface capillaries and allow your skin temperatures to fall faster than without wearing them. It sensitizes you quickly to cold.

[http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)

> **9\. Please talk about how to maximize CT. Is there something to a compression garment or not? would we have to be moving, like in Vasper, to get this lactic acid/HGH benefit?**
> At NASA Ames Research Park, scientist Peter Wasowski has developed an alternative exercise machine called Vasper. The user wears compression cuffs on the arms and legs and core-cooling panels on the chest and head. During exercise, the machine traps large amounts of lactic acid in the body, stimulating the pituitary gland and producing more HGH or human growth hormone.
> Vasper is a great tool but it cost some serious cash. I love it and it has been tested in deep space and the data behind it show the result is not just pure hormesis. My [CT protocol](http://www.jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/) is designed for the common man to do in their home to get results. A [compression vest](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B0000722HK?ie=UTF8&creativeASIN=B0000722HK&linkCode=xm2&tag=jackru-20) and ice water work too. It will just take more time than Vasper. If you can get access to Vasper, you’d be a fool not to do it.

[http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-13-the-faqs/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-13-the-faqs/)

### Tones — 2015-04-13T17:31:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothes.13081/post-161085>

You'll miss the best bit if you cover your skin, an awesome shiver walk/standtill as you let the breeze hit you while you're drying off.

# Thread: Cloud Cover, EMF's and photoelectric effect
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cloud-cover-emfs-and-photoelectric-effect.13095/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-02-27T16:04:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cloud-cover-emfs-and-photoelectric-effect.13095/post-156805>

Hi All,

This question has probably answered someone in the deep realms of the blogs or forums, but if anyone could help me to find an answer it would be gratefully received.

Living in a temperate maritime climate (UK) cloud cover can block most light (in any season) on avg probably between 1-6 days a week. The cloud cover forms a sheet of grey that colours peoples moods and energy levels in the same hue.

Does this relate to the SCN and CLOCK mechanisms as reduced light affects the ability to yoke to the circadian cycles? Or does the water vapour in the clouds effect the reflection/absorption of nnEMF? Or all of the above?

Your curiously,

Dan

I'm posting the link to the movie "Bought." You can view it free for the next week. I urge everyone to watch it and pass it on. Then do as your conscience dictates.

For me to post this on a public forum might be considered living dangerously but I will continue to do what is best for me and my family. My first job out of college was selling vaccines for the largest vaccine manufacturer at the time. I saw the political and industry side of things when in 1988 the Vaccine Injury Compensation Program was established. It was a great victory for the industry, all bought and paid for by guess who. I stand by all those who have given up careers and family to do what is right.

### Josh — 2015-02-28T05:18:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bought-a-very-important-movie.13097/post-156852>

Also fascinating is how to "defeat" a vaccine by splicing genes into the virus to ramp up the immune response to a level that bypasses the effect of the vaccine. Then there is the question of whether diseases may jump back to new hosts from people as COE's have changed. They managed to infect monkeys with high doses of smallpox which no one thought was possible at all in 2002. Until then, there was certainty in the scientific community that whatever else may have harbored smallpox besides man had died out and that no other species could be infected. Of course the USSR/Russians "weaponized" and mass produced it long after they signed a treaty so one has no idea what that may mean as time wears on....It will be interesting to understand how this ties into Ubiquination...I like extreme examples as there is always something interesting to be learned on the extreme....

Until then, there was certainty in the scientific community that whatever else may have harbored smallpox besides man had died out and that no other species could be infected. Of course the USSR/Russians "weaponized" and mass produced it long after they signed a treaty so one has no idea what that may mean as time wears on....It will be interesting to understand how this ties into Ubiquination...I like extreme examples as there is always something interesting to be learned on the extreme....

Josh, I know little enough about this area but I find myself interested and after I read this "Fear of the Invisible" I may take you up on the book you recommended. But instinctively I feel the like of Bill Gates etc are on the wrong track with this whole business of 'wiping out' viruses. It's a very militaristic mind set and probably doomed to fail. I read about some country Thailand maybe where they were trying to do this and the mayhem it causes is beyond description. Like the mayhem all the vaccines being 'tested' on very young and fragile children in this country I think it's a disgrace and very 'profit driven'

I mean the sheer AMOUNT of them it's just crazy and the TIME at which the children get them...................growing up in Ireland in the 1950's I think we received just TWO at around the age of 7 and spread apart like by a year or so. Mumps and measles were something you 'caught' naturally no big deal. I think some of the anti-vaccines people are saying also vaccines do NOT confer 'immunity' in the way catching them in nature does. It would not surprise me but hey!...............no big deal that's good for 'business' two we not need 'booster shots'. I don't think Dr K agrees but I think this whole area if rife with conflicts of interest and corrupted 'science'.

I am wondering now what Dr K is suggesting maybe that with proper Ubiquination we can sort of survive anything? Maybe so and hopefully true I mean it does strike me sometimes it's kind of amazing any of us are alive when you read about all the poisons and foreign chemicals we are exposed to. But we ARE.................alive so there is 'evidence' there but at the same time Mother Nature has limits and does break down and give in too. Especially it's a strong and sore point with me with very young and vulnerable children. They should NOT be used as 'guinea pigs' for the like of Merck, Monsanto and Psizer. Even the process of injecting them with those needles................as a child I was 'afraid' of that and I think with good reason. Aside from the pain isn't it different to be 'exposed' to a virus in the natural way to having it injected into you like that. That is another issue....................there are lots of issues............

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-02-28T13:06:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bought-a-very-important-movie.13097/post-156872>

The only virus I was 'scared' of as a child was polio. I remember seeing some children with those 'iron traps' they were in. But just reading here apparently only 1 in 100 of people who contract polio get's ill (how different is that to vaccines) and MOST children establish 'natural immunity'. Here is something about it...............

_"The key is the unexpected etiology of polio. The virus is orally communicated, mostly through shit and water, and in ‘the old days’ almost all infants acquired an immunizing taint as they emerged from the womb, or soon afterwards. But then came hygiene. Soap and water, and diminishing physical contact with others in less crowded dwellings, meant that unprecedented numbers of children had no acquired immunity. Soon it became clear that the polio virus, whose mass outbreaks had once been very rare, was developing a radically new social profile. Unlike all the plagues of the past, it tended to spare the slums and strike instead at the children of the better-off suburbs, places where a new middle class had settled with low-density housing and habits of cleanliness which combined to reduce immunity. There now appeared a large and rapidly expanding pool of children and young people with no immune defenses against the disease. As a result, polio began to occur in terrifying and uncontrollable epidemics, especially during and after the 1940s. In its timing, and in its ‘class war’ selectivity, polio was the emblematic disease of the middle 20th century."_

### BTA — 2015-02-28T23:57:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bought-a-very-important-movie.13097/post-156932>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=156883)
>
> Vaccines are no substitute for obeying the laws of this universe, depends upon how comfortable we are with being uncomfortable and the periodic culling of the herd...that is what pox viruses seem to have done historically in the populations of many organisms on this planet.

the human body deals with infectious agents - how? Evolution. People survived the Plague. Survivors developed a defensive genetic trait called hemochromatosis.

As Jack has said before - what kills us can cure us -

"As elements go, iron is a fickle and mischievous companion. Essential to life, yet impulsive, promiscuous, and destructive when allowed to roam unescorted, it poses a tremendous engineering challenge to human tissues.

Iron readily exchanges electrons with other elements. Indispensable to oxygen transport and metabolism, this property may also cause disease if iron participates in unsanctioned electron exchanges that produce free radicals -- an evanescent and particularly hot-blooded family of compounds that damage cells and DNA. As a result, all organisms dependent on iron -- from primitive bacteria to mammals -- go to great lengths to safely transport and store this potentially poisonous payload."

[http://www.theatlantic.com/health/a...-in-our-blood-that-keeps-and-kills-us/266936/](http://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2013/01/the-iron-in-our-blood-that-keeps-and-kills-us/266936/)

> "As elements go, iron is a fickle and mischievous companion. Essential to life, yet impulsive, promiscuous, and destructive when allowed to roam unescorted, it poses a tremendous engineering challenge to human tissues.
>
> Iron readily exchanges electrons with other elements. Indispensable to oxygen transport and metabolism, this property may also cause disease if iron participates in unsanctioned electron exchanges that produce free radicals -- an evanescent and particularly hot-blooded family of compounds that damage cells and DNA. As a result, all organisms dependent on iron -- from primitive bacteria to mammals -- go to great lengths to safely transport and store this potentially poisonous payload."

# Thread: Killing mechanism that can underpin Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases discovered
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/killing-mechanism-that-can-underpin-alzheimers-and-parkinsons-diseases-discovered.13109/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2015-02-28T19:54:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/killing-mechanism-that-can-underpin-alzheimers-and-parkinsons-diseases-discovered.13109/post-156902>

**Killing mechanism that can underpin Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases discovered**
_Asian News International_

02-16-15

Washington, Feb 13 ([ANI](http://www.aninews.in/)): A team of scientists has for the first time discovered a killing mechanism that could underpin a range of the most intractable neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and ALS.

The new study revealed the mechanism of toxicity of a misfolded form of the protein that underlies prion diseases, such as bovine spongiform encephalopathy ("mad cow disease") and its human equivalent, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease.

Researcher Corinne Lasmezas from the Florida campus of The Scripps Research Institute (TSRI) said that the study reveals a novel mechanism of neuronal death involved in a neurodegenerative protein-misfolding disease.

Lasmezas added that importantly, the death of these cells is preventable and in their study, ailing neurons in culture and in an animal model were completely rescued by treatment, despite the continued presence of the toxic misfolded protein. This work suggests treatment strategies for prion diseases and possibly other protein misfolding diseases such as Alzheimer's.

In the new study, the scientists used a misfolded form of the prion disease protein, called TPrP, a model they had previously developed, to study misfolded protein-induced neurodegeneration in the laboratory. Misfolded proteins are the common cause of the group of diseases comprising prion, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's diseases, ALS and other conditions.

Using biochemical techniques, the researchers demonstrated that TPrP induces neuronal death by profoundly depleting NAD+ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide), a metabolite well known as a coenzyme that is common to all cells and necessary for energy production and cellular homeostasis.

Restoring NAD+ proved to be the critical factor for the rescue of neurons subjected to TPrP injury. Even when added three days after TPrP exposure, an infusion of NAD+ reversed within only a few hours the fate of neurons that had been doomed to destruction.

The study shows for the first time that a failure of NAD+ metabolism is the cause of neuronal loss following exposure to a misfolded protein, said Lasmezas.

The study is published recently in the journal Brain. ([ANI](http://www.aninews.in/))

# Thread: Better Late Than Never: Hello Group
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-late-than-never-hello-group.13110/>

### Hans — 2015-02-28T21:50:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-late-than-never-hello-group.13110/post-156911>

A few weeks ago I found, then participated in, Dr. Jack's Facebook group while searching for groups addressing quantum physics and bioelectromagnetics because of my 1997 discovery when I changed a vine into a tree as a result of witnessing the deaths in 1988 of 37 yearling turtles under very anomalous circumstances involving electromagnetic field harmonics from 345KV utility distribution power cables and a steel railroad trestle. This event occurred during a powerful flooding storm with intense lightning. Being an electrician with paltry biophysics and biochemistry background, I approached the mystery from the Tesla angle and conventional electrical mathematics. In that I began to see patterns centered on fractals and the Golden Mean. After several years of futilely consulting university professors, government publications and using the inter-library loan services I consulted the Jews and that is where I began to see what happened. But the how of it is yet to be answered. It is one thing to do something but another to understand the depth of the how it happens. I hope to learn that here. As Jack suggests, biochemistry and biophysics are one whole. The result of my study is that I can now literally change a vine into a tree, but that is nothing to shout about. This is a first step on a journey. It is time to turn within and learn about self so that I can better understand the environment.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/better-late-than-never-hello-group.13110/post-156916>

# Thread: Possible damaged abdominal muscles from ice packs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-damaged-abdominal-muscles-from-ice-packs.13112/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kathy1967 — 2015-03-01T02:21:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-damaged-abdominal-muscles-from-ice-packs.13112/post-156940>

Hello all! I've just joined Dr. Kruse's site and this is my 1st time posting in Dr. Kruse's forum - I'm not a technology person so I'm not sure if I'm even doing this correctly! Feb 2nd 2015 I attempted to do some extra cold therapy in addition to the 10 minute cold showers I started in mid January. I put an ice pack directly on my lower abs instead of having a cloth barrier between my skin and the pack. I left the pack on for about 40 minutes (thought it was only about 15!) and when I removed it 2 areas under the pack were hard. Almost 1 month later those areas are still hard and feel like bruises but they aren't discolored at all. They feel like cords of rope actually. I'm very nervous about what it could be - I thought it would resolve but it hasn't yet? I'm going to call a doctor on Monday. Do any of you Dr. Kruse followers have any idea what this could be? I'm still doing cold therapy (ice baths) and I have no problems what so ever (besides an hour of shivering!) Thanks in advance for any help!

About a year ago I was going out in the morning and doing 5 minute planks in shorts and tank top. When the snow leaves the ground I'll start that up again! I do not have any electronics in my bedroom. I have one light (and it's yellow!), an overhead ceiling fan/light but it's always off. I have 3 electrical outlets in my room. I'm thinking about switching off the electricity in my room from the circuit breaker at bedtime except that's how I use my yellow light for reading. It depends on where I'm at as to whether or not I have fake lights on after sundown. I work at a gym until midnight and we have bright bright fake lights there all night long!!! No one at home is on board with me about the problem with blue lights so I wear my UVEX! ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: My word definitions helper extension for this site
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-word-definitions-helper-extension-for-this-site.13114/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2015-03-01T17:30:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-word-definitions-helper-extension-for-this-site.13114/post-156974>

Hey guys,

I just finished a basic extension that I would like you to try out and give me feedback. Basically you type a word and it's definition (for example EMF = **Electromotive force**) and then when you are reading through one of Jacks Blogs it will be highlighted green. If you click it, it's definition will show. It's a handy way to remember all the terms and acronyms flying about and you can word it in a way that helps you remember/learn.

Anyway it only works on google chrome for now. If you wana try it out and leave feedback I would be grateful. Here is the link : [https://chrome.google.com/webstore/...mbpbpcdhepkihfglmhohgl/related?hl=en-GB&gl=GB](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/jack-kruse-helper/dimgagppjhmbpbpcdhepkihfglmhohgl/related?hl=en-GB&gl=GB)

To use it just click the JH icon in your toolbar next to the search bit then to add a word to your dictionary type in the word (make sure you spell it correctly) and definition then click update. To delete a word, type in the word and then leave definition blank.

Oh and you have to refresh the page to see any words you recently added. Bit of a pain but I couldn't find an easy way around it.

Let me know what you think!

### PaulG — 2015-03-02T13:16:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-word-definitions-helper-extension-for-this-site.13114/post-157151>

Hi Sean

I think what you are trying to achieve is fantastic (A Kruse-Community cloud based custom glossary...which I have wanted for a long time) so thanks so much for working on this.

but perhaps implementation is not quite where it should be yet! .

-   How do you for example stop people entering in misleading or inaccurate definitions?
-   I cannot find a way of editing definitions once entered.
-   People are at different levels of understanding, so you may end up with a page full of green text
-   The popup window is basic and cannot support other links/videos etc.
-   Does not readily support collaboration over defining terms that are mutually understandable in plain english etc.

As an alternative, I took a look at similar extension and installed one called [Inline Search & Lookup](http://max99x.com/chrome-extensions/inline-search-and-lookup) that allows searching of terms from dictionaries and encyclopedia's. Ideally I think Glossary terms should be defined in the [wiki](http://jackkruse.wikidot.com/) that @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) set up (using a glossary filter). I would prefer the extension to be able to select multiple search engines as opposed to one and return results for each source. Eg. from JK wiki, then wikipedia or allow user to choose search engine using right click mouse context menu. Have emailed the developer about this.

..my email and reply from developer
Hi, thanks for your excellent extension

I think it would be a good feature to select instead of one active search source, top n. For example if you select Dictionary.com, wikidictionary and wikipedia then in the search results box you get to see the results of all three perhaps in collapsable/expandable groups. Alternatively or in addition put the top n search engines into right click menu and you can select the one you want to use.

Hope that makes sense

Hi Paul,

I'm glad the extension has been useful for you, but I have stopped maintaining it 4 years ago now, so I'm afraid no new features or even fixes are planned. That said, the source code is public

Some cool idea's in there, thanks Paul.

Anyway I love the idea of a community based dictionary but would be unsure how to implement it, in part, due to reasons you stated. However this extension is currently just for personal use. So you have to type in each definition you want and it's only visible on your browser. Kind of a ball ache but side steps some issues with the community based one.

To edit the definition simply enter the word in again and the new definition. More advice is in the help button.

As for the popup, yea it's basic as fuck but I have just finished updating it a bit, should go live in an hour, check it out.

You can add links but you would have to put in your own html inside the description. (for example: blah blaaah blaa emfs! blah blah <a href="[http://www.emfsarebad.com](http://www.emfsarebad.com)"> link </a>

### seanb4 — 2015-03-02T14:12:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-word-definitions-helper-extension-for-this-site.13114/post-157156>

UPDATE VERSION 1.1.0:

\-Highlighted words now have the same case as in the original document.

# Thread: Optimizing-Labs-PDF.pdf - Unit of measurement
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-labs-pdf-pdf-unit-of-measurement.13115/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dantothep — 2015-03-01T18:35:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-labs-pdf-pdf-unit-of-measurement.13115/post-156979>

Hi All,

Especially US forum members, when Jack wrote the PDF that is attached he forgot to include the units of measurement.

Can anyone shine a light on what they might be as it can mean quite a difference whether my labs are optimal or completely ruined:

Sex-Hormone Binding Globuline 25 or below
Estrodial (E2) <25
IGF-1 225 or above
Free T3 3.3+
Free T4 1.5+
Free Testosterone Top quartile to top 10%
TSH .5 to 1.0
Pregnenalone <130

Thanks in advance,

Dan

### dantothep — 2015-03-05T12:53:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-labs-pdf-pdf-unit-of-measurement.13115/post-157500>

Anyone help? Its taken me 4 months to save the money for these tests and now I cannot even compare them, so help would be most appreciated.

### fitness@home — 2015-03-05T15:02:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-labs-pdf-pdf-unit-of-measurement.13115/post-157509>

I just had thyroid labs and the ranges are:

**TSH** - 0.450-4.500 ulU/mL
**T4** - 4.5-12.0 ug/dL
**T3 Free, Serum** - 2.0-4.4 pg/mL
**RT3, Serum** - 9.2-24.1 ng/dL
**T4, Free (Direct)** - 0.82-1.77 ng/dL
**Thyoid Peroxidase (TPO) Ab** - 0-34 IU/mL
**Thyroglobulin, Antibody** - 0.0-0.9 IU/mL

### dantothep — 2015-03-05T15:36:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-labs-pdf-pdf-unit-of-measurement.13115/post-157512>

Thanks thats very helpful. These seem to match up with JK's sheet.

1\. You have cataracts, glaucoma, or macular degeneration, tinnitus, early onset neuro-degeneration. Your CT shows a calcified pineal. Cataracts are a protective mechanism the brain uses to reduce blue light exposure. If you also procaffeinate, you really need to bio-hack your environment.

4\. You have arrhythmia, hypertension, sleep apnea or pseudotumor cerebri or normal pressure hydrocephalus, or CPM, hx of rhabdomyolysis, alcohol destroys your sleep, have an abnormal T2 MRI of an organ.

### NeilBB — 2015-03-01T20:27:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/post-157019>

> The more protein you eat, the less exercise you should consider, based upon the linkage between ubiquination rates and the light physics of melatonin sulfation.

Jack, I'd love for you to elaborate on this a bit. How would one go about determining the optimal proportions of exercise and protein intake, for example?

Personally, I've been eating a lot of protein (with a lot of intermittent fasting and seafood) but with very little exercise past few months. And doing quite
well healthwise. So if I wanted to ramp up exercise, would someone like me have to consciously watch my large protein intake, or not so much if other factors are optimal?

Along same lines, while it's clear that too much ubiquitination is a problem, it seems intuitive that too little ubiquitinatuion is also problematic in that old misfolded proteins can be harder to clear... I suppose that if signaling has gone haywire, then you could have both too much ubiquitination in some cells and too little in others at the same time? Molecular chaos. Leptin resistance. Inflammation. Etc.

What about mTOR? Ubiquitination, mTOR, autophagy all have to be very closely related and interconnected...

It seems to me there might be an advantage to actually increasing one's ubiquitination rates coupled with increasing one's protein intake in order to support ongoing cellular reconstruction, especially in a hostile environment. Assuming of course that other thermodynamic and circadian variables, and brain-specific nutrition are being optimized. (This is kindof what my personal approach has been for years now, and by all measures I seem to have dramatically reversed my own aging and degeneration in just about all the ways I can observe...but again I eat a lot of protein and don't really exercise too much, except CT).

Would it be fair to say that if your circadian timing and environmental sensistivity are "optimized" then you can (and should) eat as much protein as you want and constantly renovate cellular construction to your advantage in that way?
