Right. Personally, I see no inherent conflict between eating a shit-ton of protein in a day and also having the benefits of IF/autophagy. All you have to do is eat all the protein at 1-2 times during the day (breakfast and/or supper) and just drink water the rest of time. That's what I do and it's been great.

I think one of the other problems out there is that there is a widespread myth out there in paleo and conventional medical world that we can only "handle" 20-30 grams of protein in a single sitting. My personal experience has been that that is total bullshit, of course. But people believe this so they "spread out" their protein intake throughout the day, which of course crushes their autophagy and defeats the purpose of the increased protein intake because now they can't turn over the old proteins. Their poor sleep from blue light compounds the problem...Hence, again, the unseen variables again lead to a false generalization, bad science, and do a ton of harm in medicine...

I think it's important to clarify when we say "increased protein" does x or y, to specify both the dose and interval that we are talking about. I suspect cross fitters are typically decreasing intervals between protein doses, which is entirely different from increasing daily protein load, in same 1-2 sittings that respect circadian cycles.

Jimmy tries to fast. I know. This theoretically should help his autophagy. But he is nn-EMF toxic and has poor sleep, so that counteracts that. And furthermore, I would think that his protein intake is so low that he would be triggering biological feedback mechanisms to prevent autophagy, because of threat of impending protein-starvation..

Cross-fitters eat plenty protein but can't achieve autophagy because of lack of sleep and fasting, and blue light issues... So is it that they really eat too much protein? Or is it actually that they can't achieve autophagy, and therefore can't effectively utilize the ingested protein, so they increase their number of meals in futility, and further suppress autophagy in a vicious cycle?

I ask because I think that I do achieve autophagy a lot and seem to be able to eat ridiculous amounts of protein without any harm... But I pay strict attention to circadian cycles and to sleep quality...

So in my case, I don't see how adding back strength training now could possibly lower my daily protein requirement. That doesn't make sense to me, regardless of Lagakos stuff. I will have to investigate further and maybe do an N=1...

### Josh — 2015-03-02T04:04:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/post-157118>

> **Conclusions**
> Even accounting for a possible negative publication bias (only 4 out of 48 reviewed papers failed to find an F2 phenotype), the weight of evidence suggests that developmental programming should be regarded as a transgenerational phenomenon. The evidence for transmission of a phenotype to an F3 or subsequent generation is less secure (nine studies were identified and five failed to show an F3 effect), but has nonetheless been demonstrated and is also identified in toxicology studies. Furthermore, there is no universal agreement on the mechanism by which developmental programming effects can be passed between generations.
>
> The debate as to whether an adverse F1 uterine environment, generated by an F0 developmental programming stimulus, is sufficient to programme an F2 or subsequent phenotype remains undetermined. However, the idea that that the conceptus itself might not be the vehicle of programming represents a distinct shift in principle from the direct transmission of a programming intervention through germline modification. The _de novo_ regeneration of the programming phenotype via the maternal reproductive tract with each generation has been referred to elsewhere as a ‘vicious cycle’ of developmental programming ([Gabory _et al_., 2009](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long#ref-55)). However, this description fails to take into account the potential benefits of developmental programming, situations where future generations adapt better to their environments. We suggest instead the term ‘propagational programming’ to distinguish those models in which the phenotype is not directly transmitted, but instead ‘re-programmed’ in subsequent generations. This class of model includes not only those where the uterine environment is thought to be the agent of propagation, but also somatic epigenetic re-programming by behavioural ([Francis _et al_., 1999](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long#ref-52); [Weaver _et al_., 2004](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long#ref-132)) or other mechanisms. Several authors also present evidence that even where a germline epigenetic modification is found, that this is propagated rather than transmitted directly to subsequent generations ([Burdge _et al_., 2011](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long#ref-29); [Radford _et al_., 2012](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long#ref-105)). **_Defining the relative contribution of propagational mechanisms of developmental programming to subsequent generations is an area that urgently requires further research, with implications for the ideas of developmental programming as a field._**

[http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long](http://humupd.oxfordjournals.org/content/20/1/63.long)

Think Pink Floyd now: A prism (can be like water and a protein interaction) can be used to see all the colors of the sun. When light passes through a prism, it bends naturally. What else can make light bend naturally? Gravity and electromagnetism. Each color of this rainbow can be seen at a different angle. Violet bends the most while red bends the least. This is important because gravity bends light.........so it has bigger effects on violet and blue light than it does on Red. Now ruminate on those bread crumbs..........

To start the reveiw:

> Neurohypophysial hormones oxytocin and [vasopressin](http://www.wisegeek.com/what-is-vasopressin.htm) differ in molecular structure and function within the mammalian body. The molecular structure of each of the two hormones slightly differs by two amino acid sequences. Despite the slight molecular variation between the two hormones, each has very different physiological functions. Vasopressin helps regulate water and solute concentrations, while oxytocin plays a role in certain reproductive processes. Differences between these substances are also seen in mood and secretion mechanisms.
>
> Physiological differences between the two similar hormones are due to the slight molecular structure variation. Vasopressin’s amino acid sequence is Cys-Tyr-Phe-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Arg-Gly-NH2, while oxytocin is Cys-Tyr-Ile-Gln-Asn-Cys-Pro-Leu-Gly-NH2. One pair of amino acids that occur in oxytocin but not vasopressin is isoleucine (Ile) and leucine (Leu). The phenylalanine (Phe) and [arginine](http://www.wisegeekhealth.com/what-is-arginine.htm) (Arg) combination is present in vasopressin but absent in oxytocin.
>
> Both oxytocin and vasopressin are synthesized in the hypothalamus and stored in the posterior pituitary gland in the brain. Varying amounts of the hormones are released directly into the brain. The rest of the hormones are secreted into the blood stream by carrier proteins called neurophysin.
>
> Unlike oxytocin, vasopressin plays an important role in fluid homeostasis within the mammalian body by regulating water, [glucose](http://www.wisegeekhealth.com/what-is-glucose.htm) and salts. The hormone is commonly referred to as antidiuretic hormone for its water conservation effects on the kidneys by decreasing the formation of urine. Secretion of vasopressin is regulated by osmoreceptors when simulated by factors such as thirst, blood pressure and fluid volume. Other potent stimuli of antidiuretic hormone secretion are nausea and vomiting.
>
> The main difference between oxytocin and vasopressin secretion is oxytocin’s ability to release itself from other tissues including the ovaries and [testes](http://www.wisegeek.com/what-are-testes.htm); while vasopressin's only secretion location is the posterior pituitary gland. Both genders produce oxytocin, but women produce the hormone in larger quantities than men. Oxytocin is released in large amounts during labor and helps to facilitate breast feeding. In child birth oxytocin is secreted when the [fetus](http://www.wisegeekhealth.com/what-is-a-fetus.htm) simulates the cervix and vagina. After oxytocin is released uterine contractions are enhanced to help induce child birth.
>
> Secretion of oxytocin and vasopressin affects moods and behavior. Women who are lactating tend to have increased levels of oxytocin released in cerebrospinal fluid. Increased oxytocin levels in women have been associated with establishing maternal behavior. Studies involving the brains of non-pregnant animals infused with oxytocin shows rapidly induced maternal behavior. Instead of inducing maternal behavior, vasopressin greatly influences social behavior and social bonding.

And just as we do not get the sun at night also we should not eat at night to avoid the light coming from the microbiome. Eating at night is therefore a circadian mismatch of a fairly strong order. I have found I do not eat at night but is helps to have that kind of scientific foundation behind it.
>

so about melatonin. It is being measured almost exclusively during the day, because no one comes to your bed in the middle of the night to take a blood sample ;-) The lab ranges should therefore be adjusted to the day levels. I have seen consistently very low melatonin on my lab results despite of falling asleep within 3 min max upon closing my eyes, dreaming vividly, waking up refreshed with sunrise and keeping almost evryday eaxtly 8.5 hours of sleep. In the blog it is said that elevated melatonin levels in standing position is a big clu circadian mismatch is present. Could you eventually use low standing/day melatonin levels as a clu circadian cycle is likely correct or the relation could not be extrapolated this way?

[https://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/hide-and-seek/201203/self-deception-ii-splitting](https://www.psychologytoday.com/blog/hide-and-seek/201203/self-deception-ii-splitting)

> Splitting diffuses the [anxiety](https://www.psychologytoday.com/basics/anxiety) that arises from our inability to grasp the nuances and complexities of a given situation or state of affairs by simplifying and schematizing the situation and thereby making it easier to think about; it also reinforces our sense of self as good and virtuous by effectively demonizing all those who do not share in our opinions and values.
>
> On the other hand, such a compartmentalization of opposites leaves us with a distinctly distorted picture of reality and a restricted range of thoughts and emotions; it also affects our ability to attract and maintain relationships, not only because it is tedious and unbecoming, but also because it can easily flip, with friends and lovers being thought of as personified virtue at one time and then as personified vice at another (and back and forth).

### NeilBB — 2015-03-02T19:35:57+0200

To me this is the base of the problem, that docs are mainly just doing their job following some methodology and guidelines during their working hours and people believe their are like JK... Traditionally, being a doc used to be more than a job. It was a profession, a calling, even a "sacred trust." Unfortunately, due to changes in society and economics, now it pretty much has become "just a job." A job still with a lot of responsibility, but now with very little authority, which makes it a pretty shitty job for most of us.

There are some "fat-cat" doctor "enterprising" types out there at the top of the pyramid who certainly do profit from chronic disease and cancer and don't want the system to change. So I don't think BTA is completely wrong. But BTA fails to see that this system was not the creation of those few greedy doctors, but instead of governments, corporations, and ultimately society-at-large. Everyone must share in the blame for what this has become...

Interesting in the distribution and availability of healthcare. Yes the relaxed regulation and pay as you go at moderate prices with highly skilled personnel make it an attractive destination. I have utilized it for this myself. What is "interesting" is how the medicine we are familiar with in the West under heavy regulation mutates in their combination of market economy underpinned with socialism and fragmented by extremes of wealth distribution, etc.. The government subsidizes parts of the system with the oversight and collusion of big pharma. My 5 day course of Cipro from 10 years ago cost me 90 cents. Most drugs such as these are available without a scrip and in 2005, the average public sector doc with a training in many ways comparable to the US and UK earned less than 100 USD per week. A fullcheck up with comprehensive bloodwork at the best Harvard affiliated center in New Delhi was about 110USD in 2005. That was a full day affair with dental check up, comprehensive physical, chest x-ray, etc...I had my knee scoped with an overnight in a high-end hospital in 2002 for about 400USD.

Consider this is a country where I have watched satellite TV in 1990 and then fiber optic to the curb beginning in late 1990's precede clean water and sanitation in many rural locations. Interesting that Modi is now getting around to toilets. I guess it comes down to comfort and priorities related to comfort vs. commerce, etc.. A lot of nuance when you leave familiar ground....

So many conspiracies...so little time....I guess they forgot to tell you that this is a prison planet...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-03T01:10:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/post-157216>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=157161)
>
> so about melatonin. It is being measured almost exclusively during the day, because no one comes to your bed in the middle of the night to take a blood sample ;-) The lab ranges should therefore be adjusted to the day levels. I have seen consistently very low melatonin on my lab results despite of falling asleep within 3 min max upon closing my eyes, dreaming vividly, waking up refreshed with sunrise and keeping almost evryday eaxtly 8.5 hours of sleep. In the blog it is said that elevated melatonin levels in standing position is a big clu circadian mismatch is present. Could you eventually use low standing/day melatonin levels as a clu circadian cycle is likely correct or the relation could not be extrapolated this way?

The fact that you're sleeping well with lots of dreams means that you're doing well

But let's touch on the sitting vs standing issue anyway ....

\----

The sitting vs standing study came up on the Facebook Group slightly over a week ago. Study summary -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:melatonin-sitting-vs-standing](http://qhwiki.com/study:melatonin-sitting-vs-standing)

I assume you're referring to these paragraphs from Ubiquination 4

When your melatonin levels are higher in the standing position, it is often a sign that you are suffering from some type of circadian light mismatch. Why? Blood flow to the pineal function are linked to the circulatory system that is energized by AM sunlight. In fact, it [does not depend upon food](http://caloriesproper.com/circadian-disruption-impairs-survival-in-the-wild/). Disrupting your SCN causes a circadian arrhythmia and an inability to entrain to LIGHT, but not FOOD. [Hyperlink](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006899397005714)

Melatonin levels are adjusted by blood pressure flows at night when we are supposed to be sleeping. When melatonin is elevated when we are awake, signaling has gone awry.​

Melatonin is the the body's way of bringing sulfur from the gut (serotonin) into the brain when there is little/dim light stimulus. Sulfur is a cooling agent (H2S), and serves to cool the brain down (by about 1C) for sleep onset to occur.

Sulfur delivery, also has the side effect of lowering blood pressure, probably a dual effect of vasodilation of vessels (vasopressin and oxytocin feature here ), as well as increased hydrophilic tendency of the vessel wall to "push blood down energy gradients" (Pollack's work on EZ water's capillary action).

The study I summarised showed greater melatonin concentrations in the standing position when there was dim light, and when it was night time. That is probably just a sign of the body trying to give an added "sleep stimulus" to get you to get down and go to sleep!  I view this as totally normal (though you should have already been lying down in the first place )

On the other hand, when you have melatonin during the day, then something is seriously wrong. You're converting serotonin to melatonin in the daytime, probably inducing some carb cravings, and everything is broken in funny ways that will manifest itself non-deterministically.

\----

**Exogenous Melatonin
**
This thread was linked to from the blog, under the premise that both High and Low levels of serotonin can lead to depression -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ow-levels-of-serotonin-cause-depression.6493/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/can-high-or-low-levels-of-serotonin-cause-depression.6493/)

You're walking a line between being able to shunt excess serotonin to melatonin or having enough serotonin to signal.

If serotonin is too high, you get too much ROS, along with the usual cascade -- excess ubiquination, lots of protein turnover, cancer if the cell decides to stick around.

If serotonin is too low, you don't get melatonin.

If you take exogenous melatonin, then you don't have a "serotonin sink" for which to dump excess serotonin.

It's pretty obvious how this is a problem affects the case of excess serotonin, since you basically don't have anywhere to put the serotonin, and you end up having high plasma serotonin.

In the case of low serotonin, you're basically giving the brain a "it's all fine " signal, when in fact your gut is screwed. Try communicating with a temporarily crazy spouse/significant-other who thinks that you're doing something wrong (against any logical conclusion) to understand how the gut feels in that situation

\----
**Another Sidenote: Eating Carbs in hopes of Improving Sleep
**
One of the main mechanisms at play here is definitely the increase in Serotonin levels.

If you can't convert that serotonin into melatonin, then this will not work.

Eating carbs at night is of course even worse, since you get a big blue light load and a high insulin level at a time when the gut is trying to shut down.

Eating carbs during the day when bright sunshine is present is possibly a way to improve sleep assuming that serotonin -> melatonin conversion is still good. This is definitely dose dependent, and contingent upon other factors.

I have talked about carb and protein refeeds (especially leucine, which does make serotonin). These will not work if your circ signalling in the pineal is screwed.

....

>
> I assume you're referring to these paragraphs from Ubiquination 4
>
>
> When your melatonin levels are higher in the standing position, it is often a sign that you are suffering from some type of circadian light mismatch. Why? Blood flow to the pineal function are linked to the circulatory system that is energized by AM sunlight. In fact, it [does not depend upon food](http://caloriesproper.com/circadian-disruption-impairs-survival-in-the-wild/). Disrupting your SCN causes a circadian arrhythmia and an inability to entrain to LIGHT, but not FOOD. [Hyperlink](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006899397005714)
>
> Melatonin levels are adjusted by blood pressure flows at night when we are supposed to be sleeping. When melatonin is elevated when we are awake, signaling has gone awry.​
>
>
>
> Melatonin is the the body's way of bringing sulfur from the gut (serotonin) into the brain when there is little/dim light stimulus. Sulfur is a cooling agent (H2S), and serves to cool the brain down (by about 1C) for sleep onset to occur.
>
> Sulfur delivery, also has the side effect of lowering blood pressure, probably a dual effect of vasodilation of vessels (vasopressin and oxytocin feature here ), as well as increased hydrophilic tendency of the vessel wall to "push blood down energy gradients" (Pollack's work on EZ water's capillary action).
>
> The study I summarised showed greater melatonin concentrations in the standing position when there was dim light, and when it was night time. That is probably just a sign of the body trying to give an added "sleep stimulus" to get you to get down and go to sleep!  I view this as totally normal (though you should have already been lying down in the first place )

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-03T03:04:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/post-157229>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=157223)
>
> Yew is finally getting it in his post above.........when couple cycles are uncoupled some rather unusual things manifest and this is what most of you did not realize in consults.............think back to consults........how many times did I ask people to get Vit D , melatonin, ASI's, and DHEA levels?
>
> 100% of the time...............

I want to put emphasis on the "**non-deterministic failures**" that I mention in my previous post, plus add on a random rant.

TL;DR:

**(1)** unless you can reproduce the exact sequence of events that occur before a failure (like a seizure), you cannot understand the actual cause, nor how to fix it.
**(2)** Events come in randomly from the external world, and controlling the internal environment (eg: direct modification of hormones) has to be finely controlled to match the external stressor to have any success
**(3)** In a coupled system, everything leading up to that point caused a failure, and the failure will cause new cascading failures.
**(4)** Nature is OBSESSED with efficiency. This forces coupling.​

This is very obvious in the distributed software systems I've worked on in the past and present. When you have thousands of messages flowing around an architecture of hundreds of machines. Maybe one machine exhausts it's ability to handle large binaries and fails abruptly. Maybe the message queue of one process on one machine is backed up, and the developers forget to build a back-pressure feedback loop to have a message expiry policy for the queue, and everyone that tries to send a message to the queue waits in line with no ability to get out of line. Or maybe some process explodes without being caught by an exception handling, but doesn't get caught by the under-eager garbage compiler and ends up causing a 2GB memory leak that causes the other 5 neighbouring processes to fail .....

What you see externally are angry Customers who can't access your website, confused developers who cannot reproduce the exact sequence of events that led to the failure state, and a malfunctioning piece of software which is only smart enough to scream "I'm broken " (which no help at all ....)

We've learnt as software developers to uncouple as many things as possible, at the expense of efficiency. Nature makes the opposite tradeoff -- efficiency at all costs!

Understanding what your code is doing is a lot more important than making it efficient; there is a fundamental tradeoff between efficiency and freedom to manipulate isolated components.

Isolating failures (not having them cascade) is more important than keeping low communication latency, and I'd rather take an additional 2 network hops to for handshaking for secure protocols (minimum 3 hops, whereas the body basically depends on instant-electric-communication to function optimally).

With software, modern logging technologies and new ways to describe computations as values allow developers to reproduce the failure state deterministically. With the body, which depends on quantum mechanical processes that change when observed, there is no such thing as observing or recording the failure state without actually causing it

\----

What I just said hits on a lot of the pain points that we've gotten into while trying to scale out software systems in a distributed computing cloud. (sidenote: I hate almost everything about "the cloud" as it currently exists )

The ways those systems fail are very similar to the ways that the body fails. Because we can "play god" so easily in software, it's been a great learning environment for me.

Lots of software devs will get what I just said above (which hits on a lot of different topics), but most are too nn-EMF-philic to apply that knowledge to Optimal Health.

Of course, with software, you can refactor code easily, or simply throw things how if they are too broken. This is analogous to autophagy and apoptosis respectively.

Legacy code rot is basically cancer. Famous saying in the software world, "Adding more programmers to a late project makes it later ...."

In modern software architecture, we componentise things in the way that is most convenient for us. When something fails, you pull out the component and make it better, and then put it back. All components speak to each other over shared communication methods, using pre-defined contracts. Implication: It doesn't matter what implements the contract, so long as the correct response is made, all is good.

In the body, you cannot pull out components this way, because we don't know how to re-establish and feedback mechanisms and the communication contracts between various components of the body. A software dev would say that this is a shitty coupled system that is really difficult to reason about.

\----

Random Computing Musings

(a) those guys who think that we can make Artificial Intelligence using Turing machines are clueless. The only way to make AI is to use substrate which is as energy efficient as (or more so than) those that the body already uses.

(b) I'm not yet convinced that Quantum Computing will become reality. I think it's more about unlocking the memory storage capacity of various substrates. eg: storing hundreds of terabytes of data in a crystal that can control water. Computers will likely continue to be serial turing machines.

(c) Content-centric networking is the way of the future for software . The body cannot afford to do this, and has to stick to a place-centric model, because of its obsession with efficiency, and constraints the physical laws of the universe place upon trying to eeke out that efficiency.

(d) Being able to reason deterministically about software systems is always the goal. Any implementation of Quantum Computing will be abstracted away to allow human brains to actually reason about the computations that they are making. ie: software systems will never approximate the energy efficiency of the body.

....

thanks, Yew, as always! i think I finally understand well the whole serotonin/melatonin issue. In this light to me it looks like that measuring both during the morning as it is usual for blood collection may have very limited clinical application. Basically it can point put that something is wrong, but it cannot tell you whether it is correct. The part abput lowering BP kept my eyes. i have been lately tracking a bit my BP and BG since the pregnancy is slowly coming to an end. I have noticed about my usual bedtime quite sharp drop of BP from 110-115/75-80 (my usual quiet daytime value) to something as low as 100/65. It happens during the time I cannot read anymore since "the letters are dancing" and my eyes are unvoluntarily closing.

### NeilBB — 2015-03-03T19:47:31+0200

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=157316)
>
> And that's why CT for life should keep chatting about crap that happens that we don't know about - because if enough people are talking about it, maybe it won't be so hard to difficult to believe and maybe the sheeple will wake up some day... but I doubt it -

Perhaps. But it's a fine line I think. If you focus too much on seemingly insoluble aspects of the problems, it tends to be paralyzing and can do more harm than good. Also, the sheep would need more than to be "scared" of Big Brother--they would also need to learn how to think for themselves and act independently. That's the hard part. It's easy to point fingers at corruption--everybody does that. But people have become so dependent on technology and on the conveniences of our division-of-mental-labor social existence that they have mostly lost their intellect, autonomy, and judgement.

Scaring people will not empower them. If the world is to improve, I don't think it will be the sheep that do it. Sheep will likely remain sheep and follow whatever leader asserts himself. The sheep's intellectual food of choice is dogma--by definition. Change has to come from those that are capable of thinking for themselves. And those that can think for themselves already probably have a pretty good idea of the challenges that lie ahead...

>

The sheep's intellectual food of choice is dogma--by definition. Change has to come from those that are capable of thinking for themselves. And those that can think for themselves already probably have a pretty good idea of the challenges that lie ahead...

Using the above terms, I believe such discussions could prove fruitful if had with those that are already 'awake'. Otherwise, it is likely just another form of fear mongering, even if said mongering is anti- the mongering that is already occurring.

There are more awake 'here' on these forums (or at least opening their eyes and glimpsing) than in most other places online that I have stumbled across... This does make it an ideal place to 'chat about' said issues/ideas in my opinion, BUT chatting about problems without presenting or chatting about the methods to solve them is still wasted energy that could be better utilized in nurturing the ideal environment to promote optimal health for the individual.

Consider that Jack didn't start blogging until he fixed himself, or at least was well on the proven path. It is only then, when the self is fixed, that there is even the potential for excess energy to be available to assist beyond the self.

Here's an analogy:
Think about 'optimal' as if it were like a bubble that surrounds you: First, your bubble must surround yourself effectively (...I believe most sheeple are still struggling to open the jar of bubbles, for what its worth ). Once you have mastered this, then you may begin to grow your bubble, and allow it to extend to helping others. Your bubble will still be small, but it may reach to those immediately around you at this point - so you may help your family, friends, SO. Once you have surpassed that level, and if your bubble is still healthy and growing, then it may help the next stage - neighbors, acquaintances. Keep growing the bubble, and you may find the energy to eventually help on a large scale, such as helping to make better changes in your community. Keep following this analogy, and you may/could eventually get to a level of helping out a state, or a nation, or potentially the Earth as a whole. But If approached backwards, i.e. trying to strum up change on a level beyond your bubble's reach, you are going to be much more likely to fail, or even end up making it worse even with the best intentions (i.e. creating fear mongering in those that cannot contemplate the implications of your message as anything but 'doom' due to a lack of energy).

### Josh — 2015-03-07T20:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/post-157776>

Relevant review:

> Mucosal proliferation, together with differentiation and apoptosis, are a continuous homeostatic process in the intestinal epithelium. The glutathione/glutathione disulfide redox status plays a key role in intestinal growth control wherein a reduced redox potential maintains a proliferative state. An oxidative shift in this potential elicits growth arrest and cell transition to a differentiated or apoptotic phenotype.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

FIGURE 1. Maintenance of cellular reduced GSH status during oxidative challenge. Coupling of GSH redox cycle to NADPH supply is shown. During peroxide elimination, the regeneration of GSH from GSSG is maintained by the GSH peroxidase and GSSG reductase system, the GSH redox cycle. The continued function of the redox cycle activity is dependent on the availability of NADPH, of which the pentose phosphate pathway is a major source. The cellular rate of NADPH supply is regulated by glucose flux in the pathway and the activity of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase.

FIGURE 2. Cellular responses to GSH/GSSG redox status and steady-state redox potential (Eh) for the cellular GSH/GSSG pool at different cell stages. A: fully differentiated cells are typically arrested in the quiescent state. Changes in the cellular GSH/GSSG redox state can initiate cell proliferation, growth arrest/differentiation, or apoptosis, depending on the cell type and the magnitude of the redox imbalance. Curves I, II, and III, respectively, represent terminally differentiated, mitotically competent, and transformed cell types. Adapted from Ref. 2. B: cells undergo a progression from a reduced to an oxidized state (i.e., from a more negative to a more positive Eh) as they transition from proliferation to growth arrest/differentiation to apoptosis. Proliferating cells exhibit ~50 mV more reduced potential than differentiated and growth-arrested cells, and the Eh in apoptotic cells is further oxidized by ~50 mV relative to growth-arrested cells. Redrawn from Ref. 9.

[http://physiologyonline.physiology.org/content/nips/18/5/201.full.pdf](http://physiologyonline.physiology.org/content/nips/18/5/201.full.pdf)

### Matty_M — 2015-03-01T20:23:59+0200

Not to be materialistic or anything, but I am very curious as to what everyone's favorite item, device, gadget, "trick", etc. is for helping them to be optimal. For some I imagine it would be an outdoor standing workstation, for others, maybe an EMF shielding hat or something, conductive moccasins, or a big outdoor rubber tub for ice baths. I thought of this because I bought a 64-oz hydroflask water bottle (that is also advertised as a beer growler), and everybody who sees it asks me about it. I can take a half-gallon of ice cold human jet fuel wherever I go, and it stays ICE cold (the ice doesn't melt) for almost a day (even though I clear it twice before then).

### prAna303 — 2015-03-01T20:55:40+0200

>

One things that changed very much for me the last couple of months is this:

Words, both spoken but mostly written is locking me up. The bandwith feels to narrow, my words can not describe the magic of the worlds, i can not "talk" with my full senses when being "live" on the web. I must take myself into the real world based upon nature, nature as my homebase and city as my visits. This lead me to become what i never thought i would, can not tell on this early stage but it is magic to live it out.

Writing on my computer makes me feel dumb, makes me feel weird because i must talk with the whole me, even writing these words make me squirm a bit, feeling off, out of sync. I could tell more with a blink of an eye, a twitch of a muscle or a movement on a stage.

I stopped writing in november, not just on computers but also on papers, i paint the world instead.

Everything we have made as add-ons to our own lifes is a mismatch, this is why gadgets and gears will never do it for us, that is an illusion which is hard to take away. We as species is the gadget, we play the world of chances and everything is possible.

When laws of modern world is laid upon us we think we need GADGETS.

### Martin — 2015-03-01T21:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-optimal-gadget.13117/post-157041>

METEX multimeter with MAGCHECK 95-E probe. Very accurate gauss meter comparable to ones costing thousands.

Best $200 I ever spent. It's helping to change my environment.

NSH

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-01T21:11:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/favorite-optimal-gadget.13117/post-157045>

Sun light and magnetism........all free. Applying them as a NS is my problem.

Hi Guys- Know anything about EMF exposure in Tesla/electric cars? I am thinking of getting one but my one hesitation is if the EMF exposure would cause health issues. Thanks!

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-03T00:35:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tesla-vehicles.13131/post-157209>

I heard on the radio today about some new Samsung cell phone and they were touting the fact it can be charged without being plugged in. It's amazing what a lure 'convenience' is just that slight bit of convenience is enough to win people over, forget ( though they don't know) about their health. And this stuff is increasing in so many areas like WTF is wrong with plugging it in to charge it...............

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-03T00:40:04+0200

### Optimalbound — 2015-07-13T23:12:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tesla-vehicles.13131/post-171865>

I found these measurements in [a Tesla forum](http://my.teslamotors.com/fr_CA/forum/forums/emf-dangers?page=1). Seems legit.

> wietse.mol | 23 JANVIER 2015
>
> I have driven a Tesla in a test drive, and had all the metering done by a specialized company with specialized equipment. Results were as follows:
>
> Low frequency fields, depending on acceleration between **100-1400 nT**
> High Frequency fields **10-700 uW/m2**
>
> Safety standards vary:
> WHO: 300-400nT
> UScongress: 200nT
>
> Natural background levels: 0,0002 nT
>
> As for the negative health consequences, saying there is no scientific evidence is mainly a result of not being informed.
>
> Please check [www.bioinitiative.org](http://www.bioinitiative.org/)
>
> For an overview of scientific research into health effects of radiation, this link can be useful:
>
> [http://www.bioinitiative.org/rf-color-charts/](http://www.bioinitiative.org/rf-color-charts/)
>
> Some 1800 scientific studies have shown a clear and significant relation between radiation exposure and adverse health effects.
>
> I understand some of the scorn, but find it somewhat immature to ridicule the facts in favor of your own opinion.
>
> Kind regards,

# Thread: calling nordic european countries members
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2015-03-03T12:45:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-157262>

Guys, it seems like that our plans to move to France are complicating a way too much and we may end up in another transit destination being somewhere around Stockholm. Obviously for me the only option is the countryside. I wonder how the nordic pace of light changes during the year affects you, how do you deal with it and whether you find in this piece of world any special benefits, that you would consider worth it to mention. I have never really been there apart of some city breaks, but my ipression is full of stunning nature and relatively unspoilt environment with good access to high quality wild fish and seafood. Would you be so kind and spend a few minutes giving me your point of view? We have to decide in a matter of days... Thanks!

### prAna303 — 2015-03-03T19:28:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-157320>

> I wonder how the nordic pace of light changes during the year affects you

I must have it this way... I mean, four different seasons where summer and winter is long, it makes me function because i accept nature and spend much time there. If one goes the other way like almost everyone else you open a door to hell around these places.

> how do you deal with it

Easy, suck it up and expose myself everyday to raw and naked nature. Winter is needed after summers crazy lifestyle...

> whether you find in this piece of world any special benefits

Okey, i speak for the northern parts now... N A T U R E !

I have the sea, mountains, woods just around the corner. Big parts is covered with firs and deep woods, water is great from streams, the air in the woods, wow.

> We have to decide in a matter of days.

This is tricky... Stockholm is wonderful in summer but ugly as hell in winters, why? No real nature to get a connection with. If possible you must go to the coastline and then the prices goes, wicked...

Can you expand your area from there? What will you do in Sweden? Search for the north is possible...

Swedes love technology, therefor cities are hell. Not just because EMF but also because they walk around like zombies with their gadgets, one gets depressed from that sight.

Just something from the north...

[http://jonnajinton.se/](http://jonnajinton.se/)

### kovita — 2015-03-05T20:34:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-157546>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=157320)
>
> I must have it this way... I mean, four different seasons where summer and winter is long, it makes me function because i accept nature and spend much time there. If one goes the other way like almost everyone else you open a door to hell around these places.
>
>
>
> Easy, suck it up and expose myself everyday to raw and naked nature. Winter is needed after summers crazy lifestyle...
>
>
>
> Okey, i speak for the northern parts now... N A T U R E !
>
> I have the sea, mountains, woods just around the corner. Big parts is covered with firs and deep woods, water is great from streams, the air in the woods, wow.
>
>
>
> This is tricky... Stockholm is wonderful in summer but ugly as hell in winters, why? No real nature to get a connection with. If possible you must go to the coastline and then the prices goes, wicked...
>
> Can you expand your area from there? What will you do in Sweden? Search for the north is possible...
>
> Swedes love technology, therefor cities are hell. Not just because EMF but also because they walk around like zombies with their gadgets, one gets depressed from that sight.
>
> Just something from the north...
>
> [http://jonnajinton.se/](http://jonnajinton.se/)

thanks a lot for your input. My husband is offerd a job in Stockholm so that is why. I was shocked by the mobile coverage map, because the south is basically lost. I feel like i would love the north, becUse I do not mind the cold and I love nature, the kids are made from the same paste like me. That would mean again that my husband and myself we have to separate. This was part of the original plan, to live for a while this way till we are able to find out how to manage job on site. Sweden is plan b because of the job offer, so if we have to think about living in different parts of Sweden, than any other reasonable place in Europe will do. And this is problem again, it is tough to look for pristine places.

Questions:
if we decide to go north, are there options for education of the kids (preschool/primary) avoiding swedish? Our kids are fluent in 4 languages now and as this suppose to be stopover this may represent a big point for decision

what about the ticks? Plenty...infected? I am sorry for this question, but walking around with lyme has been pretty tough lesson for me despite of all the good that it indirectly brought to my life.

any place you could suggest to get an idea about the possible house renting? For some reason (most likely my complete lack of swedish ;-) I am unable to find anything reasonable

despite of not being really very social myself, I like to know the people living arpund me, specially in rural areas. I feel like the people in northern Sweden must have a lot to offer due to the very particular natural conditions and relative isolation. My expwrience with Swedes is so far quite monotonous...I repeatedly emcounter people who talk different things than they think. This is sort of annoying for me since I struggle to handle this approach. I must say I only met Swedes abroad. Any call here?

### prAna303 — 2015-03-06T15:48:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-157632>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=157546)
>
> Questions:
> if we decide to go north, are there options for education of the kids (preschool/primary) avoiding swedish? Our kids are fluent in 4 languages now and as this suppose to be stopover this may represent a big point for decision

\- Most decently big cities have schools for english but only from a certain age i think.

> what about the ticks? Plenty...infected? I am sorry for this question, but walking around with lyme has been pretty tough lesson for me despite of all the good that it indirectly brought to my life.

\- Area around Stockholm is loaded with ticks... I would say you have to go at least 150km north to avoid them. Us climbers fear them down there... For sure they carry their stuff with them, many friends suffer from them.

> any place you could suggest to get an idea about the possible house renting? For some reason (most likely my complete lack of swedish ;-) I am unable to find anything reasonable

\- I would consider a place like Östersund, a great mix of good and bad. Even Söderhamn will do great, coastline deluxe. Google these places for help with renting a place.

> despite of not being really very social myself, I like to know the people living arpund me, specially in rural areas. I feel like the people in northern Sweden must have a lot to offer due to the very particular natural conditions and relative isolation. My expwrience with Swedes is so far quite monotonous...I repeatedly emcounter people who talk different things than they think. This is sort of annoying for me since I struggle to handle this approach. I must say I only met Swedes abroad. Any call here?

\- Tricky part... If you go to big towns like Stockholm you must get a place central, otherwise you end up in social projects. When you move up, we tend to be quite laidback but also not very social. I think you must choose what you main goal is, i would go with nature and build people from that.
\- We have issues with walk the talk here... One thing for sure, city humans do this but nature connected ones, not that much. This is why a town with a connection to nature is better... The ones i love is those above, climbers, surfers, skiers and wildlife persons.

> I was shocked by the mobile coverage map, because the south is basically lost.

\- We have quite good reception all over but i would say that it is better to have good than almost... This is why the most isolated places can be bad also.

Just some eyecandy.

Östersund:
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Söderhamn:

Visit their homepages:
[http://soderhamn.se/](http://soderhamn.se/)
[http://www.ostersund.se/](http://www.ostersund.se/)

you are a treasure! Sounds like a place one could fall in love. I am more and more sure Stockholm will not be possibly for me. I would rather stay on the coast. If I understood well, there are surfing sports, than would be fantastic.

### Hope — 2015-03-11T02:01:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-158147>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=157639)
>
> you are a treasure! Sounds like a place one could fall in love. I am more and more sure Stockholm will not be possibly for me. I would rather stay on the coast. If I understood well, there are surfing sports, than would be fantastic.

I have no answers for you Kovita, but I have several European friends who adore going to Sweden. I don't think you will have a problem finding a place to live to your liking. I think as people Swedes are more prone to like nature...and I remember traditional Swedish foods - like rotten fish - is very popular too. I have a friend's sister who moved to Stockholm as well....30 years ago. Became an MD there and never left. Loves it there. When she comes here, to the US, she always takes shrimp for some reason......

### Hope — 2015-03-11T02:07:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-nordic-european-countries-members.13143/post-158148>

[https://sweden.se/culture-traditions/10-things-to-know-about-swedish-food/](https://sweden.se/culture-traditions/10-things-to-know-about-swedish-food/)
Ignore the sweets, check out the fermented fish. And Lingonberries.  think Inger

Chances are you can live here for years without learning a lick of Swedish. Swedes are widely rated as world number two at English as a second language. Therefore it might take you longer to learn Swedish, and the Catch-22 is that fluency in the language is crucial to full integration. Signing up for SFI (Swedish for immigrants) could be a step in the right direction.[Learning Swedish](http://sweden.se/culture-traditions/the-challenge-of-learning-swedish/) might be a challenge, but worth the effort.

[https://sweden.se/society/20-things-to-know-before-moving-to-sweden/](https://sweden.se/society/20-things-to-know-before-moving-to-sweden/)
Check out NUMBER 7....right up your alley

I know wherever you will go, you will make an amazing adventure!

# Thread: HIV
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Melanie Procter — 2015-03-03T14:28:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/post-157269>

Hello,

I have just had a friend with HIV ask me "Does Dr. Kruse talk about HIV?" I answered "I don't know. I will check the forum." I figured Jack has been asked about HIV treatment many times. I search HIV and couldn't find anything. Anyone know if there are threads here on this subject?

My friend is about to go on the medication called "Stribild". I don't know what that is. He says his levels are not low enough to start that yet, but expects he will have to start it soon.

Mel

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-03T17:17:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/post-157301>

Melanie, I am purely speaking for myself here and quite aware it would probably paint me as some kind of 'quack' / 'weirdo'. (I am not a doctor so just 'weirdo' is fine!) BUT I think the whole HIV theory is WRONG and a big MISTAKE. I emphasize I am not speaking in any way on behalf of Dr Kruse and I know you didn't ask me but.................I think it is a tragedy and a shame the damage this 'theory' has caused. Rather than go on any more about it I would refer you/your friend to 2 people who are definitely not 'quacks/weirdos' Peter Duesberg whose book "Inventing Aids" convinced me totally that the HIV virus hunters were onto the wrong target and here is a video featuring Kary Mullis a Nobel Prize Winner in Biology for his invention of the PCR technique. The videos is also quite 'entertaining' at least I thought so.

Let me be clear also I have no 'dog in this fight' I am not gay and never tested positive for HIV. I was tested twice in the '80's as then it scared the hell out of me (both times negative) BUT what scares me now is to think the effect a 'positive' test would have on me as then I 'bought' into it totally. That's why I do talk out about it now because people can put themselves through Hell about it and it's all un-necessary they way I 'understand' it now.

Please if you have time try to watch this video and keep in mind this guy Kary Mullis IS a Nobel Prize Winner....................not just some dunce with a 'chip on his shoulder'

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-03T17:39:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/post-157304>

Is it only me................but some of the stuff Kary Mullis says and the way he says it reminds me of Dr Kruse just a little bit.................

### Melanie Procter — 2015-03-04T00:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/post-157362>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=157304)
>
> Is it only me................but some of the stuff Kary Mullis says and the way he says it reminds me of Dr Kruse just a little bit.................

I thank you for your help. I will pass it onto my friend.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-03-04T03:46:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiv.13147/post-157374>

> [Melanie Procter said:](/goto/post?id=157362)
>
> I thank you for your help. I will pass it onto my friend.

Thanks Melanie, I am usually 'allergic' to conspiracy theories but of course that bring up what exactly is a conspiracy theory. One man's conspiracy is another man's truth I suppose. But in the realm of Conspiracy Theory the HIV/AIDS one is an 'outlier' it is not mentioned much and has never caught on with the general public. BUT there are a lot of serious thinkers, doctors, political people who DO question it and for very good reasons. I have read quite a bit about it and there are so many holes in it from the 'accuracy' of the test itself, to what they are testing for, if they even find 'it' what does it do etc etc.

But to me is is interesting I don't see it as fraud or deliberate deception it's just a matter of once a 'medical theory' gets going and research and careers and reputations are at stake .............try stopping it. It can't hardly be stopped or at least it takes years and years. (Think about 'low fat' dogma) Meanwhile so many suffer and die. Dr Kruse seems to hold Luc Montagnier in high regard and to be fair to him he isolated what he called LAV as a likely 'suspect' in AIDS but Dr Robert Gallo in the US basically 'took' his virus re-christened it HTLV and then there was a big dispute between then and a 'political' settlement was made and the called the 'compromise' virus HIV. The whole thing basically stinks and I think Montagnier kind of back tracked on HIV being the cause of AIDS and later said a 'co-factor' was needed. But even that was political ass covering in my opinion or maybe a real scientist who started to see the weakness of the hypothesis.

Here's a video that goes into some of that it is especially interesting in terms of the 'political' agreement between the French (Montagnier & Chirac) and the Americans (Gallo & Reagan) and that episode alone should make people think...............

One day the shore will be swept clean, the next covered by weed; the shingle itself rises and falls. _Perpetually renewing and destroying_, the sea proposes a beginning and an ending, an alternative to our landlocked state, an existence to which we are tethered when we might rather be set free.”

# Thread: Nasal tonsil inflammation - autoimmune problem?

### Daniel — 2015-03-03T15:53:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nasal-tonsil-inflammation-autoimmune-problem.13150/post-157295>

Hi all, thank you for accepting me request to join you guys!

I'm living paleo-keto for more than a half year, and worked out great for me, but there are some problems I couldn't have a grab on. Roughly 2 years ago I had a real bad cold where my nasal tonsils got so inflamed that I got addicted on nose spray. If I didn't use nose spray my nasal tonsils blew up blocking my nose completely. The western docs over here had no idea what to do with it, so when I was in China, I went to a Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) hospital, and got myself prescribed herbal medicine, which eased the problem a lot, however not completely. Still until today, mostly at night the tonsils got swollen a little bit. It is not very bad, but not perfect either. Do you guys have any idea for me what should I try?

I visited the Chinese doctor again, and she said that I need to strengthen my immune system and it will go away, and btw my spleen looks very weak. I got some herbal medicine again, and hope it will help like last time, but I am very curious if any of you had this or similar problem, and how did you get through it?

Would love to hear Jack's suggestions as well.

Thanks a lot!
Cheers
Daniel

### nonchalant — 2015-03-03T19:33:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nasal-tonsil-inflammation-autoimmune-problem.13150/post-157322>

>
> Hi Daniel, welcome. Do you live in a big city? Do you get much sunshine?

Hi, I live in the suburbs of Budapest, but I drive a lot to the countryside, and go hiking on weekends. I just spent 2 weeks in China with plenty of sunshine  well the flight didn't do any good to my nose though.

### nonchalant — 2015-03-03T23:00:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nasal-tonsil-inflammation-autoimmune-problem.13150/post-157343>

Since your tonsils get swollen mostly at night, I would say that you have a circadian problem. Your tonsils behave when you get sunshine, and they swell when the sun starts setting. You might also notice that the problem is at it's worst at about 4-5 am.

You might want to read what Dr. Kruse says about the circadian rhythm.
[http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/)

### Daniel — 2015-03-03T23:46:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nasal-tonsil-inflammation-autoimmune-problem.13150/post-157345>

>
> Since your tonsils get swollen mostly at night, I would say that you have a circadian problem. Your tonsils behave when you get sunshine, and they swell when the sun starts setting. You might also notice that the problem is at it's worst at about 4-5 am.
>
> You might want to read what Dr. Kruse says about the circadian rhythm.
> [http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/)

Well, I have a good 8-9 hours of sleep every day. Wake up between 5:40-6:30am, and I go to sleep between 9:30-10:30. This time I even managed my flight without jetlag, and was able to switch +7 then -7 hours like nothing happened, so I think my circadian rythm is quite good. I use f.lux with the sunset, right now typing in darkroom mode
I believe it is some kind of false immune reaction, but cannot find an explanation for it, neither the cure, and the swelling has more to do with the posture. For example, when I sleep on my side, sometimes only one of the tonsils gets swollen.

Ah. I used to have something like that with my sinuses and nasal passages. Lasted for over a decade.

### Nittygrittydanny — 2015-03-03T17:15:07+0200

Sunshine again in North Florida.

Benn cloudy/raining for about a week and cold.

Outside working today as much as possible!!!

Funny how I look at sun/water/sleep now. Full moon has been an ass kicker the past few nights.

### fitness@home — 2015-03-03T18:23:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157311>

Missing sunshine here in Houston... I'm jealous

### nonchalant — 2015-03-03T19:11:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157317>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=157311)
>
> Missing sunshine here in Houston... I'm jealous

Doesn't it seem like it's been forever, Dawn?

### fitness@home — 2015-03-03T19:17:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157318>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=157317)
>
> Doesn't it seem like it's been forever, Dawn?

Yes. How much snow did you see Sally? Did you take advantage of the free CT?

### nonchalant — 2015-03-03T19:42:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157325>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=157318)
>
> Yes. How much snow did you see Sally? Did you take advantage of the free CT?

Oh, it was great! We were getting almost an inch per hour for the 7 hours it snowed. No wind, so the snow just piled up and bent the pine trees over. Lots of broken branches, unfortunately. And we were without electricity for a day and a half! Unfortunately DH finally got the generator going, lol. But getting more well water was worth it.

I still don't have the knack of walking barefoot in the snow. Too cold! But I could easily stand barefoot in patches of cleared dirt. Fun to see how I could warm my feet up from walking in the snow with flip flops by removing the shoes and standing on the ground. And I found a zip-up vest I could use as a coat, while still having my arms and legs bare. I'm sure the neighbor thinks I'm loony, wearing the same clothes year-round.

We were outside a lot, cutting firewood. The move last year put us behind on stacking up wood. Lucky we have lots of dead trees in the woods. Just have to get them before the termites do.

### Linz — 2015-03-04T10:41:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157398>

Beautiful sunny morning here in UK.

I start work outside in a sleeveless jacket too. It usually comes off after a short while. Just wondering if my old cashmere jumper is ancient enough to cut off the sleeves yet! Seems a crime but it is so much more comfortable than anything else.

### nicld — 2015-03-04T16:15:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wooo-whooooo.13151/post-157419>

It is finally going to warm up here (36F) so I can get outside and sit in the sun too.

# Thread: Jay Caff - England to Vancouver!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jay Caff — 2015-03-03T20:17:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157328>

Hey Guys,

So yeah, I've been wanting to post on here for well over a year now, but for some reason I've always refrained, I suppose the 'timing' just didn't feel quite right. After reading Jack's content pretty much everyday for a year straight, I thought it's about time I started interacting with all the familiar names on this forum - you guys truly have facilitated me and many others in our quest for optimal health.

I'm a 25 year old male from the UK, but have lived in Vancouver, Canada for the past 4 years. From a health standpoint, I've suffered heavily over the past 8 years with a variety of problems, it's only thanks to Dr Kruse that I'm beginning to firmly understand how and why things went wrong with respect to my own personal context. I believe so many of us with health issues get caught up in the notion of wanting to return to our previous state, but I love Jack's perspective on the concept of altering our trajectory in order to become an even better version of our former selves - I'm not here to be the person I was before, I'm striving for optimal!

I played 'Soccer' at a professional level in the UK, however at the age of 16 I began to experience many of the health ailments that I still suffer from to this day (I'll be sure to journal everything in the coming days). Consequently, my path was redirected and I eventually ended up here! Initially, when things began to go 'wrong' I would always question, why me? Why now? I suppose when you live a life incongruent to your biology and your brain lacks a serious amount of electrons, you're destined to ask ridiculous questions! Becoming unwell at such a young age was always difficult to comprehend, but in all honesty I'm grateful for 'breaking my mirror early on in life', it's allowed me to remain strong and focused in the face of adversity!

Intuitively, I've always known life was about timing, however after engaging in Dr Kruse's material I now understand it's true meaning and significance. To say I am 'buzzing' to see Dr Kruse present in Vancouver this coming Saturday really wouldn't do it justice. Dr Kruse, I'm sure you'll notice my distinctive Scouse accent, it stands out in Vancouver like a sore thumb!

Hopefully catch some of you on Saturday!

Cheers,

Jay

### nonchalant — 2015-03-03T20:55:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157332>

Welcome! Glad you're getting a chance to meet Dr. K.

### caroline — 2015-03-03T21:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157333>

Welcome Jay! enjoy meeting Dr. K. to the max! Please post some pics!
Have you connected with Clayton? Krista?
You are going to have a nice little support/cheer group there for Dr. Kruse

looking forward to reading your journal......

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-04T00:24:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157356>

come over and introduce yourself.........

### louise — 2015-03-04T00:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157360>

hi jay just saying hi from wales xxxxx have never visited Liverpool but did go to ellemere port I think once

### Jay Caff — 2015-03-04T02:15:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jay-caff-england-to-vancouver.13154/post-157365>

Thanks for the warm welcome guys, it's greatly appreciated - I'm really looking forward to implementing all the knowledge I've acquired through the blogs/forum over the past year.

Caroline, I'll be sure to start a journal (most likely after Dr Kruse's talk in Vancouver), it will definitely be an interesting read to you guys - quite literally a life full of mismatches.

Louise, where about in Wales are you from? I used to play football quite frequently down in Wrexham. Liverpool is a great city, I definitely miss certain aspects of it. Providing you get the chance you should definitely visit, I'm sure you'll love it - especially if you're a Beatles fan!

Dr Kruse, if you have any free time in Vancouver prior to your conference feel free to give me a shout, I'd be more than happy to show you the sites! Buzzing for Saturday!

# Thread: Long term sulfasalazine effects
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nicolemaes — 2015-03-03T21:36:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157334>

I'm hoping there is a guru out there who could shed some light on what potential effects longterm sulfasalazine could have on my children, two of whom have had Crohn's disease since age two. They've been on it for 11 and 6 years.

I read/listen to health stuff nonstop but haven't been able to figure out an in depth answer to my question. The basic answers I have gotten, many times, but I don't mind hearing them again, so feel free to comment if you have any knowledge base. Thank you!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-04T02:45:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157370>

It is in the end of the Ubiquination 4 blog............they can't mobilize serotonin to melatonin because of circadian cycles..........pretty simple. How does an antibiotic do it? Alters the frequency of light release from the microbiome and lowers H2 so that hydroxyl free radical can't be neutralized.........as process continues microbiome is degraded further and the process becomes hardwired by Hebbian learning........it is a Circadian mismatch.......and a bad one at that.

You need a pic up too!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-04T04:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157375>

Crohn's is associated with severe light loss, due to severe chronic blue light toxicity, in my opinion, resulting in very low H2 levels and tons of serotonin in the gut and very little melatonin in the pineal...........It is associated with people who strongly crave carbohydrates. It is also linked to many other autoimmune conditions and cancers.

The Crohn's, cancer, and light link is old in the literature. Since the early part of this century, it has been known that dyes which are used in histology for staining cellular components can inactivate microorganisms even under the conditions of simple microscope illumination. You might recall that Dr. Gerald Pollack stumbled into another microscope enigma, when a person in his lab realized that light alone from the microscope increased the exclusion zone in an aqueous environment and created a large amount of protons. Mitochondria also create large amount of protons when they make superoxide. It turns out microbes, bacteria, and viruses all can be activated and deactivated by photocatalysis.

Photodynamic action, usually occurs in the presence of oxygen, can proceed by a number of biologic routes either involving direct excited state reaction. These excited states occurred by electron transfer or hydrogen abstraction. I found this very interesting, but I did not connect any dots here until 2007. The photo-physics occurred indirectly by the formation of active species such as singlet oxygen or free radicals. Remember, singlet free radicals are paramagnetic and drawn to mitochondria. Oxygen free radicals have the same property, as does DHA and O2.

So can the human pituitary, as optogenetic research at Stanford University has proven. What is not well known yet, is how the human microbiome works with our cells using photocatyalsis and H2 production for signaling. In the 1930's, the literature showed that phages and viruses stained with **_certain color dyes_** became remarkably photosensitive, and in the early 1960's quantitative studies of photodynamic inactivation of bacteria and viruses were reported in the literature. I paid attention to these old papers in my residency when I learned about them because they were so unusual and counter to the education I was getting in medicine. This dance between bad microbiome, bad light and bad signaling is where Crohn's begins........antibiotics enter the picture because they further alter light release by the microbiome to agravate an already bad situation to worse.

### Nicolemaes — 2015-03-04T06:14:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157379>

Thank you so much for being so generous with your time and knowledge. I've read several of your blogs for a couple of hours trying to fully understand the info you provided, but I'm still not there yet!

Interesting connections with light. I can't imagine the light association would be causal given how young my children were when they "got" Crohns.

I wish you had a translator because I feel instinctively like the information you're providing is important, and could potentially really help in my longterm quest to keep my kids healthy (and maybe even optimally healthy!), but I just don't have the background to properly understand it. Maybe some of your books are more accessible to laypeople like me. I will check. I just discovered your work today.

Thank you again!

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=157375)
>
> Crohn's is associated with severe light loss, due to severe chronic blue light toxicity, in my opinion, resulting in very low H2 levels and tons of serotonin in the gut and very little melatonin in the pineal...........It is associated with people who strongly crave carbohydrates. It is also linked to many other autoimmune conditions and cancers.
>
> The Crohn's, cancer, and light link is old in the literature. Since the early part of this century, it has been known that dyes which are used in histology for staining cellular components can inactivate microorganisms even under the conditions of simple microscope illumination. You might recall that Dr. Gerald Pollack stumbled into another microscope enigma, when a person in his lab realized that light alone from the microscope increased the exclusion zone in an aqueous environment and created a large amount of protons. Mitochondria also create large amount of protons when they make superoxide. It turns out microbes, bacteria, and viruses all can be activated and deactivated by photocatalysis.
>
>
> Photodynamic action, usually occurs in the presence of oxygen, can proceed by a number of biologic routes either involving direct excited state reaction. These excited states occurred by electron transfer or hydrogen abstraction. I found this very interesting, but I did not connect any dots here until 2007. The photo-physics occurred indirectly by the formation of active species such as singlet oxygen or free radicals. Remember, singlet free radicals are paramagnetic and drawn to mitochondria. Oxygen free radicals have the same property, as does DHA and O2.
>
> So can the human pituitary, as optogenetic research at Stanford University has proven. What is not well known yet, is how the human microbiome works with our cells using photocatyalsis and H2 production for signaling. In the 1930's, the literature showed that phages and viruses stained with **_certain color dyes_** became remarkably photosensitive, and in the early 1960's quantitative studies of photodynamic inactivation of bacteria and viruses were reported in the literature. I paid attention to these old papers in my residency when I learned about them because they were so unusual and counter to the education I was getting in medicine. This dance between bad microbiome, bad light and bad signaling is where Crohn's begins........antibiotics enter the picture because they further alter light release by the microbiome to agravate an already bad situation to worse.

### kovita — 2015-03-04T12:51:35+0200

Nicole, Jack often talks about epigenetics and how it can be "expressed" in vertical derection between generations. it is the part which many do not like to listen at all..it is about how what your mother or grandmother did and lived affects your own kids. Women are born with all their eggs in place, no more eggs created during the women's life. Epigenetics is fascinating and scary. Just keep on reading the blogs, there is a book as well, I think it is lighter reading than the blogs. After all, there is a lot you can do now and for free,a s mostof the poeple on this forum do. Kids have unbelievable capacity of plasticity and become qickly optimal as soon as the minimal conditions are met. I wish you all the best!
