### nonchalant — 2015-03-04T22:42:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157448>

Hi Nicolemaes, welcome. To lower blue light toxicity, and restore the circadian rhythm, your children should avoid any artificial light at night. Period. They should also not eat after it gets dark, because of blue light given off by the microbiome after eating. During the day, they should be outside every minute possible, starting at sunrise.

They should not drink fluoridated water, which interferes with pineal function, and water chemistry.

The science behind Dr. Kruse's blog can be intimidating at first. Reading his book helps immeasurably.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-05T07:16:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157490>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=157375)
>
> Crohn's is associated with severe light loss, due to severe chronic blue light toxicity, in my opinion ....

Highlighting certain parts of your post, then some personal conclusions follow:

Crohn's is associated with severe light loss, due to severe chronic blue light toxicity, resulting in very low H2 levels and tons of serotonin in the gut and very little melatonin in the pineal ...........

It turns out microbes, bacteria, and viruses all can be activated and deactivated by photocatalysis.

Photodynamic action usually occurs in the presence of oxygen, and can proceed by a number of biologic routes involving direct excited state reactions. These excited states occurred by electron transfer or hydrogen abstraction. (TYW: a healthy gut does not have much O2)

The photo-physics occurred indirectly by the formation of active species such as singlet oxygen or free radicals. **Remember, singlet free radicals are paramagnetic and drawn to mitochondria**. **Oxygen free radicals have the same property, as does DHA and O2.**

What is not well known yet, is how the human microbiome works with our cells using photo-catyalsis and H2 production for signalling.

In the 1930's, the literature showed that **phages and viruses stained with _certain colored dyes_ became remarkably photosensitive**, and in the early 1960's quantitative studies of photodynamic inactivation of bacteria and viruses were reported in the literature.

This dance between bad microbiome, bad light, and bad signalling is where Crohn's begins ........

Antibiotics enter the picture because they further alter light release by the microbiome to aggravate an already bad situation.​

\----

**(1) The gut bacteria provide extra energy to the body**

Photon emission from bacteria within the gut can have diverse wavelengths, but I think the most useful wavelength is basically the UV photons.

The GALT basically acts **exactly** like the skin, and has high DHA concentration to capture the UV light that is emitted from gut bacteria, and convert that into electric current.

EZ water absorbs strongly in the UV range, so it acts as a good medium to transmit photons of particular wavelengths (only two thin bands in the UV and IR range).

\----

As for the other forms of light that bacteria emit, I will assume for now that the body somehow manages to get it to become infrared light (heat).

A plausible explanation is that actin and integrin networks in the gut exhibit entropic elasticity, which allow for any form of excitation to end up as mechanical tension and liberated as heat -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...elcome-comments-here.12199/page-5#post-148033](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/tensegrity-9-is-live-welcome-comments-here.12199/page-5#post-148033)

We already know that collagen and IR light play really well, and there is a good reason to believe that collagen strands bounce red light between each other by virtue of biophoton emission from proteins -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304416513000202](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304416513000202)

This paper basically summarised how proteins tranmist red light between each other -- [http://scitation.aip.org/content/aip/journal/jap/111/9/10.1063/1.4709420](http://scitation.aip.org/content/aip/journal/jap/111/9/10.1063/1.4709420)

And again, another paper showing biophoton transmission through neural tissue (a in-vitro study, but still useful for illustrating the principles) -- [http://static.shop033.com/resources/8B/13707/Other/Biophotons as neural communication signals.pdf](http://static.shop033.com/resources/8B/13707/Other/Biophotons%20as%20neural%20communication%20signals.pdf)

\----

**(2) The gut bacteria signal energy status to the body**

This should be pretty straightforward. Less light input from the gut is a signal to the rest of the body. This was described in the blogs:

\- OSF #1 -- [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:osf-1](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:osf-1)
\- Tensegrity #14 -- [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:tensegrity-14](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:tensegrity-14)

\----

**(3) More diverse flora produce greater spectrum of light**

This is speculative for now, because I have not seen papers describing the photon emissions coming from all the various strains of gut bacteria.

But if a more diverse flora produced a wider spectrum of light, and assuming that the gut collagen has the ability to "transduce" all of it to either IR or UV, then a wider spectral emission would favour energy assimilation of human tissues over other unwanted invaders like yeasts.

\----

**(4) Hydrogen Gas as Free Radical Scavenger**

I am unsure of how readily hydrogen gas that is produced in the colon dissolves. A review of Chaplin's interfacial water page doesn't give me much -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/interfacial\_water.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/interfacial_water.html)

For now, I will assume that hydrogen gas dissolves readily into the negatively charged water interface in the large intestine. This produces both a H+ and a H- ion. The H- ion is great at scavenging free radicals

Example of scavenging of the Hydroxyl Free radical (note: •H is basically the same as H-)

•HO + •O → O2 + •H (1A) Neutral-neutral
•HO + C+ → CO+ + •H (2A) Ion-neutral
•HO + •N → NO + •H (3A) Neutral-neutral
•HO + C → CO + •H (4A) Neutral-neutral
•HO + •H → H2O + photon (5A) Neutral-neutral

**Reaction | k (T=10 K) cm3s−1**
1A | 7.7 10−10
2A | 3.5 10−11
3A | 1.38 10−10
4A | 1.0 10−10
5A | 3.33 10−14

From wikipedia -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydroxyl\_radical](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydroxyl_radical)

The greater the k value, the more likely the reaction is to occur. So if there is an oxygen radical present, it will be the most likely to react.​

Reaction between H- and free radicals usually lead to subsequent biophoton emission. The Phosphene Phenomena paper is good to review here, which talks about ROS and RNS, and how scavenging of those radicals lead to emission of biophotons -- [http://qhwiki.com/study:phosphene-phenomenon](http://qhwiki.com/study:phosphene-phenomenon)

Personally, I think that the wavelength of light being emitted will depend on the production mechanism of the actual free radical. Most free radicals however, are produced in the mitochondrial cytochromes, via reactions with NAD/NADH, which produces UV range photons -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1751-1097.1985.tb01549.x/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1751-1097.1985.tb01549.x/abstract)

"Forcing these radicals to calm down" causes emission of light of the same energetic quanta in the UV range (which encompasses the 380nm "healing wavelength")​

Dissolving of H2 into serum leaves lots of H+ in the serum, which is a good thing if you can deal with it:

**(1)** feed them to mitochondria to liberate as free heat through uncoupling
**(2)** feed them back to GI tract bacteria as fuel to produce more light

Number (2) is a plausible explanation for why eating fermentable stuff like Galacto Oligosaccharides (GOS) or animal fibre produces positive health outcomes for gut health.

Butyrate production in the gut basically works via the same mechanism. The production of hydroxybutyrate in the large colon essential gets into plasma, and dissociates to form lots of H+ ions (this is studied greatly in diabetic ketoacidosis -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3058661/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3058661/))

...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-05T12:49:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-157499>

Regarding the post above, I think it may be useful to talk about how I go about coming up with this shit

Put simply, I literally try to come up with random scenarios that "seem likely", and then try and see if the facts fit my model.

The train of thought when it comes to elucidating new knowledge about Nature is:

\=> Haha! I'm God!
\=> I create my own arbitrary Rules. A rule is valid so long as it plays well the rest of the existing rules
\=> Design things to be the most efficient as possible
\=> Couple as many things together as possible and ensure that nothing goes to waste

So when you're reading that post above, you should be able to see where I make assumptiosn, and what conclusions can be drawn if those assumptiosn are true.

Again, I make the assumptions first, usually with no basis of evidence whatsoever , and then try to validate the assumptions after the fact using existing evidence.

If that succeeds, then great, I have successfully "become god" and learnt something new

If that doesn't work, I say to myself, "you silly bitch, which part did you get wrong ", and try and tweak the model a little bit. Sooner or later I'll know whether I was partly correct or just plain wrong.

\----

In the post above, I basically assumed that:

(a) bacteria was put there for a reason
(b) a large number of species is a good thing
(c) more bacteria is usually better
(d) any energy produced by the bacteria is put to use => disease is a result of inefficiencies of energy production

Some of these are based on empirical observations, but stuff like point (a) is basically put forth in the spirit of straightforward hubris. If I'm wrong, well, I got more information

\----

I assume that there is always an empirical "right answer" out there when dealing with things in the Natural world. If I am unable to see it, it is only because I am too incompetent. A certain degress of success has always come to me when I could afford to be incompenent

\----

However, when dealing with my regular life and work, I always try to uncouple threads of thought, simplify things as much as possible, and strive to make things understandable instead of efficient -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/page-6#post-157229](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ubiquination-4-is-now-live.13116/page-6#post-157229)

Uncoupled systems behave independently of each other, and minimize side effects when one system fails (everything fails )

Our thinking tools should be as uncoupled as possible, and as scale-dependent as possible -- I should not have some failure in a lower or higher level system affect my resaoning abstractions at the current level.

I know I'm not explaining this well ; it tends to be obvious in computer code (which is read as a tree) and not obvious at all in prose (which is read serially, one word after the next).

.....

### Ake Bono — 2015-04-09T16:43:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/post-160741>

I cant add more info on the light issues cause I'm still a beginner like you ...

I was one who was taking and (luckly  cant stand sulfasalazine...
I want to share that in my case it didnt help to reduce inflammation and other immune reactions better than natural approach and a suitable diet.
In my case the immune reactions was because antibiotics and infections in the gut.

I was a UC candidate and they recommend sulfa both rectally and orally. Great combination...
As time went by Ive learned to identify foods that aggravate my condition and
avoid them accordingly to the bugs identified and other imbalances in the body like enzymes insufficiency...

A good stool sample and identification of overgrowth helped me a ton.
In my case a big part of the problem is SRB - sulfate/sulphites reducing bacteria.
that happened after lost the good ones with antibiotics high EMF (i guess).

Sorry for your kids!

# Thread: Your Work Environment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-work-environment.13158/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2015-03-04T00:04:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-work-environment.13158/post-157350>

Call it fate, call it coincidence or call it quantum entanglement.

This morning I grabbed my Trifield meter and brought it into work again. I had already adjusted my workspace so it would be "safer" last year. We had a renovation recently and today they came to installed cable management systems under our desks. They mounted it underneath the desktop (along with the surge protector).

As soon as he finished, I took readings with the trifield meter of my desk. It was off the scale in one area that I use daily (bet you can't guess where...). I asked the installer to take it down ASAP, took pictures and sent them to Facilities to back up my decision.

I can't do much about the wifi here, but my immediate workspace is where I spend about ten hours a day, five days a week.

Check your environment people!

# Thread: Hi from down under.

**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dr SImon Thompson — 2015-03-04T07:53:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-from-down-under.13160/post-157385>

I am a 48 y.o. G.P. from Bendigo Australia.
I read paleo FX and decided to order some bloods.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-04T23:48:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-5-is-now-live-archimedes-meets-ubiquitin.13162/post-157453>

A little disorganised for my liking , but a lot of principles I basically thought, "Meh, that makes sense". This was a very intuitive read for me

\----

Sneaky reference to Big G  (gravitational constant). You know it isn't a constant, and depends on the point of reference -- [http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill](http://qhwiki.com/video:eu-nov-2014-wal-thornhill)

Now what I want to do is to measure the apparent gravity as seen by a particular cell in a particular location in the body. This of course, is scale independent -- ie: it is valid to compare gravity between mitochondria and golgi apparatus, as well as between red bloods cells and the heart, as well as between the body as a whole and external gravitational sources (including the direct and indirect effects of nn-EMF emitters)

I'll use the example of blood pressure and oxygen delivery.

The heart produces a large magnetic and electric field relative to the rest of the peripheral tissues (by virtue of it's mitochondrial density).

Apparent gravity, and thus apparent weight, is thus greater to a red blood cell (RBC) that is closer to the heart. Assuming that a person is standing upright, this means that:

**(a)** a RBC next to the heart has lesser weight than a RBC within the feet
**(b)** water next to the heart has lower density than water in the feet.

The implication is that blood-borne-objects within the feet displaces a greater weight of fluid than the blood-borne-objects near the heart. This essentially means that the bouyant force toward the direction of the heart is greater on a RBC at the feet.

I don't think that the actual mechanical effect will help much in bringing blood back to the heart easier.

But what does happen is that the changes in buoyancy allows the RBC to know where in the body it is, and to know when to dump oxygen.

Out on a limb: maybe the greater fluid displacement can lead to transient increases in zeta potential, since the space between RBCs gets larger -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3372997/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3372997/) . Higher Zeta Potential usually means more "slippery" RBCs that can dump oxygen better.​

....

> “_**The sea defines us, connects us, separates us**_. Most of us experience only its edges, our available wilderness on a crowded island - it’s why we call our coastal towns ‘resorts’, despite their air of decay. And although it seems constant, it is never the same. One day the shore will be swept clean, the next covered by weed; the shingle itself rises and falls. _Perpetually renewing and destroying_, the sea proposes a beginning and an ending, an alternative to our landlocked state, an existence to which we are tethered when we might rather be set free.”
> **― Philip Hoare, The Sea Inside
> **
> [http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-5-the-sea-within-epigenetic-control/](http://jackkruse.com/ubiquination-5-the-sea-within-epigenetic-control/)

Fascinating blog. Some random tangential thoughts I had in some possible future directions?

It's interesting to note in summation that when any object is submerged, that gravity and buoyancy are always opposing forces and that the mediator between these two forces is always the relative density between the submerged object and it's surroundings.

Of course we are focused on water now, and for good reason, since its fundamental to all life. But, it's also interesting to note as an aside that Archimedes Principle actually applies to objects submerged in gases (air) just as much as it does to objects submerged in fluids. But since air is so light, we tend to not notice this as much. But it is precisely why a balloon (you just hinted at this and did not elaborate) filled with helium--or even heated air--rises in air. Warm air and helium are less dense than cold air and therefore easily defy gravity, when they are submerged in the colder air. Therefore, actual gravitational effects are really quite dependent _even in air_ on parameters like temperature and density--not just on the relative mass of two objects as we tend to think. Kind of makes gravity seem less "fundamental" in a sense, doesn't it?

And what about the density and buoyancy in air of the important signaling _gasses_ like hydrogen sulfide, nitrous oxide, and carbon monoxide? It so happens that CO is slightly lighter than air and that H2S and NO are slightly heavier than air. Interesting fact. I don't know if it's important yet. But very interesting, because even though we rarely think about it this way, we are in fact, all actually submerged in air here on the earth's surface. Also, hydrogen gas (like helium) is significantly lighter than air and therefore in the right applications can counter gravitational forces quite significantly. So maybe Yew can someday actually build that antigravity machine he was so excited about...

Also, all of these gaseous density relationships would also have to change with increasing altitude as the composition of the surrounding atmosphere changed, and would of course be massively different in space or on another planet. I could see this having a potentially big impact on the gaseous portion of intracellular signaling too, an additional effect to the EMF effect? Not sure a space suit would really compensate for all of that. Looks like I'm gonna have to put off that vacation to Mars for now, bummer

Yeah, the way to think about water is that it charge separates to form ionic constituents which act as carriers for electric charge.

See Martin Chaplin's site -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/material\_anomalies.html#elec](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/material_anomalies.html#elec)

Quote:

The [electrical conductivity](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water_properties.html#elec) of water increases with temperature up to about 230 °C due mainly to its increased [dissociation](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water_dissociation.html) producing higher concentrations of the highly conducting H+ and OH- ions, which reach maximum concentrations at about 249 °C​

Chaplin talks in great detail about this ionic charge separation here -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water\_dissociation.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water_dissociation.html)

Sulfated lipids and proteins further filter light to get to the specific frequencies we use for energy. Ubiquitination is a topic rarely spoken in ancestral health, medicine, functional medicine. Two hormones, Oxytocin is a hormone from the posterior pituitary.........and on other hormone, ADH, is made there, ADH, controls water flows in the CNS and the rest are made from the anterior gland. The pituitary is surrounded by special vascular system. Given what you should have learned about the interaction of the pineal and Superior Cervical Ganglion and light, consider this: Sunlight is made up of a mixture of red, orange, yellow, green, blue, violet and indigo. Our modern world uses microwave technology which uses blue light exclusively........the dose makes the toxin for cells when this frequency is present and alters hormone release optogenetically..........why is blue light fundamentally bad? It destroys melatonin and DHA in you retina. Light in the retina entrains circadian cycles via the SCN.........and you need DHA in your SCN to run your circadian clocks and to control OPTICS in your brain to release hormones that control mitochondria programs that handle food electrons to generate proton signals in cytochromes and the free radicals, superoxide and hydrogen peroxide, and the hydroxyl free radical.

THE KEY EPi-PALEO Rx POINT: When your blue light toxic you lose superoxide burst at cytochrome 1 where glucose enters Electron Chain Transport.

The Epi-paleo Rx is simple........DHA with ketosis is the key. Why? DHA is never metabolized for energy. Other fats are.

DHA is used to restore the clock mechanism while other fats are used to generate electric and magnetic fields in mitochondria to get to autophagy using a free radical called superoxide. Superoxide has an unpaired electron that becomes reality by the iron sulfur couples in cytochrome mouths as protons pass through it. Without fatty acids, you can't activate or us the glycerol 3 phosphate shuttle properly to make carbs appear like fats to help the F:N ratio. This is why the PPP can be accessed without direct dietary glucose being needed. When this happens, no FADH2 input occurs to the CoQ couple in mitochondria. Without ketosis from fats, no free radicals can be made, period. When superoxide vanishes from cytochrome 1, there is no signal for mitochondrial biogenesis. Without mitochondrial biogenesis, you work with old, redox shifted mitochondria, hence, you can't recycle themselves using autophagy. In fact, this situation causes you to develop an idea glucose/fructose/carbs are fundamentally bad for biology, and you get Robert Lustig, Jimmy Moore and Gary Taubes beliefs about carbohydrates. You do get something's partially correct about the science in this situation. But being partial correct is akin to being partially pregnant, it confuses the real truth. With a "Taubian belief paradigm", you embrace ketosis, but not DHA ketosis; and this becomes a huge problem for your belief paradigm. This is where the Epi-paleo solution exceeds paleo's solution, because it fundamentally goes after the key problem. Why? It is because you do not realize that DHA is the only lipid that can keep your SCN time piece that controls the clock genes slightly ahead of your run away organ clocks because of the blue light exposure, you allow in your life. DHA is the only lipid in evolutionary history capable of turning light into a DC electric current and vice versa. This current drives the speed of the SCN clock. If a battery has no juice can it run a device faster? Nope. This is why the retina has more DHA than the brain, naturally. This is the HUGE Missing piece in the LCHF. More DHA means more current which allows the rolex in your head to say ahead of the organ clocks in the liver and pancreas. See every somatic human gene has a clock gene in front of it in our nucleic acids. This points out why DHA is not an energy source for humans and why they are not equipped to extend long unsaturated poly unsaturated fats. We need to eat them and we reserve them for the CNS provided they are in the SN-2 position. If you get them from a pill they wont be. Why is this important. SN 2 position makes DHA paramagnetic. O2 is also paramagnetic. What does this mean? Anything that is paramagnetic is drawn to things with magnetic fields. Guess what generates them in you and me? Mitochondria. NS like me do MEG and EEG daily that prove it. Maxwell's laws tells us anything with large electric currents have an associated magnetic field at 90 degrees to the current. direction Guess what ECT is? an large electric current, hence, O2 and DHA are drawn to mitochondria. Guess what else is paramagnetic? SUPEROXIDE. WHY? All free radicals have unpaired electrons......making them naturally paramagnetic.......where are they drawn? Getting the picture yet? Mr. Moore and his folks have a lot wrong............ The physics here, and it is the same ones that keep orbiting atomic clocks above your head now running 38 microseconds faster than the GPS devices on earth........Why? Gravity bends light. What else? Blue light bends more than any other frequency.........your boy Jimmy Moore is addicted to carbs........problem is he does not realize the gear he is using to make money tweeting, ipading, and bloggin is what is keeping him from results. Blue light is akin metabolically to mitochondria is the same as eating sugar........cause he does not understand optics nor physics...........neither do most biologists........but that will change. You must ask better questions......ketosis without DHA is a step in the right direction but it is a small step for mankind........we need a larger one to solve the extinction event we are creating for life.
Physics dictates biology at all levels.........they just dont realize it. Remember there are no carbohydrate, lipid, or protein transporters in the input for mitochondria......all foods break down to electrons that carry photons (light) on their back from the sun. The output of cytochromes is protons........what science describes protons and electrons actions? physics, specificially quantum physics? What is the only force in the universe that controls charged particles? The electromagnetic force......of which light is a part...........Time to upgrade your game........it has never been about food. It is about the light frequencies we allow and this dictate how electrons and protons are handled from foods.........in mitochondria........that information is what sets ubiquitin rates.......not food."

### Da-mo — 2015-03-05T05:12:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ubiquination-5-is-now-live-archimedes-meets-ubiquitin.13162/post-157483>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=157454)
>
> Fascinating blog. Some random tangential thoughts I had in some possible future directions?
>
> It's interesting to note in summation that when any object is submerged, that gravity and buoyancy are always opposing forces and that the mediator between these two forces is always the relative density between the submerged object and it's surroundings.
>
> Of course we are focused on water now, and for good reason, since its fundamental to all life. But, it's also interesting to note as an aside that Archimedes Principle actually applies to objects submerged in gases (air) just as much as it does to objects submerged in fluids. But since air is so light, we tend to not notice this as much. But it is precisely why a balloon (you just hinted at this and did not elaborate) filled with helium--or even heated air--rises in air. Warm air and helium are less dense than cold air and therefore easily defy gravity, when they are submerged in the colder air. Therefore, actual gravitational effects are really quite dependent _even in air_ on parameters like temperature and density--not just on the relative mass of two objects as we tend to think. Kind of makes gravity seem less "fundamental" in a sense, doesn't it?
>
> And what about the density and buoyancy in air of the important signaling _gasses_ like hydrogen sulfide, nitrous oxide, and carbon monoxide? It so happens that CO is slightly lighter than air and that H2S and NO are slightly heavier than air. Interesting fact. I don't know if it's important yet. But very interesting, because even though we rarely think about it this way, we are in fact, all actually submerged in air here on the earth's surface. Also, hydrogen gas (like helium) is significantly lighter than air and therefore in the right applications can counter gravitational forces quite significantly. So maybe Yew can someday actually build that antigravity machine he was so excited about...
>
> Also, all of these gaseous density relationships would also have to change with increasing altitude as the composition of the surrounding atmosphere changed, and would of course be massively different in space or on another planet. I could see this having a potentially big impact on the gaseous portion of intracellular signaling too, an additional effect to the EMF effect? Not sure a space suit would really compensate for all of that. Looks like I'm gonna have to put off that vacation to Mars for now, bummer

Remembering that gravity is just an acceleration that can be simulated in linear (car/airplane) or radial (centrifuge) fashion . . .

We can further see how reduced gravity (in space . . . in water?) can change the relative densities of gases as well as liquids.

So it becomes interesting to think about the biological effects of movement from this viewpoint. i.e pilots training in g-force simulators blacking out or maybe what the effects on consciousness are when ascending on a flight versus descending.

. . . and then there is the effect not only of the relative densities of the gases within us but also on the absolute densities of them. One of the things I do in employment is purge H2 filled generators with CO2 and then Air for maintenance and then reverse this again for service. We use the relative densities of the gases to form an interface between them and purge by displacement rather than dilution. The point is that carrying the purging procedure out at a higher pressure causes the gases to separate better - because their absolute densities are further apart at higher pressures.

. . . hence gravity and pressure can be synonymous when dealing with gases. . . .

and since it seems better to be nearer sea level where gravity is stronger than in space at the other extreme . . . . as we enter water we can substitute water pressure for gravity and increase the effect on gas densities even more . . . even to the point that some of the gases become liquids.

We could also consider that the reduced volumes would enable better infusion into tissues etc. and Dr. K. has already mentioned blood entering cells in deep water. SO it would appear possible that changes in gravity/acceleration/pressure could have an effect on what substances can go where in the body.

The key word in Ub5 to me at least was 'floating' and thinking a little about the word............it implies lack of effort, we talk about someone who 'floats' through life, someone who 'takes it all in' and responds in a kind of easy and effortless manner. I dare to day channeling Dr K that kind of person lives on the 'ledge' of the thermo-dynamic and quantum worlds whereas someone who is only in the thermo-dynamic world their life is full of effort and stress. Things for them are literally 'heavier' to move everything requires more effort and 'work'. Thermo-dynamics as far as I understand it is all about the ability to do 'work' to move 'heavy ' objects here and there.

I thought of Muhammed Ali who 'floats like a butterfly and stings like a bee' it sums him up well or as he was in his heyday. He could take it all in and 'float' to where he needed to go with less effort (usually) than his competitors. Soccer players the really good ones are often described as 'floating' through defenses, they seem to float in a zone that most players can't really enter.

Dr K would probably call this 'poetic' or 'mystic' or attempting to be but for me it means something. Most of my life has been very 'effortfull' (full of effort) I was in the moving and storage business for many years which involves......................moving large and heavy objects from here to there. Nothing for me came 'easy' so for me this business of 'floating' and how objects are literally 'lighter' seems to give a window to a world I have rarely seen and am now just glimpsing...............

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selecting-a-new-computer.13172/post-157410>

Help with selection please?
Specifically relating to EMF emission,are certain designs worse? My assumptiobs are that a PC with CPU as far away as possible is best. Next best a laptop not on lap though. Do we have any jdea jf netbooks based on android are better or worse?
In other words how does one select now after all that one knows now re EMF?
I don't care about any features except for reasonable speed,i.e pages change without delay.
Any suggestions for types,makes or models ?

### Lahelada — 2015-03-04T16:38:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selecting-a-new-computer.13172/post-157420>

Found an interesting web site. Apparently to minimise EMF some measures are quite counterintuitive. He recommends laptops with aluminium case coupled with ext monitor and keyboard. A laptop used like this on battery power is better than a desktop Pc. Check monitor for dimming circuits. If a monitor has it then dimming emits more EMF than 100% brightness. In other words f lux may not be so brilliant in such circumstances but external djmming through filters and or glasses may be more appropriate.
Full explanation here.

[http://www.conradbiologic.com/articles/ LowEMFComputing.html](http://www.conradbiologic.com/articles/%20LowEMFComputing.html)

### fitness@home — 2015-03-04T21:07:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selecting-a-new-computer.13172/post-157443>

> In other words f lux may not be so brilliant in such circumstances but **external djmming through filters** and or glasses may be more appropriate.

I have always used a privacy filter on my monitor screens at work. It does a good job of cutting the brightness.

### Lahelada — 2015-03-04T21:23:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selecting-a-new-computer.13172/post-157444>

Thank you. I am glad they work as it is probably the only thing I can find.

For now my set up looks like it will be netbook,sep keyboard and a monitor at a later stage. Money is still tight.

### cinnamon — 2015-03-06T02:21:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/selecting-a-new-computer.13172/post-157595>

I would avoid anything that has to have wifi to get on the net. Make sure your netbook has an ethernet port.

### Danny — 2015-03-04T15:44:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flux-app-for-computer.13176/post-157415>

I have resorted to using the dark room mode on my flux app to shut out all blue light from my computer all day long. I don't mind doing this, but was wondering if there was another way to shut out blue light entirely using the flux app. It appears that during daytime operation the lowest setting still emits some blue light. I only use natural lighting while I'm working so I don't want to resort to blue blockers during the day. Thanks!

# Thread: Blue blockers during the day (when exposed to blue light)?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 34
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2015-03-04T16:10:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157416>

Is it a good idea to wear blue blockers during the daytime anyway except for maybe noon since that is the colour spectrum that blue light matches the closest?

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-03-04T16:12:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157418>

..at work we are required to wear safety glasses. I could easily get yellow tinted ones.

### Lahelada — 2015-03-04T16:49:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157421>

Mike,
Yes it is and no it isn't.....
Lol, i mean that. Let me explain
Blue light is not intrinsically bad. We use it to mark the passage of time during the day. The idea of looking at the sun in the morning is to get yoir count down clock running aacurately for when it is time to do things (thinking of body functions)and when it is time to
sleep. If you wear blue blockers all day you miss the natural dose of blue light that you need.
Having said that if your office has that bright noon time frequency fluorescent lighting it might be an idea as it is also unnatural for your body to have the sun in its zenith for that long. If gour office has a natural unassjsted daylight rythm,i.e you put on artificial light at dusk I would mimic a more natural cycle putting them on when light is starting to fade. Intuitively I would say not to use it in the mornings. Just my musings .

### Shijin13 — 2015-03-04T19:15:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157433>

mike

I wear blue blockers during the day, but I'm also stuck in an office building lit by fluorescent lighting, and getting bombarded by wifi. My new office environment may actually be worse than the Pentagon. I've been wearing blue bockers during the day at work for the past 3&1/2 years. during that time frame I've healed a retinal tear.

### Penny — 2015-03-04T19:39:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157436>

Mike - I would wear the blue blockers anytime you look at a screen and when it's break time - go outside - especially in the morning between 9:00-noon - even though you might feel sleepy wearing them, at least you don't wipe out your eyes - you're young but as a canary in this particular coal mine - my eyes feel instant relief when I wear the blue blockers and when I don't, they start to ache and feel dry - so eat lots of fish and do the best you can with the light - at least look at the sun in the morning - DHA and zinc are great for your eyes - if mine ache, I can get instant relief with a zinc lozenger - it sucks because you need some blue light for your clock to work, but looking at this particular frequency wrecks your eyes... eat lots of seafood Mike

When I first started programming computers, it was a black background and red or green letters - nothing as unholy as it is today - and I can remember whenever I didn't take my vitamins - Centrum at the time - my eyes would hurt - but I've been in glasses since I was 2 so I might not be the norm... even funnier, I wouldn't watch t.v. at night because "I got too rested and couldn't sleep"... never did I correlate it with the brightness of the light coming from the t.v. -

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-04T22:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157447>

So glad someone brought this up. When I go to take exams at college, I have to go to this ridiculously cramped room littered with computers and fluorescent lighting. My eyes are extremely sensitive as it is - I mean, if I go to shovel snow, if I don't wear glasses, it almost "burns" my retinas - I get the same sensation as if I just saw the flash of a camera, where there is an after-image in your vision for awhile after; only, this lasts for minutes to hours, and my entire visual field is stained another color, like red or blue or something. Anyhow, the bright lighting, particularly rich in blue and probably green frequencies, causes massive neurological symptoms. Everything on the white paper for the exam has trails behind it, I have after images, brain fog, etc.

I was thinking of using some really dark blue light blockers specifically when going to take these exams.

Also, I kind of disagree with us needing this "natural" burst of blue light during the day. Yes, we are exposed to blue light via the sun during the day. But compare a graph of the frequencies of light, and their intensity, contained in sunlight at different hours, incandescent bulbs, CFLs/fluroescent lights, etc. and you'll see that the amount we are exposed to from artificial light (though not incandescent bulbs, candles, etc) is in no way 'natural', and we should probably shield ourselves accordingly.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-03-05T02:35:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-157472>

Thank you for all the answers!
When those CFL'S originally came out with the blue light that they call "daylight", I never liked them. I think the colour temperature is 9800K or something like that.
The "soft white" is similar looking to incandescent and I think those are 3400K. Sometimes you really have to hunt for the numbers.
I'm not advocating using CFL's, I'm just saying that when you have no choice in your lighting, maybe our bosses will be willing to change the colour temperature of the bulbs, at least where you are working. If that even makes much of a difference but it all adds up.

Congratulations on your retinal tear success!

Ahh, Ive only just noticed that you can actually change the daytime settings on [F.lux](https://justgetflux.com/) too. It can go down to 2700K (the standard daytime setting is 6500K), so that's pretty helpful.

### shads — 2015-04-06T02:38:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-160372>

> [Jenny S said:](/goto/post?id=160366)
>
> Would you pls tell me where you do this - fairly computer illiterate!

F.lux is probably going to be the easiest way to get a decent change. Just download it from their website ([https://justgetflux.com/](https://justgetflux.com/)) and install. You'll then have a little half orange half grey circle on the right side of your windows taskbar which you can right click for the 'settings' for day and night-time lighting.

If you want to go back to default lighting you can easily just uninstall it.

### cinnamon — 2019-05-07T20:01:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blockers-during-the-day-when-exposed-to-blue-light.13177/post-269883>

> [Saichi said:](/goto/post?id=268815)
>
> This is really hardcore, have you regularly been using darkroom during the day since this post?

No, not darkroom mode. I have f.lux set at 800K both day and night (except when I occasionally need to see colours). For those who might not know, you can use the keyboard shortcut Alt-Shift-Page Down to choose a setting below the standard ones - you can go as low as 800K.

I also keep the screen dimmed as much as it will let me.

I don't use my blue blocking glasses during the day but I always add them at night, in addition to the f.lux.

Hardcore? Nah, hardcore would be throwing out the laptop! hehehe

### mrc — 2019-05-13T18:18:06+0300

# Thread: What in the perplexity is my body doing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-in-the-perplexity-is-my-body-doing.13178/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### takenoadvice — 2015-03-04T16:11:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-in-the-perplexity-is-my-body-doing.13178/post-157417>

What in the perplexity is my body doing? Looking for any feedback on data below. This is why I ask, below are my weights per day:

Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, 213.4, 213.4, 212.6, 212.6,
210.8, Monday, 213.6, 211.6, 211.0, 208.8, 208.0,
207.6, 208.4, 208.2, 207.2, 208.2, 206.4, 204.6,
206.6, 206.1, 205.8, 204.8, Thursday, Friday, Saturday
EOY2014: 215.6

First slot listed is Sunday, February 8, 2015. I put in day of week for when I did not weigh myself. I weigh normally in the morning.

My scale is consistent, usually max .2 pounds difference in repeated weighings. I started trying to reset about 3 weeks ago, after coming across Dr. Kruse on a YouTube interview, and having decided over a month ago to get into a paleo diet. So I'm ASTONISHED at my results so far. I haven't seen myself below 206 pounds IN ABOUT 12 YEARS lowest floor I'd hit before was around 208/209 some years back when I was lifting weights very regularly. Above I'm figuring about 9 lbs loss in 3 weeks.

My diet is roughly:
breakfast: 2 eggs, 2-3 pieces bacon, sauteed kale or spinach (coconut oil), perhaps chicken/turkey
lunch: salad of lettuce &| spinach, sometimes some carrot, chicken/turkey/hot dog, coconut flakes, milled flaxseed, balsamic vinaigrette
dinner: salad of lettuce &| spinach, sometimes some carrot, chicken/turkey/hot dog, coconut flakes, milled flaxseed, balsamic vinaigrette
Throw in occasional burger or pork chop above. Not had in 3 weeks: no dairy or milk cheese, no legumes, no non-dairy milk substitute, no fast food, no bread or other grains, no corn, no potatoes, no honey or sugar added to coffee, no cookies/donuts, no bananas.
I feel like I'm eating a lot of food at each meal. Not sure how many calories a day but I'm thinking 1700-2200, weekends I tend to eat a great deal more of turkey / chicken, also been making turkey soup stock and having that. I have coffee most days, usually keeping to less than 4-5 cups a day, which is way less than my previous norms. Sometimes chai tea. Sometimes sip a very small amount of light almond milk. Trying to drink more water. Sometimes put salmon / sardines / tilapia in salad. Indulging in a diet soft drink every so often (but interest in that is diminishing). On an online calculator, don't remember which one, it estimated me at 1750 calories consumption a day for my age and sedentary occupation, and it projected about 3-4 lbs fat loss first month on a regimen of 25/100/139 grams of carbohydrate/protein/fat respectively.

My habit is roughly:
up usually at 6, eating by 6:30
lunch at 12 or 1, also walk for half hour
dinner at 5, sometimes 6,
bed at 10-11 -- turning off lights

My hunger cravings have diminished, esp in past 7 days. I went crazy on 2 bags of cranberry/raisin/sunflower seed about 2 weeks ago and finished one bag in just a day, about 13x140 cal portions. No interest in finishing the remnants in the 2nd bag. I am beginning to eat less than what I've written here, as I feel full all the time. Only maybe twice have I eaten after 7 pm, and only once less than 4 hours before bed. Prior to 3-4 weeks ago, my diet was okay most days, but weekends brought fast food, also movie popcorn/candy.

I am beginning to sweat more. Not much. Overall for me, very little.

My next plans: 1) try to get in more vegetative variety. 2) add more variety of seafood. 3) study up more on this site. 4) add in some exercise in the evening, mainly kettlebells and calisthenics.

So ... WHAT IS MY BODY DOING? Not written above, but I weighed just before bed last night at 206.2, slept about 6 hours, and this morning I was 204.8. Numbers re-weighed both times. Wait -- WHAT? One lbs drop over night? WHAT IS THIS .... ?

What's the most important factor in what I'm doing right now?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Kelly's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kellys-journal.13179/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ktrosper — 2015-03-05T00:07:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kellys-journal.13179/post-157457>

**"Know thyself."** \-Socrates
Many of these questions we must ask ourselves on the road to optimal are impossible to answer "correctly, in context" without this knowledge of ourselves.. It's the first and quite possibly the most critical step toward optimum, in my opinion..
**About my journal:**
I have and will continue to put a lot of thought into this.. I'll edit this as time goes, as I continue to learn and build understanding, as those I trust and respect come to influence me and as my priorities change..

**My Guiding principles**:

-   Context matters.. Always try to put everything in context.
-   Value truth above all else.. Seek it and embrace it, even if it means the destruction of a premise, belief or value..
-   Ask the right questions/Ask better questions.. Useful answers come from asking the right questions.
-   When you know better you do better/To believe in it, I must understand it.. Change is easy when I understand why the change matters.
-   This is a marathon, not a sprint.. The road to optimal is long, there's a lot of learning to be done and there are only so many hours in a day..
-   Search for conflict.. conflict reveals a possible flaw in the premise, a possible gap in the knowledge or a messed up value-system.

**My Goal**: I want to achieve optimal **HEALTH** while promoting **LONGEVITY** while **ENJOYING** my life...

Is this the same as wanting to Enjoy life, while promoting optimal Health while promoting Longevity or
wanting to achieve Longevity, while promoting optimal Health while Enjoying life? Close, but I don't think so.. The differences are subtle, but they are worth thinking about..

Defining what I want begs more questions..
Some of these obviously seem redundant, and maybe they are.. It's my way of ensuring that I've arrived at the right answer and I've set the right priority within the context of my life.

Can I enjoy life without health? Probably, but to a lesser extent..
Can I enjoy life without longevity? Of course, but I'd prefer to enjoy it for as long as possible.. Some things I enjoy come with risk.. Life is full of tradeoffs.
Can I live a long time without health? Chances are reduced, for sure.. I suppose it's possible to live a long time with a feeding tube stuffed down my throat while on a ventilator..
Would I enjoy living long without health? Certainly less enjoyable than living long with health..
Would I want longevity without enjoyment of life? No, Probably not.
Can I achieve optimal health yet lose out on longevity? Of course, accidents happen..
Can I achieve optimal health yet lose out on enjoyment of life? Yes, and that's not what I want.

These questions, in my opinion, are worth contemplating AND the answers worth documenting.. The answers help to define my focus and my priorities. They tell me where I'm at and they shine the light on the path forward.. The documentation makes them "real", allows me to scrutinize them and search for conflict, revisit them as needed..

Before I could answer any of these questions I had to **define** these objectives..
Before I could really define any objectives, I had to understand what it is that I truly value.
Know thyself..

**My current/best definitions**:

**"Enjoying life" is..**
Spending my time and energy on the people and things I love and value. (Family stuff, CheapEagle (my Powered Paraglider), Gladys (our RV), General (my truck), Hunting, Working on my Health, Learning/Self-educating, Becoming a better man, Running, Bucket List). Risk/reward value judgments are obviously a factor. Would I enjoy snorting a line of coke from here to Memphis? Probably, but I'm not willing to risk my health or longevity over it.. Where is the sweet spot?

**"Longevity" is..**
Duh! Living a LONG time.. Centurion? Sure, I'll take it.. but longevity's value is relative to health and enjoyment. Would I want to live to be 200 if I had to spend that time in a bubble in the basement? Of course not. Quality of life matters. Life is full of tradeoffs. Where is the sweet spot?

**"optimal Health" is..**
Physical, mental and spiritual functionality as close to peak as possible (optimal)- but not at any cost.. Value judgments are a factor here, too. Could I peak physically on HGH or steroid injections? Yes, but the cost to longevity is not something I'm willing to pay. Would I peak mentally if I spent 10 hours a day doing brain sharpening skills? Maybe, but that kind of investment would be too steep for the return.. Could I peak spiritually if I were to meditate 20 out of 24 hours? Maybe, but at what cost? Where is the sweet spot?

This "sweet spot" is most probably in a different place for all of us.
It all comes down to value.

Know thyself..
(to be continued...)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Association Induction Hypothesis WIKI Article under threat of Deletion
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/association-induction-hypothesis-wiki-article-under-threat-of-deletion.13189/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2015-03-05T14:12:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/association-induction-hypothesis-wiki-article-under-threat-of-deletion.13189/post-157503>

The [Wiki AIH article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_induction_hypothesis) which I have spent considerable effort (too much!) on, was flagged for deletion yesterday so I must argue (again) why I think it is a notable theory. Go [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Articles_for_deletion/Association_induction_hypothesis) for the discussion and ill informed comments such as those below.

Not sure how best to approach this - any thoughts/support appreciated.

> Extraordinary claims that would need extraordinary proof: "Water and ions in cells exist in a different physical state than in a dilute water solution", wow! Or, in a related note, statements that are [not even wrong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Not_even_wrong), as "Although much extracellular sodium ion and water molecules diffuse in and out of the cell, no energy is required for the exchange." (diffusion does not require "energy" - is that talking about [ATP generation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATP_generation) or what?).
>
> "Although much extracellular sodium ion and water molecules diffuse in and out of the cell, no energy is required for the exchange. In theory, the cell could hold these asymmetrical concentrations forever without using energy violations of the second law of thermodynamics automatically qualify anything for FRINGE per an explicit example in [WP:FRINGE/PS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:FRINGE/PS)

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-05T15:01:16+0200

Fonar's MRI machin was based upon this theory.

Pollacks Book is based upon his work.......send them that.

Both books mention Ling

Unfortunately, you seem to be dealing with the "Priests of the Temples of Syrinx," for those of you that may get my drift...

But save your research Paul! It's great work and extremely important. We just may need to find a more receptive audience for it than these "Priests of Wikipedia." And remember this the next time those bastards ask for a donation...Personally I'm not giving a goddamn cent to help these modern technocrats uphold their tired pronouncements under the deceptive banner of "free knowledge for all." Non merci.

Furthermore, our collective reliance on Wiki as the font of all knowledge is a dangerous trend for all of us, due to its centralized nature (of its editorial policies at least), and because it seems more enlightened than it actually is. And I am just as guilty of relying on it as anyone. But that is going to change, for me at least.

### PaulG — 2015-03-06T01:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/association-induction-hypothesis-wiki-article-under-threat-of-deletion.13189/post-157594>

In [http://www.gilbertling.org/lp7.htm](http://www.gilbertling.org/lp7.htm) Ling says

"**I myself have never argued that the observed shortening of NMR relaxation times was _exclusively_ or almost exclusively due to the motional restriction of bulk-phase water. On the contrary, I have repeatedly cautioned against this exclusive interpretation--- even though this interpretation came originally from scientists who had first provided NMR evidence in support of my theory**\-" (Full context is below)

Also some good papers by Phillipa Wiggins on water density and enzyme actions
[Life Depends on Two Kinds of Water](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0001406) and [Role of Water in Some Biological Processes](http://mmbr.asm.org/content/54/4/432.full.pdf) and ****[The Source of Some of the Extraordinary Powers and Properties of Enzymes](http://www.waterjournal.org/volume-1/wiggins-1) (Last two mention Ling)****

> **6) NMR RELAXATION TIMES OF WATER PROTONS IN LIVING AND KILLED CELLS**
> My former graduate student Peggy Neville and her coauthors (Science 184:1072, 1974; see also Civan and Shporer, Bioch. Biophys Acta 343:399, 1974) showed that frog lens and muscle when killed by heating showed a _shortening_ of the NMR relaxation times of their water protons, in apparent contradiction to an expected _lengthening_, if the short relaxation times of normal living cells like those studied were due to motional restriction of the bulk-phase water molecules.
>
> It is true that in the Polarized Multilayer Theory of the living cells (as an integral part of the AI Hypothesis), motional restriction of the bulk-phase water is clearly predicted. But as the author of this theory, **I myself have never argued that the observed shortening of NMR relaxation times was _exclusively_ or almost exclusively due to the motional restriction of bulk-phase water. On the contrary, I have repeatedly cautioned against this exclusive interpretation--- even though this interpretation came originally from scientists who had first provided NMR evidence in support of my theory**\---and have further pointed out that other factors including the rapid exchange with a small fraction of tightly bound water on paramagnetic ions and on cell proteins might play significant roles also (Ling, Intern. J. Neuroscience 1:129, 1970, p.138-139; Ling, in "The Aqueous Cytoplasm, ed. A.D.Keith, Marcel Dekker, Inc., New York, 1979, p.50-51; Ling and Tucker, J. Nat. Cancer Inst. 64: 1199, 1980, p.1206).
>
> In years following, an important role of proteins (Koenig and Schillinger, J. Biol. Chem. 244: 3283, 1969) and of paramagnetic ions (in particular, manganese and iron and to a much lesser extent copper) in determining the NMR relaxation times of diverse tissues have been fully established (Ling, Physiol. Chem. Phys and Med. NMR, 15: 511, 1983; Ling et al, ibid, 22:1, 1990).Other model oxygen-containing polmers like poly(ethylene glycol), which does not have direct influence on water proton relaxation as proteins do, but also polarize water in multilayers, do cause substantial reduction of water proton relaxation times, T1 and T2 (Ling and Murphy, Physiol. Chem. Phys. , 15:137, 1983).
>
> In work yet to be published, my former summer student, Gerri Magavero and I have shown that heating frog muscle in a Ringer's solution to a temperature (35 to 40 degree centigrade) enough to reduce/destroy the polarized multilayer dynamic water structure as monitored by the equilibrium distribution of sucrose probe (Ling in "Thermobiology" ed.A.H. Rose, Academic Press, New York, 1967, p. 19, Figure 10) does indeed produce lengthening of the spin-lattice relaxation time (T1); heating to 50 degree and higher , however, causes sharp reduction of T1 as Neville and others have found. Similarly, heating to 60 degrees or higher temperatures of aqueous solutions of most isolated _pure_ proteins studied (though not all) caused a similar sharp reduction of T1. While one should not place too much emphasis on the T1 lengthening at 35 to 40 degree temperature (yet)---which could be at least in part be due to an accompanying cell swelling and increase of cell water content, the clear demonstration of T1 shortening in model protein-water leaves no question that the earlier observed lack of T1 lengthening offers no evidence against the polarized multilayer theory, since T1 lengtheing would be inevitably camouflaged by the pronounced shortening due to the cell proteins and protein-paramagnetic ion complexes. For demonstration of T1 shortening in model systems where water is polarized by other oxygen-containing polymers (e.g., poly(ethylene glycol)), but without the complicating contributions from proteins, see Ling and Murphy, Physiol. Chem. Phys. 15:137, 1983.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-03-06T08:16:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/association-induction-hypothesis-wiki-article-under-threat-of-deletion.13189/post-157615>

I temporarily just copied and pasted the source code to this page: [http://qhwiki.com/page:association-induction-hypothesis](http://qhwiki.com/page:association-induction-hypothesis)

Since the wikipedia syntax is different from wikidot, almost all the links are broken, and the page is a mess (except for the one bibcite link which I fixed manually shown in the screenshot below). All the content is there though, and can be re-constructed manually.

# Thread: Seasonal affective disorder.. your thoughts?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thomas — 2015-03-05T15:26:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157511>

Hi there. I've been thinking about this for a few days. I have that affliction of being deeply affected by the weather. I live in western europe (brittany) and most of the time it's overcast. Problem is my mood goes down a lot. It's march now and a bit sunny, and the difference is incredible, motivation goes up, lethargy goes down.
I'd add that lots of things jack talks about seem to help.. the cold, avoiding artificial light/emfs, eating lots of seafood, spending as much time as possible outdoors...
The problem is it's not just me. I am quite sure that problem concerns hundreds of millions of people worldwide (billions maybe?).

I am considering moving to a more sunny place (probably the meditteranean part of europe).
Apart that solution, what can I do to decouple my mood from the weather?
Which protocols you would give priority to? ideally i'd want to have the same mood on an overcast low sun short day that on a clear summer day.
I don't think I have seen jack talk specifically about that problem.
If there are people that understand the problem in term of pathways, neurotransmitters.. feel free to explain the science (it goes much deeper than a simple lack of serotonin due to light i am sure)!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-05T15:53:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157514>

### thomas — 2015-03-07T20:13:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157769>

Yes thank you for the suggestion caroline.
So over the last few days I have been through all 167 pages of ask jack. I didn't really find much related to sad. I guess I have to read trough all the blogs to get clues I hope. (but that's gonna take 100 of days).
It's probably related to inflamation and oxydation/reduction.(genius?!)

Maybe I already know the answer. maybe it's simply to live and eat totally like our primal ancestors but it's hard for someone born in civilisation (and civilisation is everywhere too). And that's the fundamental problem for everyone on this site. We know the answers but we have one foot on each world. So we half step it and seek refuge on details, try to get the science to compromise between both..

### Inger — 2015-03-07T20:18:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157771>

Why would you like to always have the same moods?

I like variation.. sometimes I feel more like going inside me.. contemplate.. be alone.. and I like that too. I never feel depressed anymore, ever.

You need to be outside also when it is gray. Or rainy. I love rainy days now... I just love them. It is so weird..lol. I am super happy when it is rainy! Maybe I am crazy.....
You need to look into the sky, lift your face upwards, do not walk with your face facing the ground... you get too little light that way

Have it dark when it is dark outside. This is as important. Fake light = depression IMHO

### Inger — 2015-03-07T20:20:19+0200

and always optimize
do what you can, forget about the rest

I do not live far from you BTW, in northern Germany

### shah78 — 2015-03-07T21:42:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157783>

The bad news: there is no special SAD cure.  The good news: all the regular "Kruse stuff" works for SAD. In the winter you just have to do more of it. And of course it is harder to do more stuff , when you feel like hell. Sort of a cruel joke, but such is life. My SAD keeps getting better each winter. Good luck. (Of course ,I live in Florida. I have a huge tail wind)

### Josh — 2015-03-07T22:15:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157790>

> Mice engineered with a knockout gene for 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (MST) exhibited increased anxiety along with elevated levels of serotonin in the brain.\[[146](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4093745/#ref146)\] MST can produce hydrogen sulfide gas, which can subsequently be oxidized to sulfate, so it probably plays an important role in the production of serotonin sulfate and therefore in serotonin transport. Its deficiency would be predicted to result in an accumulation of unsulfated serotonin. This also suggests that it is a deficiency in serotonin_sulfate_ rather than a deficiency in serotonin that leads to anxiety, and this could explain contradictory results on the relationship between serotonin and anxiety.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4093745/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4093745/)

### QuantumRadical — 2015-03-08T06:22:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157814>

Spend some time in a really cold tub of water. That will definitely change your mood. LOL

### QuantumRadical — 2015-03-08T06:23:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-affective-disorder-your-thoughts.13191/post-157815>

And, if you can, warm up in the sun naked, even if the air temperature is not so high. Then jump into the cold tub again, and repeat with the sun. Talk about energizing!!!

# Thread: started progesterone lozenges/troches...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-05T15:58:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157515>

Hi everyone. I recently finally started the progesterone lozenges / troches that my gyn and compounding pharmacist decided would benefit me. I am to let one troche dissolve between my cheek and gum twice daily on days 12 - 26. I picked them up yesterday, on day 13, and began immediately.

(In case anyone wonders, it was covered by my insurance, I have no idea how or why but I'm thrilled! But if it wasn't they said it would cost me $45 per month supply.)

The dosage is 25 mg per dose, so 50mg per day.

This is brand new to me and I do not know if there is an optimal time of day to use them (other than approx. 12 hours apart), whether there are other things I ought to be aware/cautious of while using them (aside from avoid soy at all times), whether this is an average starting dose, whether there is a test I can/should do at some point to see how it's working, etc.

I would be very grateful for any input anyone can give me regarding these lozenges and/or progesterone in general.

Also, this month my period has gone insane for the first time ever. I am 42. I wonder if it is a symptom of peri-menopouse? The issue is, my period came 8 days early last month on February 5th and lasted the normal 5 days, thru Feb 10th. Then it came back on Feb 20th and has lasted with varying heaviness all the way through at least yesterday (which was 13 days of bleeding at that point). I have consulted my gyn a few times and we're keeping an eye on it for now since there's no pain and it's not heavy or anything. But I just wonder if this is typical of peri-menopouse, or what?

And, whatever it is, might progesterone help it to stop already? \*fingers crossed\* Thanks!

### fitness@home — 2015-03-05T19:29:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157541>

Hope,

I take my progesterone in the evening.

Some say it helps you sleep better. Not sure if that's true or not...

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-06T13:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157629>

Thanks fitness@home.. mine are to be taken 12 hours apart so I can't do that, personally.

But now I have another question for everyone:

Has anyone had any luck getting their lozenges switched out for some other mode of delivery? I don't have an hour per day to dedicate to progesterone and it's taking at least that long doing it this way. Combine the time and discomfort necessary (shoving a little rectangle between my gum and cheek is killing me, probably causing a sore on my gum by this time) with the absolutely horrible taste and this is gonna be short-lived if I have to keep doing it this way.

I have experimented with putting it in different locations in my mouth and some are less painful than others, but those are the ones where it keeps sliding upwards and onto my tongue and won't stay put.

But these take over half an hour to dissolve each time so that's an hour per day of avoiding people because it looks like I have chewing tobacco in my cheek, and that whole time I am guessing I'm not supposed to eat or drink anything which I can't do because first of all it tastes HORRIBLE so I have to drink every few seconds to wash the liquid down as it dissolves, and second of all because I don't GO that long without drinking something ever, it would be just about impossible to do.

It took from December 31 until March 3 to even finally get these things though, I don't want to have to wait until June to finally get them to give me something else that isn't disgusting and inconvenient. So is there progesterone I can just order myself online and use instead?

These are only 50mg per day anyway which I can't imagine being enough, and they're only for 2 weeks out of my cycle which I think probably also isn't going to work for me.

Thanks for any input any of you can provide. I feel like I'm barely treading water these days with this stuff. I've got a hairy upper lip starting on top of everything else and it's just got to stop.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-06T14:03:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157630>

hey I finally realized what these actually taste like (which sure as heck isn't "vanilla" which they said the flavoring would be) - it's black licorice with a vanilla overtone. So so foul. I'm considering just chewing them like a hard candy and swallowing them from now on, or swallowing them whole. Will either option do ANYTHING? This is kinda like being forced into morning sickness twice a day. Which surely doesn't make me want to have sex, which is the whole point of going on this stuff.

### nicld — 2015-03-06T23:19:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157663>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=157541)
>
> Hope,
>
> I take my progesterone in the evening.
>
> Some say it helps you sleep better. Not sure if that's true or not...

I will be going back to taking all of my Pg (on cream) in the evening and let you know if it helps. Last month I went back to what my doc has prescribed 1X in the morning and 1X in the evening and sleep when to crap 2nd half of cycle. This time I have been trying to do all in morning and that has not helped so tomorrow night, all at night.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-07T14:29:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157734>

Something I noticed is that if I drink coffee with them they're the most tolerable. I just have to take them at a time of day that I can go grab a caramel latte at dunkin donuts to sip while this dissolves and I should be okay with the flavor and stuff.

Something even more interesting - last night I had hot flashes while sleeping. Not the full body ones I've had a couple of times a year or so ago, but all under the back of my hair and around my neck. That makes me wonder could it be the progesterone (well sure, it COULD be, but has anyone else gotten hot flashes while adjusting /going on pg?) and also makes me wonder if progesterone will affect my thyroid in some way, since I've got hashi's but no doctor/meds/plan for controlling it yet.

I'd love to get that thing turned back on so that I'm not always cold and lethargic and inflamed and depressed.

### nicld — 2015-03-07T19:38:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-157756>

Sometimes things get worse before they get better. Hang in there.

### Hope2Learn — 2015-03-11T16:02:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-progesterone-lozenges-troches.13192/post-158206>

Thanks! I'm still having hot flashes at night from the progesterone. Now am having them a couple of hours after taking the daytime dose as well. Not a big deal, just an observation. My goal with my hormones now is to control my Hashi's without needing thyroid meds or an endocrinologist. The tech who did my pelvic ultrasound yesterday doesnt see anything funky to explain the bleeding and says it should stop soon based on what she saw (thin lining). Hope so. But she also said that she thinks that hormones MIGHT all actually relate to people's HEALTH and that doctors need to realize that.

I wasn't sure what to say. hehe
